title: Comparative Technical Analysis
description: Compare two or more technical approaches, algorithms, or systems along multiple dimensions with structured criteria.
when_to_use: When a task asks to compare alternatives, weigh trade-offs between approaches, or recommend one method over another based on specific criteria.
steps: Identify the items being compared and the comparison context
Define evaluation dimensions (performance, complexity, scalability, ease of use)
Gather data or evidence for each item along each dimension
Create a structured comparison matrix
Analyze trade-offs and identify which contexts favor each approach
Provide a reasoned recommendation with caveats
expected_outcome: A structured comparison table with analysis of trade-offs and context-dependent recommendations.

title: Architecture Pattern Catalog
description: Document and analyze system architecture patterns including their components, interactions, strengths, and limitations.
when_to_use: When a task involves describing, designing, or evaluating system architectures, component designs, or design patterns in ML or software systems.
steps: Identify the architecture pattern and its context of use
Document core components and their responsibilities
Map data flows and component interactions
List invariants and constraints the pattern enforces
Analyze strengths: what problems does this pattern solve well
Analyze weaknesses: where does this pattern struggle or introduce complexity
expected_outcome: A structured architecture description with component diagram, data flow, and trade-off analysis.

title: Empirical Evidence Synthesis
description: Collect and synthesize empirical results from multiple experiments, benchmarks, or case studies to draw evidence-based conclusions.
when_to_use: When a task requires backing claims with data from multiple studies, comparing experimental results across papers, or establishing what the empirical evidence shows.
steps: Define the empirical question being investigated
Identify relevant studies with quantitative results
Extract key metrics and experimental conditions from each study
Normalize results for comparability (same metrics, similar conditions)
Identify patterns: where do results agree and disagree
Draw conclusions with appropriate uncertainty and caveats
expected_outcome: An evidence summary with normalized comparisons across studies and confidence-weighted conclusions.

title: Formal Definition and Notation
description: Provide mathematically precise definitions of concepts, including formal notation, set-theoretic descriptions, and relationship to related concepts.
when_to_use: When a task requires precise formal definitions, mathematical formulations, or disambiguation of technical terminology using rigorous notation.
steps: State the concept informally to build intuition
Introduce required notation and preliminary definitions
Provide the formal definition with all conditions and constraints
Relate to adjacent concepts (generalization, special cases)
Provide a concrete instantiation or example
Note common misconceptions or subtle distinctions
expected_outcome: A rigorous formal definition with notation, connections to related concepts, and illustrative examples.

title: Failure Mode Analysis
description: Systematically identify, categorize, and analyze failure modes of systems, algorithms, or approaches.
when_to_use: When a task involves understanding why something fails, identifying risks, edge cases, or limitations of a system or method.
steps: Define the system or method and its expected behavior
Enumerate known failure modes from literature and practice
For each failure: trigger conditions, symptoms, severity, frequency
Categorize failures (systematic vs random, recoverable vs fatal)
Identify common root causes shared across failure modes
Propose mitigations or detection strategies for critical failures
expected_outcome: A categorized failure mode catalog with triggers, severity ratings, and mitigation strategies.

title: Research Gap Identification
description: Identify unexplored areas, unresolved questions, and potential research directions within a field.
when_to_use: When a task requires finding open problems, identifying what existing work does NOT address, or proposing future research directions.
steps: Map what IS known: key results, established methods, consensus findings
Map what IS NOT known: conflicting results, untested hypotheses, missing data
Identify methodological gaps: approaches not yet tried
Identify application gaps: domains where techniques haven't been applied
Assess feasibility and impact of addressing each gap
Prioritize gaps by potential contribution and tractability
expected_outcome: A prioritized list of research gaps with feasibility assessment and potential impact ratings.

title: Dataset and Benchmark Analysis
description: Analyze datasets and benchmarks including their construction methodology, biases, coverage, and suitability for specific evaluation purposes.
when_to_use: When a task involves evaluating datasets, understanding benchmark limitations, or selecting appropriate data for training or evaluation.
steps: Document dataset metadata: size, source, collection methodology, time range
Analyze distribution: class balance, coverage of edge cases, representativeness
Identify known biases and their potential impact on results
Compare to alternative datasets for the same purpose
Assess construct validity: does the benchmark actually measure what it claims
Provide usage recommendations with caveats
expected_outcome: A dataset profile with bias analysis, validity assessment, and usage recommendations.

title: Scalability and Complexity Analysis
description: Analyze the computational scalability of algorithms or systems, including asymptotic complexity, bottlenecks, and practical performance characteristics.
when_to_use: When a task involves understanding how approaches scale with data size, model size, or number of components, or when comparing computational costs.
steps: Define the scaling dimensions (data size, model parameters, number of agents, etc.)
Derive or cite asymptotic complexity (time and space)
Identify practical bottlenecks beyond asymptotic behavior (memory bandwidth, communication overhead)
Compare scaling behavior to alternatives
Identify the regime where the approach becomes impractical
Suggest optimizations or approximations for scaling beyond that regime
expected_outcome: A complexity analysis with scaling curves, bottleneck identification, and practical scaling recommendations.

title: Analogy-Based Explanation
description: Explain complex technical concepts through carefully chosen analogies that map abstract ideas to familiar, concrete domains.
when_to_use: When a task requires making a complex concept accessible to a broader audience, or when abstract ideas benefit from concrete, intuitive parallels.
steps: Identify the core mechanism or concept to explain
List the key properties that the analogy must capture
Brainstorm analogies from familiar domains (physical, everyday, business)
Test the analogy: which properties map well, which break down
Present the analogy with explicit property mapping
Explicitly note where the analogy breaks down to prevent misconceptions
expected_outcome: A clear explanation using a well-fitted analogy with explicit mappings and stated limitations.

title: Architecture Diagram Design
description: Design clear, informative system architecture diagrams that communicate component relationships, data flows, and system boundaries.
when_to_use: When a task involves describing system architecture visually, documenting component interactions, or communicating design decisions through diagrams.
steps: Identify the audience and what the diagram must communicate
List all components, external systems, and data stores
Determine the diagram type (C4, sequence, data flow, deployment)
Arrange components to minimize crossing lines and maximize readability
Label all connections with data types, protocols, or actions
Add annotations for non-obvious design decisions or constraints
expected_outcome: A clear architecture diagram with labeled components, connections, and annotations explaining design decisions.

title: Example Generation and Curation
description: Generate diverse, illustrative examples that demonstrate concepts, edge cases, and variations of a technique or pattern.
when_to_use: When a task benefits from concrete examples, demonstrations of edge cases, or multiple instances showing how a concept applies in different contexts.
steps: Identify the concept and its key dimensions of variation
Generate a canonical example that demonstrates the core idea
Generate edge cases: minimal examples, boundary conditions, degenerate cases
Generate counterexamples that show what the concept is NOT
Ensure diversity: different domains, scales, and levels of complexity
Order examples pedagogically: typical → edge case → counterexample
expected_outcome: A curated set of examples covering typical cases, edge cases, and counterexamples with clear annotations.

title: Technical Blog Post Writing
description: Write engaging technical content that combines narrative, explanation, and practical takeaways for a practitioner audience.
when_to_use: When a task requires communicating technical ideas in an accessible, engaging format aimed at practitioners or technical readers.
steps: Define the hook: what problem or question draws the reader in
Structure: hook → background → core explanation → practical implications → takeaways
Use concrete examples and data to support every abstract claim
Write in active voice with clear, specific language
Include code snippets, figures, or tables where they add clarity
End with actionable takeaways the reader can apply immediately
expected_outcome: A well-structured technical post with narrative flow, concrete examples, and actionable takeaways.

title: Scenario Planning and What-If Analysis
description: Construct multiple plausible future scenarios to explore how different assumptions or decisions lead to different outcomes.
when_to_use: When a task involves strategic planning, risk assessment, or exploring how design decisions play out under different conditions.
steps: Identify the key decision or uncertainty being explored
Define 3-4 scenarios based on different assumptions about the key variable
For each scenario: trace consequences through the system
Identify signals that would indicate which scenario is materializing
Assess robustness: which strategies work across multiple scenarios
Recommend actions with contingency plans for each scenario
expected_outcome: A set of plausible scenarios with traced consequences, indicators, and robust strategy recommendations.

title: Concept Map Construction
description: Build visual concept maps showing relationships between ideas, with labeled connections indicating the nature of each relationship.
when_to_use: When a task requires organizing complex relationships between many concepts, building a knowledge graph of a domain, or showing how ideas connect.
steps: List all relevant concepts for the domain
Identify relationship types (is-a, part-of, requires, enables, conflicts-with)
Connect concepts with labeled directed edges
Cluster related concepts spatially for readability
Identify hub concepts (many connections) and leaf concepts (few connections)
Validate completeness: are there obvious missing concepts or connections
expected_outcome: A concept map with labeled relationships showing the structure of a domain or topic.

title: Cross-Domain Pattern Extraction
description: Identify recurring patterns that appear across different domains, techniques, or applications, and abstract them into general principles.
when_to_use: When a task involves finding commonalities across different approaches, identifying universal principles, or transferring insights between domains.
steps: Collect descriptions of approaches from multiple domains
For each approach: extract the core mechanism in domain-agnostic language
Group approaches by structural similarity (not surface similarity)
Name and define each pattern with its essential properties
Identify when the pattern applies and when it breaks down
Provide instantiations of the pattern in each original domain as validation
expected_outcome: A set of named patterns with abstract definitions, applicability conditions, and concrete instantiations across domains.

title: Trade-off Analysis and Decision Matrix
description: Systematically analyze trade-offs between competing objectives, approaches, or design decisions using weighted criteria.
when_to_use: When a task requires choosing between alternatives with multiple conflicting criteria, or when the optimal choice depends on which properties you prioritize.
steps: List all alternatives being considered
Define evaluation criteria and their relative importance weights
Score each alternative on each criterion (with evidence)
Compute weighted scores and identify the top candidates
Perform sensitivity analysis: which criteria weights change the winner
Present the trade-off landscape, not just the recommendation
expected_outcome: A weighted decision matrix with sensitivity analysis showing how different priorities lead to different optimal choices.

title: Multi-Source Integration
description: Combine information from multiple heterogeneous sources (papers, documentation, benchmarks, case studies) into a coherent unified narrative.
when_to_use: When a task requires pulling together information from various sources with different formats, levels of detail, or perspectives into a single coherent account.
steps: Catalog available sources with their type, scope, and reliability
Extract key claims from each source with provenance tracking
Identify agreements: claims supported by multiple sources
Identify conflicts: where sources disagree and possible explanations
Resolve conflicts through authority weighting or contextual analysis
Compose a unified narrative that honestly represents certainty levels
expected_outcome: A coherent synthesis with claims attributed to sources, conflicts acknowledged, and certainty levels indicated.

title: Root Cause Analysis
description: Trace observed problems or unexpected results back to their fundamental causes using structured causal reasoning.
when_to_use: When a task involves understanding why something went wrong, diagnosing unexpected behavior, or finding the fundamental cause behind symptoms.
steps: Document the observed problem with specific symptoms
Generate candidate causes using the 5-Why technique
For each candidate: what evidence supports or refutes it
Distinguish correlation from causation in the evidence
Identify the root cause (the one that, if fixed, prevents recurrence)
Verify: would addressing this cause actually prevent the problem
expected_outcome: A causal chain from symptoms to root cause with evidence at each link and a verified fix.

title: Design Space Exploration
description: Systematically explore the space of possible designs or solutions by varying key parameters along defined axes.
when_to_use: When a task involves designing a system with many degrees of freedom, when you need to understand how design choices interact, or when looking for non-obvious solutions.
steps: Identify the key design dimensions and the range of values for each
Construct the design space as a multi-dimensional grid
Sample representative points in the design space (corners, center, edges)
For each sample: analyze feasibility, performance, and trade-offs
Identify regions of the design space that are Pareto-optimal
Map constraints that eliminate infeasible regions
expected_outcome: A characterized design space with Pareto frontiers, constraint boundaries, and recommended operating regions.

title: Evaluation Rubric Design
description: Design structured evaluation rubrics with clear criteria, scales, and anchor descriptions for consistent human or LLM-based assessment.
when_to_use: When a task requires creating a scoring system, defining quality criteria, or establishing standards for judging output quality.
steps: Define the dimensions of quality relevant to the task
For each dimension: define what excellent (5), adequate (3), and poor (1) look like
Write specific, observable anchor descriptions for each score level
Test the rubric on sample outputs: are raters consistent
Revise ambiguous criteria until inter-rater reliability is acceptable
Document the rubric with usage instructions and calibration examples
expected_outcome: A complete evaluation rubric with dimensions, anchor descriptions, calibration examples, and usage instructions.

title: A/B Test Design
description: Design controlled experiments to compare two or more variants with appropriate statistical rigor and power analysis.
when_to_use: When a task involves comparing approaches experimentally, designing controlled comparisons, or determining sample sizes for detecting effects.
steps: Define the hypothesis and primary metric
Identify all variables and determine which to control, randomize, and measure
Compute required sample size for desired effect size and power
Design the randomization and assignment procedure
Define analysis plan before seeing results (pre-registration)
Plan for multiple comparisons correction if testing multiple hypotheses
expected_outcome: A complete experimental design with hypothesis, power analysis, randomization scheme, and pre-registered analysis plan.

title: Benchmark Protocol Design
description: Design fair, comprehensive benchmarks that evaluate systems under controlled conditions with appropriate baselines and ablations.
when_to_use: When a task involves designing evaluation protocols, creating benchmarks, or ensuring fair comparison between systems.
steps: Define what the benchmark should measure and what it should NOT conflate
Select test cases that cover the full range of difficulty and variation
Design baselines: at minimum a random baseline, a simple heuristic, and state-of-the-art
Define the evaluation procedure with exact prompts, parameters, and preprocessing
Plan ablation studies to isolate the contribution of each component
Document the benchmark with reproduction instructions
expected_outcome: A complete benchmark protocol with test cases, baselines, ablations, and reproduction documentation.

title: Human Evaluation Design
description: Design human evaluation studies including annotator selection, task design, quality control, and agreement measurement.
when_to_use: When a task requires human judgment as part of evaluation, designing annotation tasks, or measuring inter-annotator agreement.
steps: Define what human judges should evaluate and at what granularity
Design the annotation interface with clear instructions and examples
Select appropriate annotator pool for the expertise needed
Implement quality control: calibration items, attention checks, overlap items
Measure inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa, Krippendorff's alpha)
Establish minimum agreement thresholds and adjudication procedures
expected_outcome: A human evaluation protocol with annotation guidelines, quality control plan, and agreement measurement approach.

title: Ablation Study Design
description: Design ablation studies that systematically remove or modify components to measure their individual contribution to overall performance.
when_to_use: When a task requires understanding which components of a system contribute to performance, isolating the effect of individual design choices, or justifying system complexity.
steps: List all components or design choices in the system
For each: define how to ablate it (remove, replace with simpler version, randomize)
Identify dependencies: which ablations can be independent and which interact
Design the ablation schedule (full factorial if feasible, otherwise prioritize)
Define the metric to measure impact of each ablation
Plan interaction analysis for non-independent components
expected_outcome: An ablation study design with component inventory, ablation methods, dependency analysis, and expected insights.

title: Reproducibility Assessment
description: Evaluate whether results are reproducible by checking methodology documentation, code availability, data access, and environmental specifications.
when_to_use: When a task involves assessing whether published results can be reproduced, documenting your own work for reproducibility, or identifying reproducibility risks.
steps: Check documentation completeness: are all parameters, seeds, and preprocessing steps specified
Check code availability: is the code released, does it run, does it produce the claimed results
Check data availability: is the exact data available, or can it be reconstructed
Check environment specifications: hardware, library versions, OS
Identify reproduction risks: stochastic components, deprecated APIs, proprietary dependencies
Propose a reproduction plan with expected effort and fallback strategies
expected_outcome: A reproducibility assessment with risk analysis, missing documentation, and a reproduction plan.

title: System Debugging Protocol
description: Systematically debug system failures by isolating components, testing hypotheses, and narrowing down root causes through binary search.
when_to_use: When a system is producing incorrect results, crashing, or behaving unexpectedly and the cause is not immediately obvious.
steps: Reproduce the problem with a minimal test case
Form hypotheses about the cause based on symptoms
Design a binary search: test the hypothesis that eliminates the most possibilities
Execute the test and observe results
Update hypotheses based on evidence and repeat until isolated
Verify the fix resolves the original problem and doesn't introduce regressions
expected_outcome: An isolated root cause with verified fix and regression check.

title: Pipeline Design and Orchestration
description: Design multi-stage processing pipelines with clear data contracts between stages, error handling, and monitoring.
when_to_use: When a task involves building or describing data processing pipelines, ETL flows, or multi-stage inference systems with defined interfaces between components.
steps: Define the pipeline's input, output, and quality requirements
Decompose into stages with clear single responsibilities
Define data contracts between stages (schema, format, invariants)
Design error handling: retry logic, dead-letter queues, fallback behavior
Add monitoring: throughput, latency, error rates per stage
Plan for scaling: which stages are bottlenecks and how to parallelize
expected_outcome: A pipeline design with stage definitions, data contracts, error handling, monitoring, and scaling plan.

title: Resource Estimation and Capacity Planning
description: Estimate computational resources (compute, memory, storage, time, cost) required for a project or experiment based on workload analysis.
when_to_use: When a task requires estimating costs, hardware requirements, time to completion, or API budget for a planned workload.
steps: Characterize the workload: input sizes, number of items, processing per item
Measure or estimate resource consumption per unit of work
Multiply by total workload with overhead margin (1.5x-2x for unknowns)
Identify the binding constraint (compute, memory, API rate limits, budget)
Compute total cost and elapsed time under the binding constraint
Plan for contingencies: what if estimates are 2x off
expected_outcome: A resource estimate with per-unit costs, total requirements, binding constraints, and contingency margins.

title: Monitoring and Observability Design
description: Design monitoring systems that provide visibility into system health, performance, and correctness through metrics, logs, and alerts.
when_to_use: When a task involves designing system monitoring, setting up alerting, or ensuring you can detect and diagnose problems in production.
steps: Identify the key signals that indicate system health (SLIs)
Set targets for each signal (SLOs)
Design metrics collection: what to measure, at what granularity
Set up alerting: thresholds, notification channels, escalation procedures
Design logging for diagnosis: structured logs with correlation IDs
Build dashboards that answer the first questions during an incident
expected_outcome: A monitoring design with SLIs, SLOs, metrics, alerts, logging strategy, and incident diagnosis dashboards.

title: Testing Strategy Design
description: Design comprehensive testing strategies including unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests with appropriate coverage targets.
when_to_use: When a task involves designing how to test a system, choosing testing approaches, or determining what level of test coverage is appropriate.
steps: Map the system's components and their interactions
For each component: identify the critical behaviors to test
Design the test pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, fewer E2E tests
Identify edge cases and boundary conditions for each component
Define coverage targets appropriate to the component's criticality
Plan for test maintenance: how to keep tests useful as the system evolves
expected_outcome: A testing strategy with test pyramid, coverage targets, edge case inventory, and maintenance plan.

title: LSTM Gradient Flow and Gate Mechanics
description: Long Short-Term Memory networks solve vanishing gradients through three gates (forget, input, output) that control information flow via element-wise multiplication. The forget gate f_t = σ(W_f·[h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) is the critical component — it determines gradient magnitude through the cell state highway. Common failure: forget gate bias initialized too low causes early information loss; standard practice is initializing forget bias to 1.0.
when_to_use: When explaining RNN variants, debugging sequence model training, or comparing LSTM vs GRU vs Transformer architectures on sequential data.
steps: Trace gradient flow through the cell state (the 'highway') — show how forget gate preserves gradients across time steps via ∂C_t/∂C_{t-1} = f_t
Explain each gate's role: forget (what to discard), input (what to add via tanh candidate), output (what to expose to next layer)
Compare gradient magnitudes: vanilla RNN O(λ^T) vs LSTM O(1) through cell state, where λ is the largest eigenvalue of the recurrent weight matrix
Identify common failure modes: forget gate saturation at 0 (information loss) or 1 (no forgetting), input gate competition, gradient clipping interactions at 1.0-5.0 threshold
expected_outcome: Clear explanation of LSTM gradient mechanics with specific mathematical relationships, initialization recommendations, and common debugging patterns.

title: Backpropagation Chain Rule Mechanics
description: Backpropagation computes gradients by recursive application of the chain rule: ∂L/∂w = ∂L/∂a · ∂a/∂z · ∂z/∂w, where a is the activation, z is the pre-activation, and w is a weight. For a network with L layers, the gradient at layer l involves a product of L-l Jacobian matrices. Numerical stability requires careful management of this product — if spectral norms exceed 1.0 consistently, gradients explode; below 1.0, they vanish.
when_to_use: When explaining how neural networks learn, debugging training instabilities, analyzing gradient flow through specific architectures, or discussing why certain initializations matter.
steps: Decompose the loss gradient into a chain of local Jacobians: ∂L/∂W_l = ∂L/∂a_L · ∏_{k=l+1}^{L} (∂a_k/∂a_{k-1}) · ∂a_l/∂W_l
Show the forward pass computation graph and identify which intermediate values must be cached for the backward pass (memory cost = O(n·L) for activations)
Compute local gradients for common operations: matmul (∂(Wx)/∂W = x^T), ReLU (∂/∂z = 1 if z>0, else 0), softmax-cross-entropy (y_pred - y_true)
Analyze gradient magnitude through depth — connect spectral norm of weight matrices to vanishing/exploding behavior
Demonstrate how initialization schemes (Xavier, He) set initial spectral norms near 1.0 to maintain gradient scale
expected_outcome: Step-by-step gradient computation through a multi-layer network with explicit Jacobian products and identification of numerical stability conditions.

title: Vanishing and Exploding Gradient Diagnosis
description: Vanishing gradients occur when the product of Jacobian spectral norms across layers is << 1, causing early layers to receive negligible updates. Exploding gradients produce the opposite: gradient norms grow exponentially, causing NaN losses or parameter divergence. Key diagnostic: plot per-layer gradient norms during training — healthy networks show roughly constant magnitude across layers (within 1-2 orders of magnitude).
when_to_use: When training deep networks that fail to converge, when loss plateaus early, when NaN/Inf appears in training, or when comparing architectural choices that affect gradient flow.
steps: Monitor gradient norm per layer: compute ||∂L/∂W_l||_2 for each layer l and plot across training steps
Diagnose vanishing: if gradient norms decrease by >10x per layer (e.g., 1e-2 at layer 10 vs 1e-8 at layer 2), early layers are effectively frozen
Diagnose exploding: gradient norms increasing per layer or sudden NaN — apply gradient clipping (clip_norm=1.0 is standard starting point) as immediate fix

Apply architectural solutions: residual connections (additive identity preserves gradient magnitude), batch/layer normalization (controls activation scale), careful initialization (He for ReLU: σ = √(2/n_in))
expected_outcome: Identification of gradient pathology type with specific numerical evidence and targeted architectural or hyperparameter fix.

title: GRU Architecture and LSTM Comparison
description: Gated Recurrent Units merge the forget and input gates into a single update gate z_t = σ(W_z·[h_{t-1}, x_t]) and remove the separate cell state, using: h_t = (1 - z_t) ⊙ h_{t-1} + z_t ⊙ tanh(W·[r_t ⊙ h_{t-1}, x_t]). With ~25% fewer parameters than LSTM (2 gates vs 3, no cell state), GRU trains faster and often matches LSTM on shorter sequences (<200 tokens) but underperforms on very long dependencies where the separate cell state highway matters.
when_to_use: When choosing between RNN variants for sequence tasks, when parameter efficiency matters, or when analyzing the relationship between gating mechanisms and memory capacity.
steps: Compare gate structures: GRU has update gate z_t (combines LSTM's forget+input) and reset gate r_t (controls how much past hidden state to expose)
Analyze parameter counts: GRU has 3n(2n+1) parameters vs LSTM's 4n(2n+1) for hidden size n — roughly 75% of LSTM parameter count
Trace gradient flow: GRU gradient through hidden state is (1-z_t) + z_t·∂h̃_t/∂h_{t-1}, which can maintain gradients but lacks the clean additive highway of LSTM's cell state

Apply selection criteria: prefer GRU for smaller datasets, shorter sequences, real-time applications; prefer LSTM for long sequences (>500 steps), complex temporal patterns, when cell state inspection is valuable for interpretability
expected_outcome: Informed selection between GRU and LSTM with specific parameter count comparisons, gradient flow analysis, and empirical performance boundaries.

title: Scaled Dot-Product and Multi-Head Attention
description: Self-attention computes Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V, where the √d_k scaling prevents softmax saturation as dimension grows (without scaling, dot products grow as O(d_k), pushing softmax into near-one-hot regime). Multi-head attention splits d_model into h heads of dimension d_k = d_model/h, allowing different heads to attend to different relationship types. Typical configs: BERT-base uses h=12, d_model=768, d_k=64; GPT-3 uses h=96, d_model=12288, d_k=128.
when_to_use: When explaining Transformer internals, analyzing attention patterns, debugging attention-based models, or comparing attention variants (linear, sparse, flash).
steps: Compute Q, K, V projections: Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V where W_Q, W_K ∈ ℝ^{d_model × d_k} and W_V ∈ ℝ^{d_model × d_v}
Show why scaling by √d_k is necessary: for random unit-variance q,k vectors of dim d_k, E[q·k] = 0 and Var[q·k] = d_k, so unscaled dots have std = √d_k
Demonstrate multi-head split: h parallel attention operations on d_k-dimensional slices, concatenated and projected through W_O ∈ ℝ^{hd_v × d_model}
Analyze computational cost: self-attention is O(n²d) for sequence length n, making it the bottleneck for long sequences (>2048 tokens typically)
expected_outcome: Complete mathematical walkthrough of multi-head attention with dimensionality tracking, scaling justification, and computational complexity analysis.

title: Positional Encoding in Transformers
description: Transformers are permutation-invariant without positional information, so position must be explicitly injected. Sinusoidal encoding uses PE(pos,2i) = sin(pos/10000^{2i/d_model}) and PE(pos,2i+1) = cos(pos/10000^{2i/d_model}), which allows the model to attend to relative positions since PE(pos+k) is a linear function of PE(pos). Learned positional embeddings (BERT, GPT-2) often outperform sinusoidal on fixed-length tasks but don't extrapolate. RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding) applies rotation matrices to Q,K pairs, enabling length generalization.
when_to_use: When designing or analyzing sequence models, when debugging position-dependent failures, when comparing transformer variants, or when dealing with variable-length inputs or length extrapolation.
steps: Explain why position encoding is necessary: self-attention treats input as a set (bag of tokens) — demonstrate with a permuted sequence producing identical attention weights

Derive sinusoidal properties: each dimension is a sinusoid with wavelength from 2π to 10000·2π, creating a unique position 'fingerprint' — show that PE(pos+k) = T_k · PE(pos) for a rotation matrix T_k

Compare encoding types: sinusoidal (fixed, extrapolates somewhat), learned absolute (flexible, no extrapolation beyond training length), RoPE (relative, good extrapolation, used in LLaMA/GPT-NeoX), ALiBi (linear bias, no parameters, used in BLOOM)

Identify failure modes: learned embeddings collapsing for rare positions, sinusoidal failing at very long contexts, RoPE frequency distribution needing extension (NTK-aware scaling) for lengths beyond training
expected_outcome: Understanding of positional encoding mechanics with selection guidance for different use cases and length requirements.

title: Transformer Layer Architecture: Residuals, Norms, and FFN
description: Each Transformer block contains two sub-layers: multi-head attention and a position-wise feed-forward network (FFN), each wrapped with residual connections and layer normalization. The FFN is typically a two-layer MLP: FFN(x) = GELU(xW_1 + b_1)W_2 + b_2, with inner dimension d_ff = 4·d_model by convention. Pre-norm (norm before sub-layer, used in GPT-2+) trains more stably than post-norm (original Transformer) because gradient magnitude is independent of depth.
when_to_use: When analyzing Transformer architectures in detail, debugging training stability issues, comparing pre-norm vs post-norm variants, or understanding the role of each component.
steps: Trace the data flow: input → LayerNorm → MultiHeadAttention → residual add → LayerNorm → FFN → residual add (pre-norm variant)
Explain residual connections' role: x + F(x) ensures gradient magnitude at least 1.0 at each layer, enabling training of 100+ layer models

Analyze the FFN: it acts as a key-value memory where W_1 rows are 'keys' (pattern detectors) and W_2 columns are 'values' (output patterns) — d_ff=4·d_model gives 4x overparameterization for this memory
Compare normalization placement: post-norm requires learning rate warmup (gradient scale varies with depth), pre-norm is stable without warmup but may have slightly lower final performance
Quantify parameter distribution: in a standard Transformer layer, ~33% of parameters are in attention (W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O) and ~67% are in the FFN (W_1, W_2)
expected_outcome: Detailed understanding of Transformer block architecture with specific parameter counts, gradient flow analysis, and design choice rationale.

title: Adam Optimizer Internals and Hyperparameter Tuning
description: Adam maintains per-parameter first moment (m_t = β1·m_{t-1} + (1-β1)·g_t) and second moment (v_t = β2·v_{t-1} + (1-β2)·g_t²) estimates with bias correction: m̂_t = m_t/(1-β1^t), v̂_t = v_t/(1-β2^t). The update is θ_t = θ_{t-1} - lr·m̂_t/(√v̂_t + ε). Default β1=0.9, β2=0.999, ε=1e-8 work well generally, but for Transformers β2=0.98 and ε=1e-6 are more stable. Adam's effective learning rate per parameter is lr·m̂/(√v̂+ε), which adapts to gradient statistics.
when_to_use: When selecting or tuning optimizers, debugging slow convergence or training instability, comparing optimizer behavior on different model architectures, or understanding adaptive learning rate methods.
steps: Derive Adam's update rule from first principles: combine momentum (SGD+momentum uses m_t) with RMSProp (uses v_t for per-parameter scaling), then add bias correction terms
Analyze the effective learning rate: lr/√v̂ acts as per-parameter rate — sparse gradients get larger updates (low v̂), dense gradients get smaller updates (high v̂)

Tune hyperparameters: β1 controls momentum decay (lower = less smoothing, try 0.85-0.95), β2 controls second moment decay (lower = faster adaptation to gradient scale changes), ε prevents division by zero but also sets minimum effective learning rate
Compare variants: AdamW (decoupled weight decay, preferred for Transformers), AdaFactor (memory-efficient, factored second moments), LAMB (layerwise scaling for large batch training)
expected_outcome: Complete understanding of Adam's mechanics with specific tuning recommendations for different model types and identification of when to use which variant.

title: Learning Rate Schedules and Warmup Strategies
description: Learning rate scheduling critically affects training dynamics. Linear warmup over 1-5% of total steps (e.g., 2000 steps for a 100K-step run) prevents early training divergence by allowing Adam's second moment estimates to stabilize. After warmup, cosine decay lr_t = lr_min + 0.5·(lr_max - lr_min)·(1 + cos(π·t/T)) is standard for Transformers, while step decay (÷10 at epochs 30,60,90) is common for CNNs. The peak learning rate scales roughly with √batch_size (linear scaling rule).
when_to_use: When setting up training loops, debugging early training instability, scaling to larger batch sizes, or comparing training recipes across different model sizes.
steps: Select warmup duration: 1-2% of total steps for large models (GPT-scale), up to 5-10% for smaller models; linear warmup from lr=0 to lr_max

Choose decay schedule: cosine annealing (smooth, standard for language models), linear decay (simpler, works for fine-tuning), step decay (÷10 at milestones, classic for ResNets), OneCycleLR (warmup + cosine in one cycle, good for super-convergence)
Apply batch size scaling: when increasing batch size by k, increase learning rate by √k (or linearly for SGD) and increase warmup proportionally
Debug common issues: lr too high → loss spikes or NaN in first 100 steps; lr too low → training plateaus early; no warmup with Adam → unstable first moments cause erratic updates
expected_outcome: A well-configured learning rate schedule with warmup, peak rate, and decay tailored to the specific model and dataset size.

title: SGD with Momentum and Nesterov Acceleration
description: SGD with momentum accumulates velocity v_t = μ·v_{t-1} - lr·∇L(θ_t) and updates θ_{t+1} = θ_t + v_t, where μ=0.9 is standard. This smooths gradient noise and accelerates through narrow valleys. Nesterov momentum evaluates the gradient at the 'lookahead' position: v_t = μ·v_{t-1} - lr·∇L(θ_t + μ·v_{t-1}), providing a corrective factor that reduces oscillation. SGD+momentum often generalizes better than Adam on image tasks (ResNet on ImageNet) but requires more careful lr tuning.
when_to_use: When training CNNs for image classification, when generalization is more important than convergence speed, when comparing optimizer effects on final model quality, or when training with large batch sizes.
steps: Implement basic momentum: maintain velocity buffer, update as v = μ·v - lr·grad, then θ += v — the velocity acts as an exponential moving average of past gradients

Understand Nesterov's advantage: by evaluating gradient at θ + μ·v (the position you'd reach with momentum alone), the gradient correction anticipates the next position, reducing overshooting by ~25% in practice
Tune momentum coefficient: μ=0.9 for most cases, μ=0.99 for very noisy gradients or large batch training, μ=0 reduces to vanilla SGD (use for debugging)
Apply weight decay correctly: with SGD, L2 regularization (adding λ·θ to gradient) is equivalent to weight decay (subtracting lr·λ·θ from parameters) — this equivalence breaks with Adam, hence AdamW
expected_outcome: Understanding of momentum mechanics with specific tuning guidance and clear distinction between classical and Nesterov variants.

title: Dropout Mechanics and Inference-Time Scaling
description: Dropout randomly zeros activations with probability p during training, then scales remaining activations by 1/(1-p) to maintain expected values (inverted dropout). This approximates an ensemble of 2^n sub-networks for n dropout units. Standard rates: p=0.1 for Transformer attention, p=0.1-0.3 for FFN layers, p=0.5 for fully-connected classification heads. Critical detail: dropout must be disabled during inference (model.eval() in PyTorch), and the scaling factor must match the training scheme.
when_to_use: When applying regularization to prevent overfitting, when tuning model capacity, when debugging train/eval discrepancies, or when analyzing the relationship between dropout and model uncertainty.
steps: Apply dropout correctly: during training, for each activation a_i, set a_i = 0 with probability p and a_i = a_i/(1-p) with probability (1-p) — this is inverted dropout (maintains scale without inference correction)

Select dropout rate by layer type: embedding dropout (0.1), attention dropout (0.1), FFN internal (0.1-0.3), final classifier (0.3-0.5) — higher rates for layers with more parameters or higher overfitting risk
Understand ensemble interpretation: each forward pass samples a different sub-network; at inference, using all weights with scaling approximates the geometric mean of all sub-network predictions

Debug common issues: train loss much lower than val loss with dropout off suggests underfitting the ensemble; dropout too high causes underfitting even on training set; spatial dropout (drop entire channels) is better for CNNs than element-wise
expected_outcome: Correct dropout implementation with layer-specific rate recommendations and understanding of the train/eval behavior difference.

title: Batch Normalization Internals and Failure Modes
description: Batch normalization normalizes each feature across the mini-batch: x̂_i = (x_i - μ_B)/√(σ²_B + ε), then applies learned affine transform: y_i = γ·x̂_i + β. During training it uses batch statistics; during inference it uses running estimates (momentum=0.1 by default in PyTorch: running_mean = 0.9·running_mean + 0.1·batch_mean). BN fails with small batch sizes (<16) because batch statistics become noisy, and it introduces train/eval discrepancy that can cause subtle bugs.
when_to_use: When designing CNN architectures, debugging batch-size-dependent performance, understanding normalization alternatives, or analyzing why a model performs differently in train vs eval mode.
steps: Trace BN computation: compute per-feature mean and variance over the batch dimension, normalize, then scale (γ) and shift (β) — these learned parameters allow the network to undo normalization if needed
Analyze gradient flow: BN acts as a gradient re-scaler, making the effective learning rate independent of weight magnitude — this is why BN enables higher learning rates

Identify failure modes: batch size < 16 (noisy statistics), varying batch sizes between train/eval, using BN with batch size 1 (undefined), running statistics diverging from actual statistics when data distribution shifts

Compare alternatives: Layer Norm (normalizes across features, batch-size independent, standard for Transformers), Group Norm (compromise: normalizes across channel groups, works with small batches), Instance Norm (per-sample per-channel, used in style transfer)
expected_outcome: Complete understanding of BN mechanics, its interaction with batch size and training/eval modes, and when to prefer alternative normalization methods.

title: Cross-Entropy Loss and Label Smoothing
description: Cross-entropy loss for classification is L = -∑_c y_c·log(p_c), which for one-hot labels simplifies to L = -log(p_correct). The gradient w.r.t. logits (before softmax) is simply p - y, making it numerically convenient. Label smoothing replaces one-hot targets with y_smooth = (1-α)·y_onehot + α/K for K classes (typical α=0.1), which prevents the model from becoming overconfident and improves calibration. For numerical stability, always use log-sum-exp: log(softmax(z_i)) = z_i - log(∑_j exp(z_j)).
when_to_use: When setting up classification training, when model predictions are overconfident, when analyzing calibration, or when comparing loss function choices for different tasks.
steps: Implement numerically stable cross-entropy: compute log_softmax using the log-sum-exp trick (subtract max logit before exp), never compute softmax then log separately
Apply label smoothing: replace hard targets [1,0,0,...] with [1-α+α/K, α/K, α/K,...] — this adds a KL divergence penalty toward uniform distribution, acting as a confidence regularizer
Analyze gradient behavior: ∂L/∂z_i = softmax(z_i) - y_i, which means gradients are bounded in [-1, 1] and largest for the most confident wrong predictions

Compare loss variants: focal loss (γ=2 standard) down-weights easy examples for imbalanced data, binary cross-entropy for multi-label, KL divergence for distribution matching (knowledge distillation with temperature T=4-20)
expected_outcome: Numerically stable loss implementation with appropriate smoothing and variant selection based on the specific classification scenario.

title: Contrastive and Triplet Loss for Representation Learning
description: Contrastive loss (SimCLR, CLIP) pulls positive pairs together and pushes negative pairs apart in embedding space: L = -log(exp(sim(z_i, z_j)/τ) / ∑_k exp(sim(z_i, z_k)/τ)), where τ is temperature (0.05-0.1 typical). Triplet loss uses L = max(0, d(anchor, positive) - d(anchor, negative) + margin), where margin=0.2-1.0. Both require careful negative mining — random negatives are too easy after early training; semi-hard negatives (d(a,n) > d(a,p) but d(a,n) < d(a,p) + margin) produce the best gradients.
when_to_use: When training embedding models, building retrieval systems, doing metric learning, or when classification labels are unavailable but similarity structure exists.
steps: Choose loss type: contrastive (InfoNCE) for self-supervised learning with augmented views, triplet loss for supervised metric learning with known positive/negative pairs

Design negative sampling: random negatives (easy, fast convergence initially), hard negatives (closest incorrect examples, can cause collapse), semi-hard negatives (best gradient signal, mine from batch)

Tune temperature (contrastive) or margin (triplet): lower τ sharpens the similarity distribution (more discriminative but harder to optimize), higher margin demands more separation (may underfit if too large)

Monitor for collapse: if all embeddings converge to the same point or a low-dimensional subspace, increase negative difficulty, add normalization to the embedding space, or use asymmetric architectures (BYOL, SimSiam)
expected_outcome: Well-configured contrastive or triplet learning setup with appropriate negative mining strategy, temperature/margin tuning, and collapse prevention.

title: Convolution Arithmetic and Receptive Fields
description: A convolution layer with kernel size k, stride s, padding p, and dilation d produces output size: out = ⌊(in + 2p - d(k-1) - 1)/s + 1⌋. The receptive field grows with depth: for L layers of 3×3 convolutions with stride 1, the receptive field is (2L+1)×(2L+1). Dilated convolutions expand this exponentially: dilation rates [1,2,4,8] give receptive field equivalent to a 15×15 kernel with only 4×9=36 parameters instead of 225. Parameter count per layer: k² · C_in · C_out + C_out (bias).
when_to_use: When designing CNN architectures, calculating output dimensions, analyzing what spatial context a network can access, or debugging shape mismatches in convolutional networks.
steps: Compute output spatial dimensions: apply formula out = ⌊(in + 2p - d(k-1) - 1)/s⌋ + 1 for each spatial dimension; for 'same' padding, set p = ⌊k/2⌋ with stride 1
Calculate receptive field: for a stack of layers, RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (k_l - 1) · ∏_{i=1}^{l-1} s_i · d_l — stride multiplies receptive field growth of all subsequent layers
Count parameters: conv layer has k_h × k_w × C_in × C_out + C_out parameters; a 3×3 conv with 256→256 channels = 590,080 params; 1×1 conv with 256→256 = 65,792 params (pointwise channel mixing)

Apply design patterns: use 3×3 convolutions (two 3×3 = 5×5 receptive field with fewer params), 1×1 convolutions for channel reduction (bottleneck), depthwise separable (k²·C + C·C' instead of k²·C·C') for efficiency
expected_outcome: Correct dimension calculations, receptive field analysis, and parameter-efficient design choices for convolutional architectures.

title: Residual Connections and Skip Connection Variants
description: Residual connections (y = F(x) + x) enable training of very deep networks (100+ layers) by providing gradient highways — the gradient ∂y/∂x = ∂F/∂x + I ensures a minimum gradient magnitude of 1.0 through the identity path. ResNet variants include pre-activation (BN→ReLU→Conv, better gradient flow), bottleneck blocks (1×1→3×3→1×1, reducing computation by 4x), and ResNeXt (grouped convolutions with cardinality). Dense connections (DenseNet: concatenation instead of addition) provide even stronger gradient flow but at higher memory cost.
when_to_use: When designing deep architectures, when training fails beyond certain depth, when analyzing gradient flow properties, or when comparing residual architectures (ResNet, DenseNet, U-Net).
steps: Analyze gradient flow: for L residual blocks, the gradient from loss to input involves a sum of 2^L paths of different lengths — even if many paths have vanishing gradients, the short paths (through identity connections) maintain learning
Choose residual block type: basic block (two 3×3 convs, for ResNet-18/34) vs bottleneck (1×1→3×3→1×1, for ResNet-50+; reduces FLOPs by ~4x at same depth)
Handle dimension mismatches: when spatial dimensions or channels change, use projection shortcuts (1×1 conv with stride) — zero-padding shortcuts save parameters but slightly hurt performance
Compare skip connection types: additive (ResNet, U-Net decoder), concatenative (DenseNet, U-Net skip), gated (Highway Networks, LSTM-style gating: y = g⊙F(x) + (1-g)⊙x)
expected_outcome: Principled selection and implementation of skip connections with gradient flow analysis supporting the design choice.

title: Activation Functions: ReLU Variants and Selection
description: ReLU (max(0,x)) is default but suffers from 'dying ReLU' — neurons with consistently negative inputs have zero gradient and never recover (can affect 10-40% of neurons in deep networks). Leaky ReLU (max(αx,x), α=0.01) fixes this with negligible cost. GELU (x·Φ(x) ≈ 0.5x(1+tanh(√(2/π)(x+0.044715x³)))) is standard for Transformers — it's smooth, non-monotonic near 0, and provides stochastic regularization. Swish/SiLU (x·σ(x)) performs similarly to GELU.
when_to_use: When choosing activation functions for new architectures, diagnosing dead neurons, understanding why Transformers use GELU instead of ReLU, or debugging gradient flow through activations.
steps: Diagnose dying ReLU: monitor fraction of neurons with zero activation across a validation batch — if >20% are permanently zero, consider Leaky ReLU or reduce learning rate

Select by architecture: ReLU for CNNs (fast, hardware-optimized), GELU for Transformers (smooth gradient aids attention learning), Swish for EfficientNet-style models, Mish (x·tanh(softplus(x))) for YOLO-v4+

Analyze gradient properties: ReLU gradient is 0 or 1 (no vanishing in positive regime), GELU gradient is smooth between 0 and 1+ (non-zero everywhere), sigmoid/tanh saturate at extremes (vanishing gradient for |x| > 4)
Consider output layer activations separately: sigmoid for binary/multi-label, softmax for multi-class, none (linear) for regression, tanh for bounded outputs
expected_outcome: Appropriate activation function selection with awareness of failure modes and architecture-specific conventions.

title: Weight Initialization Schemes
description: Proper initialization keeps activation variance constant across layers, preventing vanishing/exploding signals before training starts. Xavier/Glorot init (Var = 2/(n_in + n_out)) assumes linear activations and is correct for tanh/sigmoid. He/Kaiming init (Var = 2/n_in) accounts for ReLU zeroing half the distribution. For Transformers, GPT-2 scales residual layer weights by 1/√(2N) where N is the number of layers, preventing the residual stream variance from growing with depth.
when_to_use: When setting up new model training, when training diverges in early steps, when porting architectures to new frameworks, or when analyzing training dynamics of very deep networks.
steps: Match initialization to activation: Xavier for tanh/sigmoid (Var=2/(fan_in+fan_out)), He for ReLU (Var=2/fan_in, normal or uniform), LeCun for SELU (Var=1/fan_in)
Apply to different layer types: linear/dense layers use fan_in/fan_out based on weight matrix shape, conv layers compute fan based on kernel_size²×channels, embedding layers typically use N(0, 0.02)
Scale for depth: for very deep residual networks, scale the final layer of each residual block by 1/√N_layers (GPT-2 approach) or use fixup initialization (zero-init residual branches)

Verify initialization: forward pass a random batch before training — if activation magnitudes change by >10x across layers, initialization is wrong; standard deviation should be roughly 1.0 at each layer
expected_outcome: Correct initialization scheme selection with variance analysis confirming stable signal propagation through the network.

title: L1, L2 Regularization, and Weight Decay Distinction
description: L2 regularization adds λ·||w||² to the loss, producing gradient λ·2w (shrinks weights proportionally). L1 adds λ·||w||_1, producing gradient λ·sign(w) (constant magnitude push toward zero, inducing sparsity). Critical distinction: for SGD, L2 regularization and weight decay are equivalent, but for Adam they differ — AdamW applies weight decay directly (θ -= lr·λ·θ) rather than through the gradient (which gets normalized by Adam's second moment). This matters: AdamW with weight_decay=0.01 is now standard for Transformers.
when_to_use: When configuring regularization for training, when choosing between L1/L2/weight_decay, when migrating between optimizers, or when analyzing model sparsity patterns.
steps: Choose regularization type: L2/weight decay for general regularization (keeps all weights small), L1 for feature selection/sparsity (drives weights to exactly zero), elastic net (α·L1 + (1-α)·L2) for compromise

Set regularization strength: typical weight_decay=0.01 for AdamW on Transformers, weight_decay=1e-4 for SGD on CNNs; too high → underfitting (training loss stays high), too low → no regularization effect

Implement correctly for Adam: use AdamW (decoupled weight decay) not Adam + L2 — the difference is that Adam normalizes the L2 gradient term by second moments, weakening regularization for parameters with large gradients

Exclude appropriate parameters: never regularize bias terms or layer norm parameters (they need to be free to shift distributions); in PyTorch, create separate parameter groups with weight_decay=0 for these
expected_outcome: Correctly configured regularization with proper parameter group separation and optimizer-specific implementation.

title: VAE Reparameterization Trick and ELBO
description: Variational Autoencoders maximize the evidence lower bound (ELBO): L = E_q[log p(x|z)] - KL(q(z|x) || p(z)), where the first term is reconstruction quality and the KL term regularizes the latent space toward the prior N(0,I). The reparameterization trick enables backprop through sampling: instead of z ~ N(μ, σ²), compute z = μ + σ ⊙ ε where ε ~ N(0,I). This moves the stochasticity to the input (ε), making z differentiable w.r.t. μ and σ. Common issue: KL collapse (posterior matches prior, decoder ignores z) — fix with KL annealing (β from 0→1 over training) or free bits (min KL per dimension).
when_to_use: When explaining generative model mechanics, implementing VAEs, debugging latent space quality, or comparing VAE vs GAN vs diffusion approaches to generation.
steps: Derive the ELBO from log p(x) ≥ E_q[log p(x|z)] - KL(q||p): show that the gap equals KL(q(z|x)||p(z|x)) ≥ 0, meaning maximizing ELBO tightens the bound on log-likelihood
Implement the reparameterization trick: encoder outputs μ and log(σ²) (log-variance for numerical stability), sample ε ~ N(0,I), compute z = μ + exp(0.5·log(σ²))·ε
Compute KL divergence analytically for Gaussian: KL(N(μ,σ²)||N(0,1)) = 0.5·∑(μ² + σ² - log(σ²) - 1)

Address posterior collapse: apply β-VAE annealing (start β=0, increase to 1 over 10-50% of training), use free bits (δ=0.5 per dimension minimum KL), or add skip connections to make decoder depend on z
expected_outcome: Working VAE implementation with proper reparameterization, ELBO computation, and strategies to prevent posterior collapse.

title: GAN Training Dynamics and Mode Collapse
description: GANs train a generator G and discriminator D in a minimax game: min_G max_D E[log D(x)] + E[log(1-D(G(z)))]. Training is inherently unstable because the optimal D provides vanishing gradients to G (when D is perfect, ∂log(1-D(G(z)))/∂G → 0). Mode collapse occurs when G maps all z to a few outputs that fool D. Practical solutions: Wasserstein loss with gradient penalty (WGAN-GP), spectral normalization of D, progressive growing, two-timescale learning rates (D:G ratio of 2:1 to 5:1 updates).
when_to_use: When implementing or debugging generative adversarial networks, when comparing GAN variants, when diagnosing training instability in adversarial setups.
steps: Analyze the training objective: D maximizes log(D(x)) + log(1-D(G(z))), G minimizes log(1-D(G(z))) or equivalently maximizes log(D(G(z))) (non-saturating loss, better gradients early in training)

Diagnose mode collapse: check generated sample diversity (FID between batches should be low), monitor D(G(z)) — if it's consistently high but generated images lack diversity, G found a mode that fools D

Stabilize training: apply spectral normalization to D (constrains Lipschitz constant ≤ 1), use WGAN-GP (gradient penalty λ=10 on interpolated samples), balance D/G training (train D more often if it's losing)
Evaluate quantitatively: FID score (lower is better, <50 is decent, <10 is good for faces), IS (Inception Score), precision/recall for disentangling quality vs diversity
expected_outcome: Stable GAN training setup with appropriate loss function, regularization, and evaluation metrics to detect and fix common failure modes.

title: Diffusion Model Forward and Reverse Process
description: Diffusion models gradually add Gaussian noise over T steps (forward process): q(x_t|x_{t-1}) = N(x_t; √(1-β_t)·x_{t-1}, β_t·I), then learn to reverse: p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t). The key insight is that x_t can be sampled directly from x_0: x_t = √ᾱ_t·x_0 + √(1-ᾱ_t)·ε where ᾱ_t = ∏(1-β_i). The network predicts the noise ε_θ(x_t, t), trained with simple MSE: L = ||ε - ε_θ(x_t, t)||². Typical T=1000 with linear β schedule from 1e-4 to 0.02; DDPM sampling takes T steps but DDIM reduces to ~50 steps via deterministic sampling.
when_to_use: When explaining diffusion model mechanics, comparing with VAEs/GANs, analyzing image generation architectures, or discussing noise schedules and sampling strategies.
steps: Derive the closed-form sampling: show q(x_t|x_0) = N(√ᾱ_t·x_0, (1-ᾱ_t)·I), enabling direct jump from x_0 to any x_t without sequential noise addition
Explain the denoising objective: the network ε_θ(x_t, t) takes noisy image and timestep, predicts the noise added — equivalent to predicting x_0 or the score ∇_x log p(x_t)

Analyze noise schedules: linear (β from 1e-4 to 0.02, original DDPM), cosine (ᾱ_t = cos²(π/2 · (t/T + s)/(1+s)), better for high resolution), sigmoid (shifted sigmoid curve, stable for latent diffusion)

Compare sampling methods: DDPM (stochastic, T steps, highest quality), DDIM (deterministic, 20-100 steps, slightly lower quality), DPM-Solver (ODE solver, 10-20 steps), classifier-free guidance (scale=7.5 typical for image generation)
expected_outcome: Understanding of diffusion model mathematics with practical knowledge of noise schedules, training objectives, and efficient sampling strategies.

title: Feature Normalization and Preprocessing Strategies
description: Input feature normalization dramatically affects optimization: unnormalized features create elongated loss surfaces where gradient descent oscillates. Standard scaling (z = (x-μ)/σ) gives zero mean and unit variance, optimal for most neural networks. Min-max scaling ([0,1]) preserves zero entries (important for sparse data). For image data, per-channel normalization (ImageNet: mean=[0.485,0.456,0.406], std=[0.229,0.224,0.225]) is standard. Log transforms help with power-law distributed features (e.g., word frequencies, prices).
when_to_use: When preprocessing data for model training, when debugging poor convergence with heterogeneous features, when transferring pretrained models that expect specific input distributions.
steps: Select normalization strategy: standard scaling for dense features with Gaussian-like distribution, min-max for bounded features or when zero matters, robust scaling (using median/IQR) for data with outliers
Apply per-feature statistics: always fit on training set only, transform train/val/test with same parameters — data leakage from computing stats on full dataset inflates validation performance

Handle special cases: log(1+x) for right-skewed distributions, power transforms (Box-Cox, Yeo-Johnson) for non-Gaussian features, leave ordinal encodings as-is (or treat as categorical with embeddings)

Verify normalization: after preprocessing, check each feature has mean≈0, std≈1 (for standard scaling); check for NaN/Inf from zero-variance features; monitor first-layer activations to confirm reasonable magnitude
expected_outcome: Properly normalized input features with train/val/test consistency and verification of numerical stability.

title: Data Augmentation for Images and Text
description: Data augmentation provides training-time regularization by artificially expanding the dataset. For images: random crop (224×224 from 256×256), horizontal flip, color jitter (brightness/contrast/saturation ±0.4), RandAugment (N=2 random operations at magnitude M=9 from a pool of 14, standard for ViT training). For text: back-translation, synonym replacement, random deletion (p=0.1), and EDA (Easy Data Augmentation). Mixup (x̃ = λ·x_i + (1-λ)·x_j, λ~Beta(α,α) with α=0.2) and CutMix interpolate between samples for improved calibration.
when_to_use: When training on limited data, when model is overfitting, when designing training pipelines for vision or NLP tasks, or when comparing augmentation strategies.
steps: Select augmentation strategy by data type: images (geometric + color + erasing), text (back-translation + synonym + random ops), tabular (SMOTE for class imbalance, noise injection, feature dropout)
Configure augmentation intensity: start mild and increase if overfitting persists — RandAugment M=5-15 (higher = stronger augmentation), color jitter ±0.2-0.4, random erasing p=0.25

Apply sample-mixing methods: Mixup (linear interpolation of images and labels, α=0.1-0.4), CutMix (paste region from one image onto another, better than Cutout), both improve calibration and reduce overconfidence

Validate augmentation doesn't corrupt signal: ensure augmented samples are still recognizable/valid (e.g., don't flip text, don't rotate digits 6/9, don't aggressively crop small objects); check that augmented training converges
expected_outcome: A well-tuned augmentation pipeline appropriate for the data modality with verified augmentation quality.

title: Knowledge Distillation: Temperature and Loss Design
description: Knowledge distillation transfers knowledge from a large teacher to a smaller student by training on soft targets: L = α·T²·KL(σ(z_t/T), σ(z_s/T)) + (1-α)·CE(y, σ(z_s)), where T is temperature (4-20 typical), σ is softmax, z_t/z_s are teacher/student logits. The T² factor compensates for temperature scaling reducing gradient magnitude. Higher T reveals more inter-class relationships (dark knowledge) — at T→∞, soft targets approach class frequencies. Common recipe: T=4-8, α=0.5-0.9 (weight soft targets more), train student 2-3x longer than standalone training.
when_to_use: When compressing large models for deployment, when teacher-student training is proposed, when analyzing what knowledge transfers between models, or when comparing compression methods.
steps: Set up distillation loss: compute teacher soft targets with temperature T (higher reveals more structure), student trained on weighted combination of soft target KL divergence and hard target cross-entropy
Select temperature: T=1 gives hard targets (no distillation benefit), T=4-8 for most tasks, T=20+ for very confident teachers — tune by monitoring student validation accuracy

Configure training: student typically needs longer training (2-3x epochs), lower learning rate than standalone, α=0.7-0.9 (emphasize teacher knowledge), optionally match intermediate representations (FitNets, attention transfer)

Evaluate compression ratio: measure accuracy retention (student acc / teacher acc × 100%) vs compression (teacher params / student params) — good distillation retains >95% accuracy at 4-10x compression
expected_outcome: Effective distillation setup with appropriate temperature, loss weighting, and training schedule that achieves target compression with minimal accuracy loss.

title: Model Pruning: Structured vs Unstructured
description: Pruning removes parameters to reduce model size and computation. Unstructured pruning (magnitude-based: remove weights with |w| < threshold) achieves high sparsity (90-95%) with minimal accuracy loss but requires sparse hardware for speedup. Structured pruning removes entire filters/attention heads/layers, giving immediate speedup on standard hardware but less aggressive compression. The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis shows that sparse subnetworks exist at initialization that can match dense performance — but finding them requires train→prune→rewind→retrain cycles.
when_to_use: When deploying models on resource-constrained devices, when analyzing model redundancy, when comparing compression techniques, or when discussing efficient inference.
steps: Choose pruning granularity: unstructured (individual weights, highest compression ratio, needs sparse hardware), structured (whole filters/heads, 2-4x speedup on standard GPUs), semi-structured (2:4 sparsity on Ampere GPUs, 2x speedup guaranteed)
Apply magnitude pruning: rank all weights by |w|, remove bottom p% (start p=50%, incrementally increase to 90-95%), retrain for 10-30% of original schedule after each pruning step
Implement iterative pruning schedule: gradual pruning outperforms one-shot — remove 20% per round over 5 rounds to reach 67% sparsity total, retraining between rounds
Evaluate: measure actual latency (not just FLOPs) on target hardware, check accuracy vs sparsity curve — expect <1% accuracy drop at 50% sparsity, 1-3% drop at 90% sparsity for well-tuned pruning
expected_outcome: Pruning strategy selection with appropriate granularity, schedule, and evaluation metrics for the target deployment scenario.

title: Quantization: INT8, INT4, and Mixed Precision
description: Quantization reduces numerical precision to shrink model size and speed up inference. FP16 mixed precision training (FP16 forward/backward, FP32 master weights) gives ~2x training speedup with minimal accuracy loss using loss scaling. Post-training quantization (PTQ) maps FP32→INT8 with calibration: q = clip(round(x/scale + zero_point), 0, 255), where scale = (max-min)/255. QAT (quantization-aware training) simulates quantization during training via straight-through estimators, achieving INT4 with <1% accuracy drop on LLMs (GPTQ, AWQ).
when_to_use: When optimizing model inference speed, reducing memory footprint for deployment, understanding precision-performance tradeoffs, or comparing model compression approaches.
steps: Select quantization approach: PTQ for quick deployment (calibrate on 128-1024 samples), QAT for aggressive quantization (INT4/INT8 with minimal loss), mixed precision for training speedup

Configure calibration: collect activation statistics (min/max or percentile-based for outlier robustness) on representative data; per-channel quantization for weights, per-tensor for activations (trade-off between accuracy and overhead)

Apply to specific layers: quantize linear/conv layers (most compute), keep layer norms and attention softmax in FP16/FP32 (sensitive to precision), first and last layers often kept at higher precision
Validate: compare quantized vs full-precision output distributions (cosine similarity >0.99 is good), benchmark actual throughput on target hardware (INT8 Tensor Cores give 2-4x speedup on A100)
expected_outcome: Quantized model with verified accuracy retention and measured inference speedup on target hardware.

title: Ensemble Methods: Bagging, Boosting, and Stacking
description: Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) trains models on bootstrap samples and averages predictions, reducing variance without increasing bias — Random Forest adds feature subsampling (√p features per split). Boosting (AdaBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM) trains sequentially, each model focusing on previous errors: XGBoost uses regularized objective L = ∑l(y_i, ŷ_i) + ∑(γT + 0.5λ||w||²) where T is number of leaves. Stacking uses a meta-learner trained on base model predictions. Typical ensemble of 5 models reduces error by 2-5% on Kaggle benchmarks.
when_to_use: When maximum prediction accuracy is needed, when comparing single model vs ensemble performance, when designing ML competition solutions, or when explaining gradient boosting internals.
steps: Select ensemble strategy: bagging for high-variance models (deep trees, neural nets), boosting for high-bias models (shallow trees), stacking for combining heterogeneous models

Configure gradient boosting: learning_rate=0.01-0.1 (lower = more trees needed but better), max_depth=3-8, n_estimators=100-3000, early stopping on validation set (patience=50 rounds), subsample=0.8 for regularization

Implement stacking: train k base models with k-fold cross-validation (out-of-fold predictions for meta-features), train meta-learner (logistic regression or shallow neural net) on concatenated OOF predictions

Analyze diversity: ensemble benefit comes from uncorrelated errors — use different algorithms, different feature subsets, different training data samples; measure pairwise prediction correlation (lower is better for ensemble)
expected_outcome: Well-configured ensemble with diverse base models and appropriate combination strategy for the target performance requirement.

title: Bias-Variance Tradeoff and Overfitting Diagnostics
description: Total error decomposes as E[(y-ŷ)²] = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Noise. High bias (underfitting): both train and val loss are high, model is too simple. High variance (overfitting): train loss is low but val loss is high, model memorizes rather than generalizes. The gap between train and val loss is the key diagnostic — typical healthy gap is 5-15% of validation loss. Learning curves (loss vs training set size) reveal whether more data helps (high variance) or a more complex model is needed (high bias).
when_to_use: When diagnosing why a model underperforms, when deciding whether to add more data or increase model complexity, when tuning regularization, or when explaining fundamental ML concepts.
steps: Plot learning curves: train loss and val loss vs (a) training steps and (b) training set size — if both plateau high, it's bias; if they diverge, it's variance
Quantify the gap: compute (val_loss - train_loss) / val_loss — if >20%, likely overfitting; if <5% and both are high, likely underfitting

Apply targeted fixes: for high bias → increase model capacity, add features, reduce regularization, train longer; for high variance → add regularization (dropout, weight decay), get more data, reduce model size, use ensemble
Use validation strategies: k-fold cross-validation (k=5-10) gives robust estimate of generalization, stratified splits for imbalanced classes, time-based splits for temporal data
expected_outcome: Clear diagnosis of bias vs variance problem with specific remediation strategy supported by learning curve analysis.

title: Transfer Learning and Fine-Tuning Strategies
description: Transfer learning reuses representations from a pretrained model (typically trained on large data like ImageNet or web text) for a target task. Three strategies: (1) linear probing — freeze all layers, train only the classification head (best for small target datasets or when domains are very similar); (2) full fine-tuning — unfreeze all parameters with lower learning rate (2e-5 for BERT-base vs 3e-4 for training from scratch); (3) progressive unfreezing — unfreeze layers gradually from top to bottom over epochs, preserving low-level features. Feature reuse decreases with domain distance.
when_to_use: When training on limited labeled data, when leveraging pretrained models, when adapting foundation models to new domains, or when comparing fine-tuning approaches.
steps: Assess domain distance: similar domain (ImageNet→medical imaging) = linear probe first; distant domain (text→code) = may need full fine-tuning with careful learning rate
Set layer-wise learning rates: lower layers (general features) get lr/100, middle layers get lr/10, top layers and new head get full lr — this is discriminative fine-tuning from ULMFiT

Choose fine-tuning recipe: learning rate 2e-5 to 5e-5 for BERT-style models, weight decay 0.01, linear warmup for 6-10% of steps then linear/cosine decay, 3-5 epochs for most NLP tasks, 10-30 epochs for vision

Apply parameter-efficient methods when full fine-tuning is infeasible: LoRA (adds low-rank matrices, r=8-64, trains 0.1-1% of parameters), prefix tuning (prepends learnable tokens), adapters (small bottleneck modules between frozen layers)
expected_outcome: Effective transfer learning setup with appropriate strategy selection based on dataset size, domain distance, and compute budget.

title: Focal Loss for Class Imbalance
description: Focal loss modifies cross-entropy by down-weighting easy examples: FL(p_t) = -α_t(1-p_t)^γ · log(p_t), where γ is the focusing parameter (γ=2 standard) and α is class weight. When γ=0, it reduces to standard cross-entropy. For a well-classified example with p_t=0.9, the focal weight (1-0.9)²=0.01 reduces its loss by 100x compared to a hard example with p_t=0.1 where weight is (1-0.1)²=0.81. Originally designed for object detection (RetinaNet) where background:foreground ratio can be 100000:1.
when_to_use: When dealing with severe class imbalance, when training object detectors with many negative examples, when standard cross-entropy focuses too much on easy examples, or when comparing loss functions for imbalanced datasets.
steps: Diagnose the need: compute class distribution — if minority class is <10% of data or if easy negatives dominate the gradient, focal loss can help
Set focal parameters: γ=2.0 is the standard starting point (higher γ = more focus on hard examples, diminishing returns above γ=5), α=0.25 for the dominant class in binary detection tasks
Implement correctly: FL = -α_t · (1-p_t)^γ · log(p_t) where p_t = p if y=1 else 1-p; use numerically stable version with log_softmax and clamp p_t to [ε, 1-ε]

Compare alternatives: class-weighted cross-entropy (simpler, often sufficient for moderate imbalance), OHEM (Online Hard Example Mining, selects top-k hardest examples per batch), balanced sampling (oversample minority class)
expected_outcome: Appropriate loss function selection for imbalanced classification with correctly configured focal parameters and comparison against simpler alternatives.

title: Layer Normalization vs Batch Normalization in Transformers
description: Layer normalization computes statistics across the feature dimension for each sample independently: LN(x) = γ · (x - μ_feat) / √(σ²_feat + ε) + β, where μ_feat and σ_feat are computed over the d_model dimension. Unlike batch norm, it has no batch dependency — same computation in train and eval, works with batch_size=1, and handles variable-length sequences naturally. In Transformers, LN is applied per-token (normalizing the d_model-dimensional representation), while BN would normalize across the batch and sequence dimensions, mixing statistics of different positions.
when_to_use: When choosing normalization for Transformer or RNN architectures, when debugging train/eval inconsistencies, when batch size is constrained, or when analyzing why certain normalization works better for specific architectures.
steps: Understand the normalization axis: BN normalizes across batch dimension (features share statistics across samples), LN normalizes across feature dimension (each sample is independently normalized)

Explain why LN suits Transformers: sequence lengths vary (BN would compute statistics over different-length sequences), autoregressive decoding uses batch_size=1 (BN undefined), attention mechanisms benefit from per-token normalization
Apply RMSNorm (used in LLaMA, Gemma): RMS(x) = x / √(mean(x²) + ε) · γ — simpler than LN (no centering/bias), empirically equivalent performance, ~10% faster
Compare normalization statistics: BN has 2·d learnable parameters + 2·d running statistics, LN has 2·d learnable parameters + 0 running statistics, RMSNorm has d learnable parameters
expected_outcome: Correct normalization choice based on architecture type with understanding of the computational and statistical differences.

title: Attention Pattern Analysis and Sparse Attention
description: Standard self-attention is O(n²) in sequence length, becoming the bottleneck at n>2048. Attention patterns reveal model behavior: early layers attend locally (±50 tokens), later layers attend to semantically related tokens regardless of distance, and some heads specialize (positional heads, syntactic heads, rare-token heads). Sparse attention reduces complexity: local windows (Longformer, window_size=256), strided patterns, global tokens (CLS attends to everything), and their combinations achieve O(n√n) or O(n·log(n)). Flash Attention doesn't change the mathematical result but reduces memory from O(n²) to O(n) via tiling.
when_to_use: When processing long sequences (>2048 tokens), when analyzing what a Transformer has learned, when optimizing attention memory usage, or when comparing efficient Transformer variants.
