steps: Analyze attention patterns: extract attention weight matrices, visualize per-head patterns — look for diagonal (local), vertical stripes (global tokens), block patterns (segment-level), or diffuse (semantic) attention

Select sparse attention pattern: sliding window (O(n·w) for window w, good for local tasks), dilated window (larger effective receptive field), global+local (Longformer: few global tokens + sliding window), block-sparse (BigBird: random blocks + local + global)

Implement Flash Attention for memory efficiency: mathematically identical to standard attention but uses tiling to avoid materializing the n×n attention matrix, reducing memory from O(n²) to O(n) and improving speed 2-4x on GPU
Evaluate attention head importance: compute head importance scores (gradient-based or ablation), prune redundant heads — typically 20-40% of heads can be removed with <1% accuracy loss
expected_outcome: Efficient attention configuration for the target sequence length with analysis of attention pattern behavior and memory optimization.

title: Pooling Operations and Global Context Aggregation
description: Pooling reduces spatial dimensions while preserving important features. Max pooling (takes maximum value in each window) preserves dominant features and introduces translation invariance. Average pooling preserves mean activation, better for dense features. Global Average Pooling (GAP) replaces fully-connected layers by averaging each feature map to a single value — introduced in Network-in-Network and used by GoogLeNet/ResNet (reduces parameters from millions to thousands). Adaptive pooling in PyTorch outputs a fixed-size regardless of input dimensions, enabling arbitrary input sizes.
when_to_use: When designing CNN architectures, when choosing between pooling strategies, when connecting convolutional features to classification heads, or when analyzing translation invariance properties.
steps: Choose pooling type: max pooling for texture/edge-dominant tasks (preserves strongest activations), average pooling for holistic features (preserves overall distribution), stochastic pooling (sample from activation distribution) for regularization

Apply Global Average Pooling: replace final FC layers with GAP — for a feature map of H×W×C, GAP produces a C-dimensional vector; eliminates spatial dimensions entirely, adds 0 parameters, acts as structural regularizer

Configure pooling parameters: typical 2×2 with stride 2 (halves spatial dims), 3×3 with stride 2 and padding 1 (overlapping, smoother, used in some Inception variants), strided convolution (learned pooling) as alternative

Analyze information loss: max pooling discards magnitude information, average pooling dilutes strong signals; attention pooling (weighted average by learned attention) combines benefits and is used in modern architectures (Set Transformer, Perceiver)
expected_outcome: Informed pooling strategy selection with parameter impact analysis and understanding of information preservation tradeoffs.

title: Domain Adaptation and Distribution Shift
description: Domain adaptation addresses the shift between training (source) and deployment (target) distributions. Covariate shift (P(X) changes, P(Y|X) stays) is most common — e.g., training on professional photos, deploying on smartphone images. Methods: adversarial adaptation (DANN: domain classifier with gradient reversal layer, λ from 0→1 during training), maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) minimization in feature space, self-training (pseudo-label confident target predictions and retrain). Unsupervised domain adaptation (no target labels) typically recovers 50-80% of the supervised performance gap.
when_to_use: When deploying models to new domains, when training and test distributions differ, when analyzing model robustness to distribution shift, or when explaining why models fail in production.
steps: Diagnose distribution shift type: covariate shift (input distribution changes), label shift (class proportions change), concept drift (P(Y|X) changes over time) — each requires different adaptation strategy

Apply unsupervised domain adaptation: DANN (add domain discriminator with gradient reversal, shared features become domain-invariant), CORAL (align second-order feature statistics), or self-training (iterative pseudo-labeling with confidence threshold >0.95)

Use test-time adaptation for rapid shifts: Tent (update BN statistics and BN affine parameters on test batches via entropy minimization), TTT (self-supervised auxiliary task head updated at test time)

Evaluate adaptation: measure target domain accuracy, compute dataset distance metrics (A-distance, MMD between source/target features), check for negative transfer (adaptation hurting performance when domains are very dissimilar)
expected_outcome: Appropriate domain adaptation strategy with distribution shift diagnosis and evaluation of adaptation effectiveness.

title: ReAct Loop: Thought-Action-Observation Interleaving
description: ReAct (Yao et al., 2023) interleaves reasoning traces with actions and observations in a Thought→Action→Observation loop. The reasoning trace grounds action selection — experiments on HotpotQA showed ~40% fewer hallucinated tool calls vs direct action generation. The key failure mode is 'thought drift' where the reasoning trace becomes self-referential and stops grounding in observations, typically after 5-7 loop iterations.
when_to_use: When designing or debugging agent loops that combine reasoning with tool use, especially when the agent hallucinates actions or loses track of intermediate results.
steps: Structure each loop iteration as explicit Thought (what I know, what I need), Action (tool call with parameters), Observation (tool output)
Constrain the Thought step to reference the most recent Observation — this prevents thought drift
Set a maximum iteration count (typically 7-10) with an explicit 'give up gracefully' exit path
Parse the Observation to update a running summary of known facts before the next Thought
expected_outcome: An agent loop that maintains grounded reasoning across multiple tool calls with measurably fewer hallucinated actions.

title: Tool Selection via Function Schema Matching
description: Modern tool-use agents select tools from a registry of function schemas (name, description, parameters with types). The LLM matches the current subgoal to the most appropriate schema. Performance degrades sharply beyond ~20 tools in context — at 50+ tools, selection accuracy drops 15-25% (Qin et al., 2023). Hierarchical tool indexing (category → subcategory → tool) recovers most of this loss.
when_to_use: When building or optimizing an agent's tool selection mechanism, especially when the tool count exceeds 15-20 and selection accuracy drops.
steps: Define each tool with a precise name, 1-sentence description, and typed parameter schema (JSON Schema format)
Group tools into 4-7 semantic categories and present category selection as a first-pass filter
Include 2-3 few-shot examples per tool showing correct parameter extraction from natural language
Add negative examples for commonly confused tool pairs (e.g., search vs retrieve, update vs replace)
Monitor tool selection accuracy and add disambiguation prompts for tools with >10% misselection rate
expected_outcome: A tool registry architecture that maintains >90% selection accuracy even with 30+ available tools.

title: Working Memory via Scratchpad Prompting
description: Agent working memory can be implemented as an explicit scratchpad section in the prompt that the LLM reads and rewrites each turn. Unlike chat history (which grows linearly), a scratchpad maintains a fixed-size summary of relevant state. The critical design choice is what to include: typically (1) current goal, (2) completed steps, (3) key facts discovered, (4) next action. Scratchpads that exceed ~500 tokens lose effectiveness as the LLM stops reading them carefully.
when_to_use: When an agent needs to maintain state across multiple turns but chat history grows too large, or when the agent repeatedly 'forgets' earlier discoveries.
steps: Define a structured scratchpad template with labeled sections (Goal, Progress, Facts, Next Step)
After each tool call, prompt the LLM to update the scratchpad by rewriting it (not appending)
Cap scratchpad size at 300-500 tokens — force summarization of Facts when it exceeds the limit
Place the scratchpad immediately before the action prompt so it's in the LLM's recent attention window
expected_outcome: An agent that maintains coherent multi-step state without growing context windows, with the scratchpad serving as compressed working memory.

title: Tree-of-Thought Planning for Complex Decomposition
description: Tree-of-Thought (ToT) extends chain-of-thought by exploring multiple reasoning paths as a tree, using BFS or DFS with evaluation at each node (Yao et al., 2023). Each node represents a partial solution; the LLM generates 2-5 candidate continuations and scores them. ToT excels on tasks with dead-end paths (Game of 24, crosswords) but adds 3-10x token cost. The key parameter is breadth (candidates per node) vs depth (maximum path length) — typically b=3, d=5 works well.
when_to_use: When a task has multiple viable solution paths with dead ends that require backtracking, such as multi-step planning, puzzle solving, or strategy generation.
steps: Decompose the problem into sequential decision points where branching is meaningful
At each node, generate b=3 candidate next steps using temperature 0.7-1.0 for diversity
Evaluate each candidate with a separate LLM call asking 'Is this path promising? Rate 1-5 with justification'
Prune paths scoring below 3/5 and expand the best-scoring node (BFS) or deepest promising node (DFS)
When a path reaches the goal, backtrack to verify no better path exists among unexplored branches
expected_outcome: A structured exploration of solution space that finds higher-quality solutions than single-chain reasoning, at the cost of increased token usage.

title: Chain-of-Thought Failure Modes and Mitigations
description: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting fails in predictable ways: (1) unfaithful reasoning — the CoT says one thing but the answer reflects different logic (~10-30% of cases per Turpin et al., 2023), (2) reasoning chain corruption — an early error propagates through all subsequent steps, (3) verbosity without progress — the model generates plausible-sounding but circular reasoning. Mitigation: verify CoT fidelity by checking if the answer actually follows from the stated reasoning.
when_to_use: When debugging an agent that produces plausible-looking reasoning traces but wrong answers, or when chain-of-thought prompting yields inconsistent quality.
steps: Add a verification step after CoT that checks: 'Does the final answer logically follow from steps 1-N?'
For multi-step arithmetic or logic, extract intermediate results and verify each independently
Detect circular reasoning by checking if step N+1 adds new information beyond step N
If CoT corruption is detected, restart the reasoning chain from the last verified-correct step rather than from scratch
expected_outcome: Identification and mitigation of specific CoT failure modes, improving reasoning reliability from ~70% to ~85% on tasks prone to reasoning chain corruption.

title: System Prompt Engineering for Agent Personas
description: Agent system prompts establish behavioral constraints through role specification, capability boundaries, and response format. Effective system prompts follow a structure: identity → capabilities → constraints → output format → few-shot examples. The critical insight from production deployments is that capability boundaries must be stated as explicit 'do not' rules — vague capability descriptions lead to 30-50% more out-of-scope attempts. System prompt length sweet spot is 500-1500 tokens.
when_to_use: When designing or refining an agent's system prompt, especially when the agent frequently violates behavioral boundaries or produces inconsistent response formats.
steps: Define identity in 1-2 sentences: who the agent is, what its primary function is
List 3-5 explicit capabilities as 'you CAN' statements with specific examples
List 3-5 explicit boundaries as 'you MUST NOT' statements — be specific, not vague
Specify output format with a concrete template or example (JSON schema, markdown structure)
Add 2-3 few-shot examples that demonstrate both correct behavior and boundary enforcement
Test with adversarial prompts that try to violate each stated boundary
expected_outcome: A system prompt that reliably constrains agent behavior within defined boundaries while maintaining natural interaction quality.

title: Observation-Action Loop with Stopping Conditions
description: Agent loops cycle through observe→decide→act until a stopping condition is met. The three standard stopping conditions are: (1) goal achieved (task-specific detector), (2) budget exhausted (token/step/time limit), (3) stuck detection (same state for N iterations). Without explicit stuck detection, agents commonly enter infinite loops — empirically, if an agent takes the same action twice in a row, it will repeat it indefinitely ~80% of the time. The step budget should typically be 2-3x the expected solution length.
when_to_use: When implementing an agent loop's termination logic, or when debugging an agent that runs too long, terminates too early, or gets stuck in repetitive cycles.
steps: Implement three stopping conditions: goal detector (task-specific), step budget (e.g., max 15 actions), and stuck detector (same action repeated or no state change for 3 turns)
Track action history and compare the last 3 actions — if identical, trigger stuck recovery (inject a different prompt or reset context)
Set the step budget to 2-3x the expected number of steps for the task category
When budget is 80% consumed, inject a 'wrap up' prompt telling the agent to produce its best answer with remaining budget
expected_outcome: An agent loop that terminates appropriately under all conditions — success, budget exhaustion, or getting stuck — without wasting tokens on repetitive actions.

title: Reflection and Self-Correction via Critic Prompting
description: Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) adds a self-critique step after each agent action or episode: the LLM reviews its own output, identifies errors, and generates corrective instructions for the next attempt. Key finding: self-correction works only when the model can actually detect its errors — on tasks where error detection accuracy is <60%, reflection degrades performance by adding confident-sounding but wrong corrections. Two-pass reflection (generate, then critique with fresh context) outperforms inline 'wait, let me reconsider' by ~15%.
when_to_use: When building agents that need to improve across attempts, or when an agent produces good first drafts but fails on edge cases it could catch with review.
steps: After the agent's initial output, make a separate LLM call with the prompt: 'Review this output for errors. List specific mistakes with evidence.'
Filter the critique: only accept corrections that cite specific evidence from the output (not vague 'could be better')
Feed accepted corrections back as additional context for a second generation pass
Track correction acceptance rate — if <40% of corrections are actually improvements, disable reflection for that task type to avoid degradation
expected_outcome: A reflection mechanism that improves output quality on tasks where errors are detectable, without degrading performance on tasks where self-critique is unreliable.

title: Code Generation Agent with Sandbox Execution
description: Code generation agents follow a generate→execute→observe→fix loop. The sandbox (Docker container, E2B, or subprocess with resource limits) must enforce: memory limits (512MB typical), time limits (30s per execution), network isolation, and filesystem restrictions. The critical failure mode is 'fix oscillation' — the agent alternates between two broken versions. Solution: maintain a diff history and detect if the current fix reverts a previous fix. Empirically, if code isn't correct after 3 fix attempts, regeneration from scratch outperforms continued fixing ~70% of the time.
when_to_use: When building or debugging a code generation agent that executes code in a sandbox, especially when the agent enters fix loops or produces insecure code.
steps: Configure sandbox with resource limits: 512MB RAM, 30s timeout, no network, restricted filesystem
Capture both stdout and stderr from execution — feed the full error traceback to the fix prompt
Maintain a history of all code versions and their errors — detect if the fix is reverting to a previously failed version
After 3 failed fix attempts, regenerate from scratch with a different approach rather than continuing to patch
Parse test results structurally (pass/fail counts) rather than relying on the LLM to interpret raw output
expected_outcome: A code generation loop that converges to correct solutions within 3-5 iterations and avoids fix oscillation or infinite retry loops.

title: Context Window Management for Conversational Agents
description: Conversational agents face a hard constraint: context window size (4K-128K tokens). Management strategies: (1) sliding window — drop oldest messages (loses early context), (2) summarization — compress older turns into a summary (loses detail), (3) hierarchical — recent turns verbatim + summary of older turns + key facts extracted. The hierarchical approach preserves ~90% of relevant information at 40% of token cost. Critical: summarization must preserve entity references and commitments the agent made, not just topic summaries.
when_to_use: When a conversational agent loses track of earlier context, contradicts itself in long conversations, or hits context window limits.
steps: Implement a three-tier context structure: system prompt (fixed) + conversation summary (compressed) + recent turns (verbatim, last 4-6 turns)
Trigger summarization when total context exceeds 70% of the window — summarize the oldest 50% of verbatim turns
Extract and preserve: (1) entities mentioned, (2) commitments the agent made, (3) user preferences stated, (4) key decisions reached
Use a separate, cheap LLM call for summarization to avoid consuming the main model's budget
Test by asking the agent about information from early in the conversation after summarization occurs
expected_outcome: A conversational agent that maintains coherence across long conversations without contradicting earlier statements or forgetting key information.

title: Goal Decomposition for Autonomous Agents
description: Autonomous agents decompose high-level goals into executable subgoals using hierarchical task networks (HTNs) or LLM-based decomposition. The LLM approach generates subgoals by prompting: 'To achieve X, what are the prerequisite steps?' Key failure mode: over-decomposition — breaking a simple task into 10+ substeps where 3 would suffice, wasting tokens and introducing unnecessary failure points. Rule of thumb: if a subgoal can be completed in a single LLM call or tool use, don't decompose further.
when_to_use: When building agents that handle complex, multi-step goals, or when an agent fails because it tries to do too much in a single step or decomposes into too many micro-steps.
steps: Prompt the LLM to decompose the goal into 3-7 subgoals (enforce this range explicitly in the prompt)
For each subgoal, classify as 'atomic' (single action) or 'compound' (needs further decomposition) — only decompose compound subgoals
Identify dependencies between subgoals and order them topologically — parallelize independent subgoals where possible
After executing each subgoal, verify its completion before starting dependent subgoals
If a subgoal fails, re-plan from the current state rather than restarting the entire goal
expected_outcome: A goal decomposition that balances granularity — fine enough to be executable, coarse enough to avoid unnecessary complexity.

title: Output Filtering and Safety Guardrails
description: Safety guardrails for agents operate at three layers: (1) input validation — reject or sanitize harmful user inputs before they reach the LLM, (2) action validation — check proposed tool calls against an allowlist/denylist before execution, (3) output filtering — scan generated text for PII, harmful content, or policy violations before returning to the user. The most critical layer for tool-using agents is action validation — a hallucinated 'delete all files' tool call must be caught before execution, not after. Human-in-the-loop (HITL) gates should be placed on irreversible actions.
when_to_use: When designing safety mechanisms for an agent that takes real-world actions, handles sensitive data, or interacts with external systems.
steps: Classify all available actions as reversible or irreversible — irreversible actions require HITL confirmation
Implement an action allowlist: before any tool execution, verify (tool_name, parameters) against allowed patterns
Add parameter validation: check that arguments are within expected ranges (no SQL injection in query params, no path traversal in file paths)
Scan outputs for PII patterns (SSN, email, phone) before returning to the user — mask or reject
Log all action proposals (including rejected ones) for post-hoc safety audit
expected_outcome: A layered safety architecture that prevents harmful actions before they execute and sanitizes outputs before they reach users.

title: State Machine Agents for Deterministic Workflows
description: State machine agents define explicit states (e.g., 'gathering_info', 'analyzing', 'responding') with valid transitions between them. The LLM operates within the constraints of the current state — only seeing tools and instructions relevant to that state. This prevents the common failure of LLMs skipping steps or executing them out of order. Key design choice: state transitions can be LLM-decided (flexible but error-prone) or rule-based (rigid but reliable). Hybrid approach: LLM decides within states, rules govern transitions.
when_to_use: When an agent must follow a specific workflow with ordered phases, or when a free-form agent skips important steps or executes them out of order.
steps: Define 3-7 states representing workflow phases, with clear entry/exit criteria for each
For each state, specify: available tools, system prompt additions, and valid next states
Implement rule-based transitions: define conditions (e.g., 'all required fields collected') that trigger state changes
Add a 'stuck in state' detector: if an agent has been in the same state for >N turns, force transition or escalate
Log state transitions for debugging — most workflow bugs manifest as unexpected transition sequences
expected_outcome: An agent that reliably follows multi-step workflows in the correct order while maintaining flexibility within each phase.

title: RAG Architecture: Retrieval-Augmented Generation Pipeline
description: RAG pipelines follow query→retrieve→augment→generate. The retrieval step uses dense embeddings (cosine similarity over vector DB) or sparse methods (BM25) or hybrid. Critical design decisions: chunk size (256-512 tokens optimal for most QA tasks — larger chunks add noise, smaller chunks lose context), top-k retrieval count (k=3-5 balances precision and context budget), and whether to rerank retrieved chunks before augmenting. Without reranking, the top-1 retrieved chunk is only the most relevant ~60% of the time (Nogueira et al.).
when_to_use: When building or optimizing a retrieval-augmented generation system, especially when the agent gives answers not grounded in the retrieved documents.
steps: Chunk documents at 256-512 tokens with 50-100 token overlap between chunks to preserve cross-boundary context
Implement hybrid retrieval: dense (embedding similarity) + sparse (BM25) with reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) to combine rankings
Add a reranking step using a cross-encoder model on the top-20 retrieved chunks, then take top-3-5 for context
Format retrieved chunks with source attribution in the prompt: '[Source: doc_name, chunk_id] content...'
Add a 'groundedness' check: verify that the generated answer cites or paraphrases the retrieved chunks, not hallucinated knowledge
expected_outcome: A RAG pipeline that retrieves relevant context and generates grounded answers with proper source attribution.

title: Fine-Tuning vs Prompting Decision Framework for Agent Capabilities
description: Fine-tuning is warranted when: (1) the task requires consistent output format that prompting can't reliably enforce (>5% format violation rate), (2) the behavior needs to generalize across diverse inputs without few-shot examples consuming context, (3) latency matters and you can't afford long system prompts. Prompting is preferred when: (1) behavior needs frequent iteration (<1 week cycles), (2) training data is scarce (<500 examples), (3) the capability is a composition of things the base model already knows. The crossover point is typically around 1000 labeled examples and stable requirements.
when_to_use: When deciding whether to fine-tune a model or use prompt engineering for a specific agent capability, especially when both approaches are viable.
steps: Measure the current prompting approach's failure rate — if format violations or capability gaps are <5%, prompting is sufficient
Estimate available training data: <500 examples → prompting; 500-5000 → LoRA fine-tuning; >5000 → full fine-tuning viable
Assess iteration speed requirements: if the behavior spec changes weekly, fine-tuning creates unsustainable retraining overhead
For hybrid approaches, fine-tune for format/style compliance while using prompts for task-specific instructions
If fine-tuning, start with LoRA (rank 8-16) on the last few layers — it captures style/format with minimal catastrophic forgetting
expected_outcome: A justified decision on whether to fine-tune or prompt-engineer a given agent capability, with clear criteria for revisiting the decision.

title: Token Budget Management and Cost-Aware Agent Design
description: Agent token costs scale with: (1) system prompt size × number of turns, (2) context carried forward per turn, (3) number of tool call round-trips. A ReAct agent with a 2000-token system prompt averaging 8 turns uses ~16K input tokens per task — at GPT-4 pricing that's ~$0.48/task. Optimizations: compress system prompts (remove redundant examples), use cheaper models for intermediate steps (tool selection, summarization), cache frequent tool outputs. The biggest cost lever is usually reducing unnecessary turns, not prompt compression.
when_to_use: When an agent's per-task cost is too high, when optimizing for production deployment at scale, or when designing cost-aware agent architectures.
steps: Instrument token usage per component: system prompt, user context, tool calls, reasoning traces, output
Identify the largest cost contributor — typically it's either system prompt repetition or excessive tool call round-trips
Route cheap subtasks (classification, extraction) to smaller/faster models (GPT-3.5, Haiku) while keeping complex reasoning on the expensive model
Cache tool outputs that don't change between calls (API schemas, reference data) instead of re-fetching
Set per-task token budgets and implement graceful degradation: if budget is 80% consumed, switch to a simpler strategy
expected_outcome: An agent architecture with understood and controlled token costs, typically achieving 30-50% cost reduction without significant quality loss.

title: Multi-Turn Reasoning Consistency via Commitment Tracking
description: LLM agents frequently contradict themselves across turns: they commit to an approach in turn 3, then propose a conflicting approach in turn 7 without acknowledging the change. This is distinct from legitimate course-correction. Commitment tracking extracts explicit commitments ('I will use approach X', 'The answer is Y because Z') and surfaces them when the agent's current output conflicts. Studies show that simply including prior commitments in the prompt reduces contradictions by ~60% (though it can also reduce beneficial pivots).
when_to_use: When a multi-turn agent contradicts its own earlier statements or decisions, or when consistency across a long interaction is critical (e.g., technical support, tutoring).
steps: After each agent turn, extract statements of commitment: decisions made, facts asserted, approaches chosen
Store commitments in a structured list with the turn number they were made
Before generating each new turn, include the commitment list in the prompt with: 'Previous commitments (contradict only if explicitly stating why):'
When the agent's output conflicts with a commitment, add a post-processing check that asks: 'You previously stated X but now say Y. Which is correct and why?'
Allow legitimate pivots — the goal is awareness of contradictions, not preventing all changes
expected_outcome: A multi-turn agent that maintains consistency or explicitly acknowledges when and why it changes its position.

title: Error Handling and Graceful Degradation in Agent Systems
description: Agent errors fall into categories: (1) tool failures (API timeout, rate limit, malformed response), (2) LLM failures (refusal, format violation, hallucination), (3) logic failures (wrong tool chosen, incorrect parameter). Each requires different recovery strategies. The most dangerous pattern is silent failure — the agent proceeds as if the tool call succeeded when it actually returned an error. Production agents need explicit error classification and per-category recovery: retry for transient errors, fallback for permanent errors, escalate for unknown errors.
when_to_use: When building production agent systems that must handle failures gracefully, or when debugging an agent that produces wrong results due to unhandled errors.
steps: Classify every tool response as success, transient_error (retry), permanent_error (fallback), or unknown_error (escalate)
For transient errors: retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) — log but don't surface to user
For permanent errors: switch to a fallback strategy (alternative tool, cached data, or honest 'I cannot do this')
Wrap all tool calls in try/catch that prevents the agent from proceeding without valid output
Add a 'confidence degradation' signal: if 2+ tool calls fail in a task, append to the prompt 'Note: some information sources were unavailable — reduce confidence in answer'
expected_outcome: An agent that handles all failure modes explicitly, never silently proceeds with bad data, and degrades gracefully when components fail.

title: Few-Shot Prompting Mechanics for Agent Tool Use
description: Few-shot examples for tool-using agents must demonstrate the full thought→action→observation→response cycle, not just input→output pairs. The examples must cover: (1) normal case (tool returns expected data), (2) empty result case (tool returns nothing — agent should not hallucinate), (3) error case (tool fails — agent should recover). The order of examples matters: placing the most similar example last (closest to the actual query) improves accuracy by ~10-15%. Three examples is the sweet spot — fewer is unreliable, more wastes context tokens without proportional gains.
when_to_use: When crafting few-shot examples for an agent that uses tools, especially when the agent misuses tools, hallucinate results, or fails to handle edge cases.
steps: Create 3 few-shot examples covering: (1) successful tool use, (2) tool returns empty/no results, (3) tool returns an error
Each example must show the complete cycle: user query → reasoning → tool call → tool output → final response
Order examples from least to most similar to the expected queries — recency bias means the last example has the strongest influence
Include explicit handling in the empty-result example: the agent says 'I couldn't find X' rather than making up an answer
Test with and without each example to verify each one contributes — remove examples that don't improve accuracy
expected_outcome: A few-shot prompt that reliably guides agent tool use across normal, empty, and error cases with minimal context token overhead.

title: Plan-and-Solve: Upfront Planning Before Execution
description: Plan-and-Solve (Wang et al., 2023) separates planning from execution: the LLM first generates an explicit plan (numbered steps), then executes each step sequentially. This outperforms zero-shot CoT by ~5-10% on math and multi-step reasoning tasks because it forces the model to consider the full problem structure before acting. The failure mode is plan rigidity — the agent follows a flawed plan rather than adapting. Solution: after each step, ask 'Is the plan still valid given what we learned?' and allow re-planning.
when_to_use: When an agent tackles multi-step problems where the sequence of steps matters, or when zero-shot chain-of-thought produces disorganized reasoning.
steps: Prompt the LLM: 'Before solving, create a step-by-step plan. Number each step. Do not execute yet.'
Validate the plan: check for completeness (does it cover the full problem?), ordering (are dependencies respected?), and granularity (3-7 steps)
Execute each step sequentially, prefixing each with 'Executing Step N of the plan:'
After each step, evaluate: 'Given the result of Step N, is the remaining plan still valid?' Allow re-planning if new information invalidates later steps
After final step, verify the output against the original plan to ensure all planned deliverables are present
expected_outcome: Structured, complete solutions to multi-step problems with explicit plans that can adapt to new information during execution.

title: Parameter Extraction from Natural Language for Tool Calls
description: Extracting structured tool parameters from user queries is the most failure-prone step in tool-use agents. Common errors: (1) type coercion failures (string '3' vs integer 3), (2) missing required parameters not detected, (3) ambiguous parameter mapping ('delete the second one' — which parameter is 'the second one'?). JSON mode / structured output helps with format but not semantic accuracy. The most robust approach is a two-step process: first extract parameters as a flat key-value list, then validate against the schema before calling.
when_to_use: When an agent produces malformed tool calls, misses required parameters, or maps user intent to the wrong parameter fields.
steps: Define tool parameters with strict JSON Schema including types, required fields, enums, and descriptions
Prompt the LLM to extract parameters as a flat JSON object — use constrained decoding or JSON mode to enforce format
Validate the extracted JSON against the schema: check required fields, type correctness, enum values, range constraints
For missing required parameters, prompt the user for clarification rather than guessing or using defaults
For ambiguous references ('the second one'), maintain a reference resolution context from recent conversation turns
expected_outcome: Reliable parameter extraction from natural language with <5% malformed tool calls and explicit handling of missing or ambiguous parameters.

title: Long-Term Memory via Retrieval from Episodic Store
description: Long-term memory for agents stores past interactions as episodic records (timestamped summaries of past conversations or task completions) in a vector database. At query time, the agent retrieves relevant episodes via embedding similarity. Key design decisions: what to store (full transcript vs summary vs key facts), granularity (per-turn vs per-conversation), and staleness handling (down-weight old episodes via time decay, typically exponential with half-life of 7-30 days). Without time decay, agents retrieve obsolete information with high confidence.
when_to_use: When building agents that need to remember past interactions or learn from previous task completions over days/weeks.
steps: After each conversation, extract and store: (1) a 2-3 sentence summary, (2) key entities and facts, (3) user preferences expressed, (4) timestamp
Embed the summary using the same embedding model used for query-time retrieval to ensure alignment
At query time, retrieve top-5 episodes by embedding similarity, then apply time decay: score × exp(-λ × days_elapsed) with λ tuned to the use case
Format retrieved episodes in the prompt as 'Past context (may be outdated):' to signal the LLM to use them cautiously
Implement a forgetting mechanism: archive episodes older than 90 days to cold storage unless they have high retrieval frequency
expected_outcome: An agent with persistent memory across sessions that appropriately weights recent vs older memories and avoids acting on stale information.

title: Graph-of-Thought for Non-Linear Reasoning Structures
description: Graph-of-Thought (GoT) extends Tree-of-Thought by allowing nodes to merge — multiple reasoning paths can combine their conclusions into a single refined answer (Besta et al., 2023). This is particularly powerful for tasks that benefit from aggregation: generating text that satisfies multiple constraints, combining evidence from multiple sources, or synthesizing conflicting viewpoints. The graph is typically DAG-structured (no cycles). Implementation uses a controller that tracks which nodes can be merged based on semantic compatibility.
when_to_use: When a task requires combining partial conclusions from multiple reasoning paths, such as multi-constraint satisfaction or multi-perspective synthesis.
steps: Generate 3-5 initial reasoning paths (branches) each addressing the problem from a different angle or constraint
For each branch, generate a partial conclusion capturing the key insight from that reasoning path
Identify pairs of partial conclusions that are compatible (not contradictory) and can be merged
Merge compatible branches: prompt the LLM with both partial conclusions and ask for a synthesis that preserves insights from both
If contradictions exist between branches, explicitly note them in the final output rather than silently dropping one
expected_outcome: A synthesized answer that incorporates insights from multiple reasoning perspectives, handling contradictions explicitly rather than ignoring them.

title: Role-Playing Prompts for Specialized Agent Behavior
description: Role-playing ('You are an expert X with Y years of experience') activates domain-specific knowledge in LLMs. Research shows that domain-specific roles improve accuracy by 5-15% on in-domain tasks (Xu et al., 2023) but can degrade out-of-domain performance by encouraging confident confabulation. The role must match the task — assigning a 'Python expert' role for a JavaScript task is worse than no role. Layered roles ('You are a security expert reviewing code as a code reviewer') combine perspectives but increase prompt complexity and can create conflicting behavioral incentives.
when_to_use: When designing agent personas for domain-specific tasks, or when a general-purpose agent underperforms on specialized tasks that a domain expert would handle better.
steps: Match the role to the task domain precisely — use specific titles ('senior database architect') not vague ones ('smart assistant')
Include 2-3 behavioral traits that an expert in this domain would exhibit (e.g., 'always considers edge cases', 'cites specific RFC numbers')
Add a scope limiter: 'If a question falls outside your expertise in X, say so rather than guessing'
Test the role on both in-domain tasks (should improve) and adjacent out-of-domain tasks (should not degrade by more than 5%)
For multi-skill agents, dynamically assign roles based on the detected task type rather than using a fixed role
expected_outcome: An agent with a well-matched domain persona that improves in-domain accuracy without creating confident confabulation on out-of-domain tasks.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Gating for High-Stakes Agent Actions
description: HITL gating places approval checkpoints before agent actions that are irreversible, high-cost, or high-risk. The challenge is calibrating the gate: too many approvals cause 'alert fatigue' (users auto-approve everything), too few let dangerous actions through. Effective gating categorizes actions into three tiers: (1) auto-approve (read-only, reversible), (2) notify-and-proceed (low-risk writes, 10s timeout), (3) require-explicit-approval (deletes, financial transactions, external communications). The tier assignment should be based on the action's blast radius, not just its verb.
when_to_use: When designing approval workflows for agents that take real-world actions, especially when balancing safety against interaction latency and user experience.
steps: Inventory all agent actions and classify by blast radius: read-only (tier 1), reversible-write (tier 2), irreversible or high-cost (tier 3)
Tier 1: execute immediately, log for audit. Tier 2: notify user with 10-30s auto-approve timeout. Tier 3: block until explicit 'yes' from user
Present tier 3 approval requests with full context: what action, what parameters, what it will affect, and what could go wrong
Track approval rates: if a tier 3 action is approved >95% of the time, consider downgrading to tier 2 to reduce friction
Implement an 'emergency stop' that halts all pending agent actions — this must be always accessible regardless of agent state
expected_outcome: An approval workflow that protects against dangerous agent actions while minimizing unnecessary friction on safe operations.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Agent Memory with Importance Scoring
description: Not all memories are equally worth storing or retrieving. Importance scoring assigns a weight to each memory based on: (1) information density — does this memory contain novel facts?, (2) emotional/stake salience — does this involve decisions with consequences?, (3) reference frequency — is this memory retrieved often? Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) used LLM-based importance scoring (1-10 scale) and found that importance-weighted retrieval produced 40% more human-rated 'believable' agent behavior than recency-only retrieval.
when_to_use: When an agent's memory retrieval returns too many low-value memories, or when important past events are drowned out by trivial recent interactions.
steps: After each interaction, score the memory's importance on 1-10 using an LLM prompt: 'On a scale of 1-10, how important is this memory for future interactions? 1=mundane, 10=life-changing'
Combine retrieval scoring: final_score = α×relevance + β×recency + γ×importance (typical weights: α=0.5, β=0.3, γ=0.2)
Calibrate importance scores by including 3 reference examples in the scoring prompt spanning the full range (1, 5, 10)
Periodically re-score old memories as importance can change with new context (e.g., a casual mention of a tool becomes important when the user starts using that tool daily)
expected_outcome: A memory retrieval system that surfaces the most relevant AND important memories, not just the most recent or textually similar ones.

title: Structured Output Enforcement via Constrained Decoding
description: Constrained decoding forces LLM outputs to conform to a grammar or schema (JSON Schema, regex, CFG) by masking invalid tokens at each generation step. Libraries: Outlines (Python), LMQL, Guidance, or API-native JSON mode. Key trade-off: strict constrained decoding guarantees format but can degrade content quality by 5-10% because it sometimes forces the model into low-probability continuations to satisfy the schema. Mitigation: use constrained decoding only for the structured shell (keys, types) while allowing free generation for value content.
when_to_use: When an agent must produce outputs in a strict format (JSON, XML, function calls) and prompt-based format instructions fail >5% of the time.
steps: Define the required output schema in JSON Schema format with strict types, required fields, and enums
Choose the enforcement method: API JSON mode (simplest), Outlines/LMQL (flexible, supports regex/CFG), or post-hoc parsing with retry
If using constrained decoding, test content quality vs unconstrained generation — if quality drops >10%, switch to post-hoc parsing with retry
For nested structures, constrain the outer structure but allow free text in leaf string fields to preserve generation quality
Implement a fallback: if constrained decoding fails (timeout, invalid state), retry with unconstrained generation + post-hoc validation
expected_outcome: Agent outputs that reliably conform to the required schema while maintaining content quality, with a fallback for edge cases.

title: Agentic RAG: Iterative Retrieval with Query Reformulation
description: Standard RAG retrieves once and generates. Agentic RAG adds a feedback loop: if the initial retrieval doesn't contain sufficient information, the agent reformulates the query and retrieves again. This improves recall by 20-30% on complex questions that require multiple pieces of information. The agent decides to re-retrieve based on a self-assessment: 'Do I have enough information to answer completely?' Key failure mode: infinite retrieval loops when no relevant documents exist. Solution: cap retrieval rounds at 3 and force an answer (with uncertainty acknowledgment) after the cap.
when_to_use: When a single retrieval pass misses relevant information for complex queries, or when the agent needs to combine information from multiple documents that wouldn't be retrieved by a single query.
steps: After initial retrieval and a draft answer, prompt the LLM: 'Is this answer complete? What additional information would improve it? Generate a refined search query.'
If the LLM identifies gaps, reformulate the query to target the missing information (not just rephrase the original query)
Retrieve with the reformulated query and merge new chunks with previous chunks, deduplicating by chunk ID
Cap retrieval rounds at 3 — after 3 rounds, force answer generation with: 'Answer with available information, noting what could not be found'
Track retrieval round count per query type to identify topics where the knowledge base has systematic gaps
expected_outcome: More complete answers for complex queries through iterative retrieval, with explicit acknowledgment when information gaps remain.

title: Agent Budget Management: Step, Token, and Time Limits
description: Production agents need three budget dimensions: step budget (max actions), token budget (max total tokens across all LLM calls), and time budget (wall-clock timeout). These interact: a complex reasoning step might consume 2000 tokens (hitting token budget) while a simple tool call consumes 100 tokens but 5 seconds of API latency (hitting time budget). The budget manager should track all three and trigger the most restrictive limit. Budget allocation strategy: reserve 20% for the final answer generation — agents that exhaust budget during reasoning often produce truncated or no answers.
when_to_use: When building production agents that must operate within cost and latency constraints, or when debugging agents that timeout or produce unexpectedly large bills.
steps: Define three budgets per task: max_steps (e.g., 15), max_tokens (e.g., 50K total), max_time (e.g., 60s)
After each action, check all three budgets — trigger wrap-up when ANY budget hits 80%
Reserve 20% of token budget for final answer generation — this means the reasoning phase gets only 80% of tokens
When budget is nearly exhausted, inject a system message: 'You have N steps remaining. Produce your best answer now.'
Log budget utilization per task type to calibrate future budget allocations — some task types consistently need 2x the default
expected_outcome: An agent that operates within defined resource constraints, producing complete answers even when budgets are tight.

title: Self-Ask: Recursive Question Decomposition for Complex Queries
description: Self-Ask (Press et al., 2022) handles compositional questions by having the LLM generate and answer sub-questions. For 'What is the population of the capital of France?', the agent generates: 'What is the capital of France? → Paris. What is the population of Paris? → 2.1M.' This outperforms direct CoT on compositional questions by 15-20% because it forces explicit intermediate answers that can be independently verified. The recursion depth rarely needs to exceed 3 levels. At each level, the agent decides: 'Can I answer this directly, or do I need to decompose further?'
when_to_use: When an agent struggles with multi-hop or compositional questions that require answering intermediate sub-questions before the final answer.
steps: When receiving a complex question, prompt the LLM: 'Can you answer this directly? If not, what sub-question do you need to answer first?'
If a sub-question is generated, recursively apply the same process (max depth 3)
At each level, verify the sub-question answer before proceeding: 'Is [sub-answer] consistent with known facts?'
Compose the final answer by chaining sub-answers: 'Since [sub-answer-1], and [sub-answer-2], therefore [final-answer]'
If tool use is available, route sub-questions to appropriate tools (search, calculator, database) for factual verification
expected_outcome: Correct answers to compositional questions through explicit sub-question decomposition with verifiable intermediate steps.

title: Prompt Chaining: Sequential LLM Calls with Data Flow
description: Prompt chaining decomposes a complex task into a sequence of simpler LLM calls where the output of one becomes the input of the next. Unlike agent loops (which decide dynamically what to do next), chains are predetermined pipelines. Example: extract→classify→generate→format. The advantage is debuggability — each step can be independently tested and optimized. The failure mode is error propagation: if step 2 of 5 produces a wrong intermediate result, steps 3-5 amplify the error. Mitigation: add validation gates between steps that catch and correct errors before propagation.
when_to_use: When a task has a clear sequence of independent subtasks that can each be handled by a focused LLM call, and when debuggability is more important than flexibility.
steps: Decompose the task into 3-6 sequential steps, each with a single clear input and output
For each step, write a focused prompt that only addresses that step's subtask — don't include context from other steps
Define the data contract between steps: what format step N outputs and step N+1 expects
Add a validation gate between steps: check that the output conforms to the expected format and is semantically reasonable
If a validation gate fails, retry the failing step (up to 2 times) before failing the entire chain
expected_outcome: A modular, debuggable pipeline of LLM calls that can be independently tested and optimized at each step.

title: Conversation Summarization for Context Compression
description: When conversation history exceeds context limits, progressive summarization compresses older turns while preserving critical information. The hierarchical approach works best: (1) verbatim for last 3-4 turns, (2) detailed summary for turns 5-15, (3) key points only for turns 16+. Critical information that must survive summarization: user goals and constraints, decisions already made, entities and their relationships, any numbers or specific data points. The most common summarization failure is losing negations — 'the user said they do NOT want X' gets summarized as 'discussed X' which reverses the intent.
when_to_use: When implementing context compression for long conversations, or when a conversational agent starts contradicting or forgetting earlier parts of the discussion.
steps: Trigger summarization when context reaches 70% of window size — don't wait until it's full
Summarize the oldest 50% of verbatim history using a dedicated summarization prompt that emphasizes: preserve negations, commitments, numbers, and entity relationships
Structure the summary with explicit sections: [User Goals], [Decisions Made], [Key Facts], [Open Questions]
After summarization, verify by prompting: 'Based on this summary, what does the user want?' — compare against the original conversation
Keep a separate 'critical facts' buffer (max 200 tokens) that is never summarized — add items manually or via extraction
expected_outcome: Effective context compression that preserves conversation coherence and critical details while reducing token usage by 40-60%.

title: Action Validation via Pre-Execution Simulation
description: Before executing a potentially harmful action, the agent can simulate its effects: 'If I execute [action] with [params], what will happen?' This is implemented as a separate LLM call that predicts consequences without actually executing. The simulation prompt includes: current state, proposed action, and asks for (1) expected outcome, (2) potential side effects, (3) reversibility assessment. This catches ~60% of harmful actions that input validation alone misses, particularly parameter value errors (e.g., deleting the wrong file, sending a message to the wrong person).
when_to_use: When an agent takes actions with real-world consequences that are difficult to reverse, particularly when parameter errors could cause significant harm.
steps: Before executing tier-2 or tier-3 actions, make a simulation call: 'Predict the outcome of [action] with parameters [params] given current state [state]'
The simulation should output: expected_result, side_effects (list), is_reversible (bool), risk_level (low/medium/high)
If simulation predicts high risk or irreversible side effects, escalate to HITL approval before executing
Compare post-execution actual results with simulation predictions — divergence indicates either simulation inaccuracy or unexpected system behavior
Use simulation prediction accuracy to calibrate trust: if simulation is wrong >30% of the time for an action type, skip simulation (it adds latency without value)
expected_outcome: A pre-execution safety check that catches parameter errors and unintended side effects before they cause harm.

title: Dynamic Tool Registration and Discovery
description: Static tool lists don't scale — as agents gain more capabilities, the tool list grows and selection accuracy drops. Dynamic registration allows tools to be loaded on-demand based on the detected task type. Implementation: maintain a tool index (short descriptions only, ~20 tokens each) that the LLM scans to select 3-5 relevant tools, then load full schemas for only those tools. This keeps the active tool context under 1000 tokens even with 100+ available tools. The index must be updated when tools are added/removed — stale indices cause tool discovery failures.
when_to_use: When an agent has more than 20 tools and selection accuracy is degrading, or when building a plugin/extension system where tools are added dynamically.
steps: Create a tool index with one-line descriptions: 'tool_name: what it does in <20 tokens'
At task start, present the index and prompt: 'Which 3-5 tools are most relevant for this task? List their names.'
Load full schemas (descriptions, parameters, examples) only for selected tools into the active context
If the agent can't complete the task with selected tools, allow a 're-browse' of the index to select additional tools
Version the tool index and validate it against the actual tool registry on startup — flag mismatches
expected_outcome: An agent that maintains high tool selection accuracy with large tool registries by loading only relevant tool schemas into context.

title: Confidence Calibration and Abstention in Agent Responses
description: Well-calibrated agents know when they don't know. Confidence calibration techniques: (1) verbalized confidence — ask the LLM to state its confidence (poorly calibrated, tends to be overconfident), (2) consistency-based — generate N responses at temperature 0.7 and measure agreement (well-calibrated but expensive, N=5 is minimum), (3) retrieval grounding — confidence = fraction of claims supported by retrieved documents. Agents should abstain (say 'I'm not sure') when confidence is below a task-specific threshold rather than producing a plausible-sounding wrong answer. Setting the threshold requires a calibration dataset with known ground truth.
when_to_use: When an agent needs to communicate uncertainty rather than always providing a definitive answer, especially in high-stakes domains where wrong confident answers are worse than honest uncertainty.
steps: Implement consistency-based confidence: generate 5 responses at temperature 0.7, measure semantic agreement using embedding similarity (threshold: >0.85 = high agreement)
Define an abstention threshold per task type: if agreement is below threshold, respond with 'I'm not confident in this answer because [reason]'
For RAG-backed agents, compute grounding score: what fraction of claims in the answer are directly supported by retrieved passages?
Calibrate thresholds using a held-out set of 50-100 tasks with known answers — adjust until false confidence rate is <10%
Present calibrated confidence to users as categories (high/medium/low) not numbers — users misinterpret '72% confident' but understand 'I'm moderately confident'
expected_outcome: An agent that appropriately abstains on questions it can't reliably answer, with calibrated confidence that users can trust for decision-making.

title: BM25 Scoring Internals and Parameter Tuning
description: BM25 uses term frequency saturation: score(t,D) = IDF(t) · (tf(t,D) · (k1+1)) / (tf(t,D) + k1 · (1 - b + b · |D|/avgdl)). The k1 parameter (typically 1.2-2.0) controls TF saturation — higher values let repeated terms matter more. The b parameter (typically 0.75) controls document length normalization — b=0 ignores length, b=1 fully normalizes. IDF is computed as log((N - df(t) + 0.5) / (df(t) + 0.5) + 1) in the BM25L variant to avoid negative IDF for very common terms.
when_to_use: When implementing or tuning keyword search, comparing BM25 variants (BM25, BM25+, BM25L), or debugging why short/long documents are over/under-ranked.
steps: Compute IDF for each query term using the corpus-level document frequency — watch for negative IDF with high-df terms
Apply term frequency saturation with k1: plot the TF curve to verify diminishing returns behavior (tf=1 should score ~50% of tf=10 with k1=1.2)
Tune b by testing on queries where relevant documents vary in length — if long docs are always preferred, increase b; if short docs are buried, decrease b
Validate scoring by manually computing BM25 for a few query-document pairs and comparing against the engine output
expected_outcome: Correctly configured BM25 with parameter values justified by the corpus characteristics, producing relevance scores that handle document length variation appropriately.

title: Fixed-Size vs Semantic Chunking Strategies
description: Fixed-size chunking splits documents at token/character boundaries (e.g., 512 tokens with 50-token overlap), which is fast but breaks mid-sentence. Semantic chunking detects topic boundaries using embedding similarity between consecutive sentences — when cosine similarity drops below a threshold (typically 0.3-0.5), a new chunk begins. Recursive chunking splits by hierarchy: first by headings, then paragraphs, then sentences, only splitting smaller units when chunks exceed the size limit.
when_to_use: When designing a document ingestion pipeline and choosing how to split documents for embedding, or when retrieval quality is poor because chunks contain incomplete or mixed-topic content.
steps: Profile your corpus: measure average document length, presence of headings/sections, and topic density per page
For structured documents (papers, manuals), use recursive chunking respecting headers > paragraphs > sentences hierarchy
For unstructured text (chat logs, transcripts), use semantic chunking with sentence-level embedding similarity and a breakpoint percentile (e.g., split at the 10th percentile of similarity scores)
Set overlap to 10-15% of chunk size to preserve cross-boundary context
Validate by sampling 50 chunks and checking: does each chunk contain a single coherent topic? Are important passages split across chunks?
expected_outcome: Chunks that are topically coherent, appropriately sized for the embedding model's context window, and preserve enough overlap for cross-boundary queries.

title: Dense Embedding Model Selection and Dimensionality Tradeoffs
description: Dense embedding models map text to fixed-dimensional vectors where semantic similarity correlates with cosine distance. Key tradeoffs: higher dimensions (e.g., 1536 for text-embedding-3-large vs 384 for MiniLM) capture more nuance but increase storage and latency linearly. Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL) trains embeddings where the first d dimensions are a valid d-dimensional embedding, allowing dimension truncation at query time. Normalized embeddings (unit vectors) make cosine similarity equivalent to dot product, enabling faster computation.
when_to_use: When selecting an embedding model for a retrieval system, optimizing vector storage costs, or deciding between accuracy and latency tradeoffs in production.
steps: Benchmark candidate models on your domain using MTEB or a custom evaluation set — general leaderboard rankings often don't transfer to specialized domains
Test dimension reduction: embed your eval set at full dimension and at 256/512 truncations, measure recall@10 degradation
Calculate storage requirements: num_documents × dimensions × 4 bytes (float32) or × 2 bytes (float16) for the vector index
Measure p99 latency for your target QPS — models with >768 dimensions may need quantization or dimension reduction to meet latency SLAs
Verify the model handles your max document length — most models truncate at 512 tokens, some (e5-mistral, nomic-embed) support 8192+
expected_outcome: A justified embedding model choice with measured recall, storage, and latency characteristics for the target domain and scale.

title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion for Hybrid Search
description: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines ranked lists from multiple retrieval methods without requiring score normalization: RRF_score(d) = Σ 1/(k + rank_i(d)) where k is a constant (typically 60) that dampens the influence of high-rank positions. This outperforms simple score interpolation because BM25 scores and cosine similarities have incomparable distributions. Alternative: Convex Combination (CC) normalizes scores to [0,1] then combines as α·sparse + (1-α)·dense, but requires score distribution calibration per query.
when_to_use: When combining keyword (BM25/TF-IDF) and semantic (dense embedding) search results into a single ranked list, or when fusing results from multiple embedding models.
steps: Retrieve top-K results from each system independently (e.g., top-100 from BM25 and top-100 from dense)
Assign ranks within each list (1-indexed), marking documents absent from a list as rank=infinity (excluded from that list's contribution)
Compute RRF score for each unique document across all lists using k=60 as the default smoothing constant
Sort by RRF score descending and take the final top-N results
Tune k on a validation set: lower k (10-30) gives more weight to top-ranked items, higher k (60-100) is more democratic across positions
expected_outcome: A fused ranked list that captures both lexical and semantic relevance, typically outperforming either individual retrieval method by 5-15% on recall metrics.

title: HNSW Index Construction and Search Parameters
description: Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graphs build a multi-layer graph for approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search. Construction parameters: M (edges per node, typically 16-64) controls graph connectivity — higher M means better recall but more memory (each vector stores ~M×4 bytes of edge data). ef_construction (typically 100-500) controls build-time search width. Search parameter ef (typically 50-400) controls query-time accuracy/speed tradeoff. Memory footprint: vectors + M×8 bytes per vector for bidirectional edges. Recall@10 >0.99 is achievable with ef=200, M=32 on most datasets.
when_to_use: When configuring a vector database index (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, pgvector), diagnosing slow ANN queries, or tuning the recall-latency tradeoff in production.
steps: Set M based on dataset size: M=16 for <1M vectors, M=32 for 1-10M, M=48 for >10M — higher M improves recall but increases memory by ~M×8 bytes per vector
Set ef_construction to at least 2×M (e.g., 128 for M=64) — this is a one-time build cost, so err on the higher side
Profile recall@10 vs latency by sweeping ef from 50 to 400 on a held-out query set with known ground truth neighbors
Monitor memory usage: total = N × (dim × 4 + M × 8 + overhead) bytes — for 1M 768-dim vectors with M=32, expect ~3.3GB
If memory is constrained, consider IVF+PQ instead of HNSW — PQ compresses vectors to 8-64 bytes at some recall cost
expected_outcome: An HNSW index configuration that meets the target recall (e.g., >0.95) within latency and memory constraints, with a documented recall-latency curve.

title: Cross-Encoder Reranking Pipeline
description: Cross-encoders jointly encode the query-document pair through a transformer, producing a relevance score via cross-attention between query and document tokens. This is far more accurate than bi-encoder similarity (typically +10-20% NDCG) but O(N) per query since each candidate must be scored individually. Pipeline pattern: retrieve top-100 with a fast bi-encoder, then rerank top-20-50 with a cross-encoder. Models like ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 score ~2ms/pair on GPU; larger models like deberta-v3-large score ~15ms/pair but are more accurate.
when_to_use: When initial retrieval recall is adequate but precision is poor (relevant documents exist in top-100 but not top-10), or when the quality gap between bi-encoder and human judgment is unacceptable.
steps: Profile the bi-encoder's recall@100 — reranking can only promote documents that were retrieved, so if recall@100 is low, fix retrieval first
Select a cross-encoder model: ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 for speed (<3ms/pair), or cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-12-v2 for accuracy
Score each (query, document) pair independently — cross-encoders take concatenated input [CLS] query [SEP] document [SEP] and output a single logit
Re-sort candidates by cross-encoder score and take top-K for the final result
Set the rerank window (how many candidates to score) based on latency budget: window × per-pair-latency must be < acceptable total latency
expected_outcome: A two-stage retrieval pipeline where cheap first-stage recall feeds expensive but accurate cross-encoder reranking, improving NDCG@10 by 10-20% within latency constraints.

title: HyDE: Hypothetical Document Embeddings for Query Expansion
description: Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE) generates a hypothetical answer to the query using an LLM, then embeds that answer instead of the query for retrieval. The intuition: a generated answer is closer in embedding space to relevant documents than the short query is. HyDE is particularly effective for questions where the query terms don't overlap with document vocabulary (vocabulary mismatch). Limitation: if the LLM hallucinates an incorrect answer, the embedding will retrieve documents about the wrong topic.
when_to_use: When queries are short or use different vocabulary than the document corpus, when zero-shot retrieval has low recall, or when users phrase questions in natural language but documents use technical terminology.
steps: Prompt an LLM to generate a hypothetical answer to the user's query (e.g., 'Write a short paragraph answering this question: {query}')
Embed the hypothetical answer using the same embedding model used for the document index
Retrieve documents using the hypothetical answer embedding instead of (or in addition to) the query embedding
Optionally combine HyDE results with standard query results using RRF to mitigate hallucination risk
Evaluate on queries where standard retrieval fails — HyDE typically helps most on 'vocabulary gap' queries and hurts on precise keyword queries
expected_outcome: Improved recall for natural-language queries by bridging the vocabulary gap between user questions and technical documents, with awareness of hallucination risks.

title: NDCG, MRR, and MAP Evaluation Metrics for Ranked Retrieval
description: NDCG@K (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) measures ranked quality with graded relevance: DCG@K = Σ(i=1..K) (2^rel_i - 1) / log2(i+1), normalized by the ideal DCG. MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) = mean(1/rank of first relevant result) — good for navigational queries. MAP (Mean Average Precision) = mean of AP per query, where AP = Σ(k=1..N) P@k · rel(k) / total_relevant — captures both precision and recall. Key distinction: NDCG handles graded relevance (0-3 scale), while MAP assumes binary relevance.
when_to_use: When evaluating retrieval system quality, comparing two retrieval configurations, or setting up offline evaluation pipelines for search systems.
steps: Choose the metric based on your relevance model: NDCG for graded relevance (e.g., 0=irrelevant, 1=partial, 2=relevant, 3=perfect), MAP for binary relevant/irrelevant
For each query in the eval set, compute the metric at the target cutoff (typically @5, @10, @20) against ground-truth relevance judgments
Average across all queries — report mean and standard deviation to capture per-query variance
Use MRR as a supplementary metric for known-item search where users want the single best result
Perform statistical significance testing (paired t-test or bootstrap) when comparing two systems — a 1-2% NDCG difference may not be significant
expected_outcome: Rigorous evaluation of retrieval quality with appropriate metrics, significance testing, and understanding of what each metric captures about system behavior.

title: Sliding Window Chunking with Overlap Calibration
description: Sliding window chunking moves a fixed-size window across the document with a configurable stride (stride = chunk_size - overlap). Overlap ensures that passages near chunk boundaries appear in at least two chunks, preventing retrieval failures when relevant text spans a boundary. Optimal overlap depends on average query length — if queries match 2-3 sentences, overlap should be at least 2-3 sentences. Excessive overlap (>25%) wastes storage and increases false-positive retrieval. The deduplication problem: overlapping chunks can return near-identical results, requiring post-retrieval deduplication by source position.
when_to_use: When documents are long and unstructured (e.g., transcripts, legal documents, novels) without clear section boundaries, or when chunk-boundary retrieval failures are observed.
steps: Set chunk size to match the embedding model's sweet spot — typically 256-512 tokens for most bi-encoders (longer is not always better due to information dilution)
Set overlap to 10-20% of chunk size as a starting point (e.g., 50-token overlap for 512-token chunks)
Test boundary queries: manually create queries whose answers span two adjacent chunks and verify at least one chunk retrieves it
Implement post-retrieval deduplication: if two retrieved chunks overlap by >50% in source position, keep only the higher-scored one
Calculate storage overhead: num_chunks ≈ doc_length / stride, so 20% overlap creates ~25% more chunks than zero overlap
expected_outcome: A chunking configuration that eliminates boundary-crossing retrieval failures while controlling storage overhead and duplicate results.

title: Sparse Retrieval with Learned Sparse Representations (SPLADE)
description: SPLADE (SParse Lexical AnD Expansion) learns sparse representations where each dimension corresponds to a vocabulary term. Unlike BM25 which uses raw term frequencies, SPLADE uses a transformer to predict term importance weights, including weights for terms NOT in the document (expansion). The output is a sparse vector over the full vocabulary (~30K dimensions) with typically 100-300 non-zero entries. This enables inverted index efficiency with learned semantic matching. SPLADE v2 adds regularization (FLOPS loss) to control sparsity: L = L_ranking + λ · FLOPS(sparse_repr).
when_to_use: When you need semantic matching with inverted index efficiency, when dense retrieval is too expensive at scale, or when you want the interpretability of term-based retrieval with learned expansion.
steps: Understand the architecture: BERT encoder → MLM head → log(1 + ReLU(logits)) per token → max-pool across tokens to get document-level term weights
Index the sparse representations in an inverted index (e.g., Lucene, Anserini) — each term's posting list contains (doc_id, weight) pairs
At query time, encode the query with the same model to get query term weights, then score via dot product of sparse vectors
Tune the FLOPS regularization coefficient λ: higher λ produces sparser representations (faster search, lower recall), lower λ produces denser ones
Compare against BM25 and dense retrieval on your benchmark — SPLADE typically matches dense retrieval quality while being 5-10x faster on CPU
expected_outcome: A learned sparse retrieval system that combines semantic understanding with inverted-index efficiency, with quality comparable to dense retrieval at lower latency.

title: ColBERT Late Interaction for Efficient Reranking
description: ColBERT computes token-level embeddings independently for queries and documents, then scores via late interaction: MaxSim computes max cosine similarity between each query token and all document tokens, then sums across query tokens. This gives cross-encoder-like quality (captures token-level interactions) with bi-encoder-like efficiency (document embeddings are precomputed). ColBERTv2 adds residual compression: cluster centroids + 2-bit residuals reduce storage from ~150 bytes/token to ~10 bytes/token. The index stores per-token embeddings, so storage scales as num_docs × avg_tokens × compressed_dim.
when_to_use: When cross-encoder reranking is too slow but bi-encoder quality is insufficient, or when you need a single model that balances accuracy and throughput for large-scale retrieval.
steps: Encode all documents token-by-token into 128-dim embeddings (ColBERT's default) and store per-token representations
At query time, encode query tokens (with [Q] token augmentation and query padding to 32 tokens) and compute MaxSim against each candidate document
Apply ColBERTv2 compression: cluster token embeddings into centroids using k-means, store centroid_id + quantized residual per token
Build a PLAID index for end-to-end retrieval: centroid interaction pre-filters candidates, then full MaxSim scores the shortlist
Profile storage: for 1M documents with avg 200 tokens, uncompressed = 1M × 200 × 128 × 2 bytes ≈ 48GB; with ColBERTv2 compression ≈ 2-4GB
expected_outcome: A late-interaction retrieval system with near-cross-encoder quality and practical storage/latency characteristics through token-level compression.

title: RAG Pipeline: Retrieve-Then-Read Architecture
description: The standard RAG pipeline has three stages: (1) Retrieve top-K passages from a corpus using the user query, (2) Stuff retrieved passages into the LLM's context window with the query, (3) Generate an answer grounded in the retrieved evidence. Key design choices: K (number of passages, typically 3-10), context ordering (relevant-first vs relevant-last due to lost-in-the-middle effect), and whether to include passage metadata (source, date) for attribution. The lost-in-the-middle finding shows LLMs attend more to the beginning and end of context — placing the most relevant passage first or last improves answer quality.
when_to_use: When building a question-answering system over a document corpus, when LLM responses need to be grounded in specific sources, or when designing the core retrieval-to-generation pipeline.
steps: Retrieve top-K passages (start with K=5) using the best available retrieval method for the corpus
Format the context: prepend each passage with its source metadata (document title, page number) for attribution
Order passages by relevance score descending (most relevant first) to exploit primacy bias, or interleave if diversity matters
Construct the prompt: system instruction specifying to answer only from provided context, then context block, then user question
Add a faithfulness check: instruct the model to say 'I don't have enough information' if no passage addresses the query rather than hallucinating
expected_outcome: A functional RAG pipeline that answers questions using retrieved evidence with source attribution and controlled hallucination behavior.

title: Product Quantization for Vector Compression
description: Product Quantization (PQ) compresses high-dimensional vectors by splitting each vector into m subvectors, quantizing each subvector to its nearest centroid from a learned codebook of k centroids. A 768-dim float32 vector (3072 bytes) split into m=96 subvectors with k=256 centroids becomes 96 bytes (96 × 8-bit centroid IDs). Distance computation uses precomputed centroid-to-centroid distance tables (ADC: Asymmetric Distance Computation). The accuracy-compression tradeoff: fewer subvectors or fewer centroids = more compression but lower recall. OPQ (Optimized PQ) rotates the vector space to minimize quantization error before splitting.
when_to_use: When vector index memory exceeds available RAM, when scaling to billions of vectors, or when storage cost per vector needs to drop below 100 bytes.
steps: Choose m (number of subvectors): dim must be divisible by m, and m=dim/8 is a common starting point (e.g., m=96 for 768-dim)
Train codebooks on a representative sample of your vectors (10K-100K vectors) using k-means within each subspace
Apply OPQ rotation before quantization: learn a rotation matrix R that minimizes reconstruction error after PQ, then encode R×v instead of v
Measure recall@10 degradation: compare PQ-compressed ANN search against exact search on a test set — expect 5-15% recall loss for 32x compression
Combine with IVF: IVF partitions the space into Voronoi cells (typically sqrt(N) cells), then PQ compresses vectors within each cell — this is the IVF-PQ pattern used by FAISS
expected_outcome: Compressed vector index achieving 16-64x storage reduction with quantified recall degradation, enabling billion-scale vector search on commodity hardware.

title: Query Decomposition for Complex Multi-Hop Questions
description: Complex queries often combine multiple information needs that no single passage can satisfy. Query decomposition breaks a complex query into atomic sub-queries, retrieves for each independently, then synthesizes. Example: 'Compare the GDP growth rates of the top 3 countries by population' decomposes to (1) 'top 3 countries by population', (2) 'GDP growth rate of [country1]', (3) 'GDP growth rate of [country2]', (4) 'GDP growth rate of [country3]'. This is essential for multi-hop reasoning where the answer depends on chaining facts from different documents.
when_to_use: When queries require combining information from multiple documents, when single-retrieval RAG returns incomplete answers, or when queries contain implicit sub-questions.
steps: Classify the query: single-hop (one retrieval suffices) vs multi-hop (requires composition of multiple facts) — use an LLM to detect dependency structure
Decompose into atomic sub-queries where each can be answered by a single passage — preserve dependency order (some sub-queries depend on answers to earlier ones)
Execute sub-queries sequentially for dependent chains or in parallel for independent sub-queries
