Collect retrieved passages for each sub-query and synthesize: pass all sub-query results to the LLM with the original complex query
Handle failures gracefully: if a sub-query retrieves nothing relevant, note the gap rather than hallucinating the sub-answer
expected_outcome: Accurate answers to complex multi-hop queries by decomposing into retrievable atomic sub-questions with explicit handling of inter-query dependencies.

title: Metadata Filtering and Structured Pre-Filtering in Vector Search
description: Metadata filtering restricts vector search to documents matching attribute constraints (e.g., date > 2024, category = 'finance'). Implementation approaches: pre-filtering (filter then search — fast if filter is selective but misses if index partitioning doesn't align), post-filtering (search then filter — can return fewer than K results), and integrated filtering (hybrid indexes like Qdrant's payload indexes that filter during HNSW traversal). Pre-filtering works well when the filter selects <10% of the corpus; post-filtering works when it selects >50%. Between 10-50%, integrated filtering avoids both failure modes.
when_to_use: When retrieval needs to respect structured constraints (time ranges, categories, access controls, document types) alongside semantic similarity.
steps: Identify which metadata fields will be used for filtering — index these as filterable fields in the vector database (not just stored payload)
Choose the filtering strategy based on selectivity: pre-filter for <10% of corpus, post-filter for >50%, integrated for 10-50%
For pre-filtering: ensure the vector database supports filtered HNSW search (Qdrant, Weaviate) or use partitioned indexes (one HNSW per partition)
For post-filtering: over-retrieve by 3-5x the target K to ensure enough results survive the filter (e.g., retrieve top-50 then filter to top-10)
Test edge cases: what happens when filters match zero documents? When the filtered subset is very small (<100 vectors) and HNSW degrades?
expected_outcome: A retrieval system that correctly combines semantic similarity with structured attribute filtering without sacrificing recall or returning empty result sets.

title: Self-RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Self-Reflection
description: Self-RAG trains the LLM to emit special reflection tokens that control retrieval and assess its own outputs. Three key tokens: [Retrieve] (should I retrieve? yes/no/continue), [IsRel] (is retrieved passage relevant to the query?), [IsSup] (is my generated response supported by the passage?). The model learns to retrieve only when needed, reject irrelevant passages, and verify its outputs are grounded. This avoids unnecessary retrieval (improves efficiency) and reduces hallucination (the model self-checks grounding). Trained by distilling critic labels from GPT-4 into the generator.
when_to_use: When standard RAG pipelines hallucinate despite having relevant passages, when retrieval is sometimes unnecessary and adds latency, or when you need fine-grained control over when and how retrieval is used.
steps: Understand the three reflection token types: [Retrieve] decides whether to invoke retrieval, [IsRel] filters irrelevant passages, [IsSup] checks factual grounding
For training: generate critic labels using a strong LLM (GPT-4) on (query, passage, response) triples, then fine-tune the target model to predict both response and reflection tokens
At inference: the model generates tokens; when it produces [Retrieve=Yes], pause generation, retrieve passages, score each with [IsRel], keep relevant ones, continue generation
After generation: use [IsSup] scores to select the best response among beam candidates or flag unsupported claims
Compare against standard RAG on your benchmark — Self-RAG typically improves on tasks requiring selective retrieval (mix of knowledge-intensive and reasoning queries)
expected_outcome: A RAG system that adaptively decides when to retrieve, filters irrelevant passages, and self-verifies factual grounding, reducing hallucination while maintaining efficiency.

title: Distance Metrics for Vector Similarity Search
description: The three main distance metrics for vector search: Cosine similarity = (A·B)/(||A||·||B||), range [-1,1], measures angular similarity independent of magnitude — best for normalized embeddings. Euclidean (L2) distance = sqrt(Σ(a_i - b_i)²) — sensitive to magnitude, useful when vector norms carry meaning. Dot product = Σ(a_i · b_i) — equivalent to cosine for unit vectors, faster to compute since no normalization. For Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS), dot product is correct because magnitude encodes importance. Most embedding models produce normalized vectors, making cosine and dot product interchangeable.
when_to_use: When configuring a vector database index and selecting the distance metric, or when debugging retrieval quality issues caused by metric mismatch.
steps: Check if your embedding model outputs normalized vectors (||v|| ≈ 1.0 for all vectors) — if yes, cosine and dot product are equivalent; use dot product for speed
If embeddings are NOT normalized and you want pure semantic similarity, use cosine similarity (or normalize before indexing and use dot product)
For MIPS tasks (e.g., retrieval where vector magnitude encodes document importance or quality), use dot product without normalization
Verify metric consistency: the metric used for indexing MUST match the metric used for querying — mixing metrics silently degrades results
Benchmark: index the same vectors with each metric and measure recall@10 — for normalized embeddings, results should be identical for cosine and dot product
expected_outcome: Correct distance metric selection aligned with the embedding model's output characteristics, preventing silent quality degradation from metric mismatch.

title: Table Parsing and Structured Data Extraction for Retrieval
description: Tables in documents (PDFs, HTML, scanned images) require specialized extraction because standard text chunking destroys tabular structure. Approaches: (1) Rule-based extraction using PDF layout analysis (pdfplumber, Camelot) detects cell boundaries by line intersections, (2) Vision-based extraction (Table Transformer, GPT-4V) handles scanned/complex tables, (3) Linearization converts tables to text rows ('Column1: value1, Column2: value2') for embedding. Key challenge: embedding models perform poorly on raw tabular data — linearizing with column headers produces embeddings 2-3x more retrievable than raw HTML table markup.
when_to_use: When the document corpus contains tables that are important for answering queries, when retrieval fails on tabular data, or when building ingestion pipelines for PDFs with mixed content.
steps: Detect table regions in documents: use pdfplumber for born-digital PDFs (line-based detection) or Table Transformer for scanned documents
Extract cell contents preserving row-column associations — verify by reconstructing the table from extracted data
Linearize each table row as 'Header1: value1 | Header2: value2 | ...' — include the table caption and surrounding context as metadata
Create both a per-row chunk (for specific cell lookups) and a full-table summary chunk (for aggregate questions about the table)
Test retrieval on table-specific queries (e.g., 'What was revenue in Q3 2024?') and verify the correct row/cell is retrieved
expected_outcome: Reliable extraction and indexing of tabular data that enables semantic search over table contents with correct column-value associations.

title: Embedding Fine-Tuning with Contrastive Learning and Hard Negatives
description: Fine-tuning embedding models on domain data dramatically improves retrieval quality (typically +10-30% recall for specialized domains). Contrastive learning uses InfoNCE loss: L = -log(exp(sim(q,d+)/τ) / Σ exp(sim(q,d_i)/τ)) where d+ is the positive and d_i are negatives. Hard negatives — documents that are similar but irrelevant — are critical: random negatives are too easy. Mining strategies: BM25 top-K negatives (lexically similar but semantically different), in-batch negatives (other queries' positives), or cross-encoder scored negatives (high bi-encoder score, low cross-encoder score). Temperature τ (typically 0.05-0.1) controls how peaked the distribution is.
when_to_use: When off-the-shelf embedding models underperform on domain-specific retrieval, when you have domain-specific query-document pairs for training, or when retrieval precision on specialized terminology is critical.
steps: Collect training triples: (query, positive_document, hard_negatives) — minimum 10K triples for meaningful improvement, 100K+ for large gains
Mine hard negatives: retrieve top-50 with the current embedding model, filter out true positives, keep top-5 hard negatives per query
Fine-tune using contrastive loss with in-batch negatives (batch size 32-128) plus mined hard negatives — use τ=0.05 and learning rate 2e-5
Apply matryoshka loss if you need dimension flexibility: compute contrastive loss at multiple dimension truncations (e.g., 64, 128, 256, 768)
Evaluate on a held-out test set with exact recall@10 (not ANN recall) to isolate embedding quality from index quality — expect 15-30% improvement on domain data
expected_outcome: A domain-adapted embedding model with significantly improved retrieval quality on specialized terminology and domain-specific query patterns.

title: Caching Strategies for Retrieval Pipeline Latency Optimization
description: Retrieval pipelines have multiple cacheable components: (1) Query embedding cache (LRU, keyed on query text) — eliminates embedding model inference for repeated queries, (2) Result cache (keyed on query + filters) — returns precomputed results for identical queries, (3) KV-cache for LLM generation in RAG — avoids re-encoding retrieved passages across turns. Semantic caching extends this by matching new queries to cached queries using embedding similarity (threshold ~0.95 cosine) — risky because near-identical queries may need different results. Cache invalidation is critical when the document index is updated.
when_to_use: When retrieval pipeline latency exceeds SLA targets, when query patterns show high repetition, or when embedding model inference is the latency bottleneck.
steps: Profile the pipeline to identify the latency bottleneck: embedding inference (typically 20-50ms), ANN search (5-20ms), reranking (50-200ms), or LLM generation (200-2000ms)
Implement query embedding cache with LRU eviction and TTL — hash the query text for the cache key, store the embedding vector
For result caching: hash (query_text + sorted_filter_params) as the cache key, store the ranked result list with TTL matching index refresh frequency
If using semantic caching: embed new queries and check cosine similarity against cached query embeddings — only serve cached results above 0.98 threshold (not 0.95, which is too aggressive)
Implement cache invalidation: when documents are added/updated/deleted, invalidate result caches but preserve embedding caches (query embeddings don't change with index updates)
expected_outcome: Reduced p50 and p99 latency for the retrieval pipeline through targeted caching of the most expensive operations, with proper invalidation preventing stale results.

title: Bi-Encoder vs Cross-Encoder Architecture Tradeoffs
description: Bi-encoders encode query and document independently into fixed vectors, enabling precomputation of document embeddings and ANN search at O(log N). Cross-encoders jointly encode the (query, document) pair with full cross-attention, achieving higher accuracy but requiring O(N) computation per query. Quantitative gap: on MS MARCO, cross-encoders achieve ~38-40 MRR@10 vs bi-encoders at ~33-36 MRR@10. Distillation bridges this gap: train a bi-encoder using cross-encoder scores as soft labels (margin-MSE loss), recovering 60-80% of the quality gap while maintaining O(log N) serving.
when_to_use: When choosing between retrieval architectures, when deciding whether to add a reranking stage, or when designing a distillation pipeline to improve bi-encoder quality.
steps: Establish baseline: evaluate both a bi-encoder and cross-encoder on your evaluation set to quantify the accuracy gap for your specific domain
If the gap is <5% MRR, use bi-encoder alone — the complexity of a two-stage pipeline may not be worth the marginal gain
If the gap is >10% MRR, implement two-stage: bi-encoder retrieval (top-100) → cross-encoder reranking (top-10-20)

To improve the bi-encoder without reranking overhead: distill cross-encoder knowledge by scoring 100K (query, document) pairs with the cross-encoder, then fine-tune the bi-encoder with margin-MSE loss on these scores
Monitor in production: track the rank correlation between bi-encoder scores and cross-encoder scores — if it drops, the bi-encoder may need retraining
expected_outcome: An informed architecture decision between bi-encoder, cross-encoder, or two-stage retrieval with quantified quality-latency tradeoffs for the specific use case.

title: Pseudo-Relevance Feedback for Automatic Query Expansion
description: Pseudo-relevance feedback (PRF) assumes the top-K initially retrieved documents are relevant and uses their content to expand the query. RM3 (Relevance Model 3) extracts expansion terms by computing P(w|R) ∝ Σ(d∈top-K) P(w|d)·P(d|q), then interpolates: q_expanded = α·q_original + (1-α)·q_feedback where α is typically 0.5-0.8. This helps when relevant documents use synonyms or related terms not in the original query. Risk: if initial retrieval returns irrelevant documents (query drift), expansion amplifies the error. Mitigation: use small K (3-5) and high α (0.7+) to limit drift.
when_to_use: When queries are short and retrieval recall is limited by vocabulary mismatch, when manual query expansion is not feasible, or when improving recall for tail queries.
steps: Retrieve initial top-K results (K=3-5 to minimize drift risk) using the original query
Extract candidate expansion terms: compute term frequency across the top-K documents, weighted by document score
Score expansion terms using RM3: P(w|R) proportional to sum of P(w|d)·P(d|q) across top-K documents
Select the top-M expansion terms (M=10-20) and interpolate with the original query using α=0.7 (70% original, 30% feedback)
Re-retrieve with the expanded query and compare recall@100 against the original query — expect 5-15% improvement when PRF works, but monitor for query drift failures
expected_outcome: Improved recall through automatic query expansion with controlled drift risk, measurably better performance on vocabulary-mismatch queries.

title: Multi-Modal Retrieval with CLIP Embeddings
description: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training) encodes images and text into a shared 512/768-dim embedding space where semantically similar images and text have high cosine similarity. This enables: text-to-image search (embed query text, find nearest image embeddings), image-to-text search, and image-to-image search. CLIP scores are typically in the range [0.15, 0.35] for relevant pairs — much lower than text-text similarities. For domain-specific retrieval, fine-tuning CLIP on domain (image, caption) pairs using contrastive loss significantly improves relevance. SigLIP replaces softmax loss with sigmoid, enabling better batch scaling.
when_to_use: When building search systems over image collections, when queries mix text and images, or when retrieval needs to match text descriptions to visual content.
steps: Encode all images in the corpus using CLIP's vision encoder — store as float32 vectors (normalize to unit length for cosine search)
At query time, encode the text query using CLIP's text encoder — the resulting vector is in the same space as image embeddings
Retrieve using cosine similarity — note that CLIP similarity scores are uncalibrated (0.25 might be a strong match), so use rank-based evaluation rather than threshold-based
For mixed corpora (images + text documents), embed text chunks with the text encoder and images with the vision encoder — they share the same space
If domain-specific accuracy is poor, fine-tune on domain (image, text) pairs using contrastive loss — even 5K-10K pairs can substantially improve domain performance
expected_outcome: A cross-modal retrieval system that finds relevant images from text queries (or vice versa) using shared embedding space, with calibrated expectations for similarity score ranges.

title: Knowledge Graph-Augmented Retrieval
description: Knowledge graphs (KGs) complement vector retrieval by providing structured relational knowledge that embedding similarity cannot capture. Pattern: extract entities from the query, traverse the KG to find related entities and relationships, then use these as additional retrieval signals or context. Example: query 'side effects of metformin' → KG traversal finds metformin→treats→diabetes, metformin→interacts_with→alcohol, metformin→side_effect→lactic_acidosis → retrieve documents about each expanded concept. GraphRAG builds a hierarchical community structure over the KG for summarization-oriented queries.
when_to_use: When queries require relational reasoning (comparisons, causal chains, hierarchical relationships), when the corpus has rich entity structure, or when vector retrieval misses documents with implicit rather than explicit relevance.
steps: Build or import a knowledge graph: extract (entity, relation, entity) triples from the corpus using NER + relation extraction, or import from domain KGs (UMLS for medical, Wikidata for general)
Implement entity linking: map query mentions to KG entities using string matching + embedding similarity for disambiguation
Define traversal strategies: 1-hop for direct relations, 2-hop for indirect connections, with edge-type filtering to stay on-topic
Combine KG-expanded entities with the original query for retrieval: either as additional search terms (BM25) or as additional embedding queries (dense)
For summarization queries, build community hierarchies (GraphRAG pattern): cluster KG entities into communities, generate community summaries, and retrieve at the appropriate abstraction level
expected_outcome: Enhanced retrieval that captures relational and structural knowledge beyond surface-level semantic similarity, improving answers for multi-entity and relational queries.

title: Context Window Stuffing vs Selective Retrieval Strategies
description: With LLMs supporting 128K+ context windows, a naive strategy is to stuff as many retrieved passages as possible. However, more context often hurts: the 'lost in the middle' effect shows recall degrades for passages in positions 5-15 of 20, and irrelevant passages actively degrade answer quality (noise dilution). Selective retrieval alternatives: (1) retrieve top-3-5 high-confidence passages only, (2) use a relevance threshold (e.g., cosine > 0.7) instead of fixed K, (3) iterative retrieval — generate a draft answer, identify gaps, retrieve targeted follow-up passages. Empirically, 3-5 high-quality passages outperform 15-20 mixed-quality passages on most QA benchmarks.
when_to_use: When deciding how many passages to include in the LLM context, when answer quality degrades with more retrieved passages, or when optimizing the precision-recall balance in RAG.
steps: Establish baseline: measure answer quality (correctness, faithfulness) as a function of K (number of retrieved passages) from 1 to 20 — plot the curve to find the optimal K

Implement a relevance threshold: only include passages with similarity score above a calibrated threshold — determine the threshold using a validation set where you know which passages are truly relevant
Apply the 'lost in the middle' mitigation: order passages as [most_relevant, least_relevant, ..., second_most_relevant] to exploit primacy and recency bias
For complex queries, use iterative retrieval: generate initial answer → identify unsupported claims → retrieve passages to support or refute each claim → regenerate
Compare total tokens consumed vs answer quality: fewer, better-targeted passages often improve quality AND reduce cost/latency
expected_outcome: Optimized passage selection that maximizes answer quality per context token, avoiding the diminishing returns and quality degradation of indiscriminate context stuffing.

title: IVF (Inverted File) Indexing for Large-Scale Vector Search
description: IVF partitions the vector space into nlist Voronoi cells using k-means clustering. At query time, only nprobe nearest cells are searched, reducing scan from N vectors to N×nprobe/nlist vectors. Typical configuration: nlist = sqrt(N) (e.g., 1000 for 1M vectors), nprobe = 1-10% of nlist. IVF alone uses flat (exact) search within cells; IVF-PQ combines partitioning with Product Quantization for both speed and memory savings. Training requires at least 30×nlist vectors for stable centroids. Key pitfall: cluster imbalance — if some cells contain 10x more vectors, those cells are slow to search.
when_to_use: When dataset size exceeds what HNSW can handle in memory (>10M vectors), when build time is constrained, or when combining with PQ for extreme compression.
steps: Set nlist based on dataset size: nlist = 4×sqrt(N) is a practical starting point (e.g., 4000 for 1M vectors, 12600 for 10M vectors)
Train centroids on a representative sample of at least 30×nlist vectors using k-means (FAISS provides GPU-accelerated training)
Build the index by assigning each vector to its nearest centroid — check for cluster imbalance by examining the distribution of cluster sizes
Tune nprobe at query time: sweep nprobe from 1 to nlist/10, measure recall@10 vs latency — typically nprobe=8-16 achieves >0.9 recall@10
If memory is constrained, add PQ compression within each cell (IVF+PQ): this gives both search speedup (IVF) and memory reduction (PQ)
expected_outcome: A scalable ANN index that reduces search time proportionally to nprobe/nlist while maintaining high recall, with quantified tradeoffs for the target dataset.

title: OCR Integration for Scanned Document Retrieval
description: Scanned documents, images, and PDFs without embedded text require OCR before retrieval indexing. Modern OCR pipelines: Tesseract (open-source, ~95% accuracy on clean scans), PaddleOCR (multilingual, better on complex layouts), or cloud APIs (Google Vision, AWS Textract — 98-99% accuracy with table/form understanding). Post-OCR processing is critical: (1) deskewing and binarization improve OCR accuracy by 5-10%, (2) confidence scores per word enable filtering low-quality text, (3) spatial layout information (bounding boxes) preserves document structure for table extraction. OCR errors compound in retrieval: a misspelled term won't match keyword search and may produce a misleading embedding.
when_to_use: When the document corpus includes scanned PDFs, photographs of documents, or images with embedded text that needs to be searchable.
steps: Classify documents: born-digital PDFs (extract text directly with pdfplumber/pymupdf) vs scanned (require OCR) — check if PDF text layer exists and is non-empty
Preprocess scanned images: deskew (detect rotation angle), binarize (Otsu thresholding), and denoise to maximize OCR accuracy
Run OCR with confidence scores: filter out words below 0.7 confidence or flag them for manual review
Preserve spatial layout: use bounding box coordinates to reconstruct reading order, detect columns, and identify table regions
Post-process OCR text: apply spell-checking (only for known-vocabulary domains), merge hyphenated words across line breaks, and normalize whitespace
expected_outcome: Searchable text extracted from scanned documents with controlled error rates, spatial layout preservation, and quality metadata for downstream retrieval.

title: Iterative Retrieval with FLARE and Active RAG
description: FLARE (Forward-Looking Active REtrieval) triggers retrieval dynamically during generation rather than only before generation. The model generates until it reaches a low-confidence token (probability < threshold, typically 0.5), then retrieves passages relevant to the generated-so-far context plus the uncertain continuation. This produces more targeted retrieval than single up-front retrieval because the model knows what specific information it needs. Active RAG extends this by having the LLM explicitly generate retrieval queries mid-generation when it recognizes knowledge gaps. Both approaches reduce hallucination in long-form generation where a single retrieval cannot cover all needed facts.
when_to_use: When generating long-form answers that require multiple distinct facts, when single up-front retrieval fails to cover all information needs, or when hallucination in later parts of long answers is a problem.
steps: Monitor token-level generation probabilities: flag spans where consecutive tokens have probability < 0.5 as potential hallucination points
At each low-confidence span: extract the sentence-in-progress as the retrieval query (or use the model to reformulate a better query from context)
Retrieve passages relevant to the specific uncertain claim, not the original user query — this targets the actual knowledge gap
Insert retrieved passages into the context and regenerate from the uncertain point — either replace the low-confidence text or let the model choose
Set a maximum retrieval budget (e.g., 5 retrievals per generation) to bound latency, and fall back to explicit uncertainty expression ('I'm not sure about...') if budget is exhausted
expected_outcome: Long-form generated text with dynamically retrieved evidence supporting each claim, reduced hallucination in the middle and tail of long answers.

title: Recall@K Analysis and Retrieval Error Diagnosis
description: Recall@K = (number of relevant documents in top K) / (total relevant documents) measures retrieval coverage. Analyzing Recall@K at multiple cutoffs (K=1, 5, 10, 50, 100) reveals different failure modes: low Recall@1 but high Recall@100 means ranking is poor but the index contains relevant documents (fix: better reranking). Low Recall@100 means relevant documents are missing from the index or embeddings can't match them (fix: better chunking, embeddings, or hybrid search). The Recall@K curve shape is diagnostic: concave = good ranking, linear = random ranking, convex = relevant documents buried deep.
when_to_use: When diagnosing why a retrieval system fails on specific queries, when distinguishing between embedding quality issues and ranking issues, or when setting up retrieval monitoring.
steps: Compute Recall@K at K=1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 on a labeled evaluation set with known relevant documents per query
Plot the Recall@K curve: concave (good) — most relevant docs are ranked high; linear (bad) — ranking is essentially random; convex (very bad) — relevant docs are systematically ranked low
For queries with low Recall@100: inspect the relevant documents — are they in the index? Do their embeddings have reasonable similarity to the query embedding?
For queries with high Recall@100 but low Recall@10: the documents are retrievable but poorly ranked — compare bi-encoder scores vs cross-encoder scores to identify ranking errors
Segment analysis by query type (short vs long, entity-specific vs conceptual) to identify which query categories need different treatment
expected_outcome: Root-cause diagnosis of retrieval failures with specific, actionable interventions for each failure mode (embedding quality, ranking, indexing gaps).

title: Semantic Search vs Keyword Search: When to Use Which
description: Keyword search (BM25) excels at exact term matching, entity names, codes, and queries with precise terminology — it will always find 'CVE-2024-1234' if it exists. Semantic search (dense embeddings) excels at paraphrases, conceptual queries, and vocabulary mismatch — 'heart attack treatment' matches 'myocardial infarction therapy'. Neither dominates: keyword search fails on synonyms and paraphrases (semantic gap), while semantic search fails on rare entities, exact codes, and negation ('not diabetes' may match diabetes documents). Hybrid search (combining both) typically outperforms either alone by 5-15% on diverse query sets.
when_to_use: When choosing a retrieval approach for a new system, when explaining retrieval failures to stakeholders, or when deciding whether hybrid search complexity is justified.
steps: Categorize your query workload: what fraction are keyword-oriented (entity names, IDs, exact phrases) vs conceptual (natural language questions, paraphrases)?
Test both approaches on a representative eval set: measure recall@10 for keyword queries with BM25 and semantic queries with dense retrieval
Identify failure categories: BM25 failures (vocabulary mismatch, paraphrase) vs dense failures (rare entities, exact codes, negation)
If both failure types are present, implement hybrid search: BM25 + dense retrieval fused with RRF (k=60)
Monitor query-level performance: track which queries are answered only by keyword, only by semantic, or by both — this validates the hybrid approach and identifies residual gaps
expected_outcome: An evidence-based retrieval strategy selection that addresses the specific query patterns in the workload, with hybrid search justified by measured complementarity.

title: Parent-Child Document Retrieval Architecture
description: Parent-child retrieval indexes small chunks for precision but returns their parent context for completeness. Implementation: split documents into large parent chunks (e.g., 2000 tokens per section), then split each parent into small child chunks (e.g., 256 tokens). Index only child chunks for embedding search. When a child matches, retrieve its parent chunk (or siblings) for the LLM context. This solves two problems: (1) small chunks embed better (less information dilution) but provide too little context for answer generation, (2) returning full sections gives the LLM enough context to generate comprehensive answers. Store parent-child relationships via doc_id + chunk_id hierarchy.
when_to_use: When retrieval precision is good (correct passages are found) but generated answers are incomplete because individual chunks lack sufficient context, or when chunks are too small for meaningful answer generation.
steps: Define the parent-child hierarchy: parent = section/page (1000-2000 tokens), child = paragraph/passage (200-400 tokens)
Index children with metadata linking each to its parent: {child_id, parent_id, position_in_parent, child_embedding}
At retrieval time: search over child embeddings, deduplicate by parent_id (keep highest-scored child per parent), retrieve the full parent text
Optionally include sibling chunks: if the query matches a child at the boundary of a parent, include the adjacent parent as well
Tune the parent-child size ratio: if parents are too large, they dilute the context; if too small, they don't add much over children alone — typically 4-8x size ratio works well
expected_outcome: A retrieval system that achieves high precision through small-chunk matching while providing sufficient context through parent-chunk retrieval for comprehensive answer generation.

title: Embedding Model Alignment with RLHF for Retrieval
description: Standard contrastive training optimizes for similarity between queries and relevant documents, but doesn't directly optimize for downstream task performance (e.g., answer quality in RAG). RLHF for retrieval uses feedback from the generation stage: if the LLM produces a correct answer from the retrieved passages, the retrieval gets a positive reward; if it fails or hallucinates, negative reward. The reward model can be the downstream QA accuracy itself or a learned preference model. Training uses PPO or DPO to update the embedding model's encoder. This aligns the retriever's notion of 'relevant' with what actually helps the generator.
when_to_use: When retrieval metrics (recall, NDCG) are high but downstream RAG performance is poor — the retriever finds 'relevant' documents that don't actually help answer the question. When optimizing end-to-end RAG quality beyond standard retrieval metrics.
steps: Collect (query, retrieved_passages, generated_answer, correctness_label) tuples from the existing RAG pipeline — use automated evaluation or human labels for correctness
Identify retrieval patterns that lead to generation failure: passages that are topically relevant but don't contain the specific answer (false positive relevance)
Define the reward signal: +1 for retrieval sets that lead to correct generation, -1 for retrieval sets that lead to hallucination or incorrect answers
Fine-tune the embedding model using DPO: prefer passage sets that produce correct answers over passage sets that produce incorrect answers for the same query
Evaluate end-to-end: measure both retrieval metrics (recall@10) and generation metrics (answer correctness, faithfulness) — retrieval metrics may decrease while generation metrics improve
expected_outcome: An embedding model aligned with downstream generation quality, where 'retrieved relevance' correlates with 'actually helps produce a correct answer' rather than just topical similarity.

title: Precision-Recall Curves and Optimal Cutoff Selection
description: Precision-recall curves plot precision (fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant) vs recall (fraction of relevant documents that are retrieved) at varying score thresholds. The area under the PR curve (AUC-PR) summarizes overall quality — random retrieval gives AUC-PR ≈ (num_relevant / num_total). The optimal operating point depends on the application: high-recall applications (legal discovery, medical search) optimize for recall ≥ 0.95 even at precision = 0.3; precision-focused applications (customer-facing search) optimize for precision ≥ 0.8 even at recall = 0.5. The F1 point on the PR curve balances both: F1 = 2PR/(P+R).
when_to_use: When setting a relevance score threshold for filtering retrieval results, when comparing two retrieval systems on a labeled dataset, or when communicating retrieval quality tradeoffs to stakeholders.
steps: Compute precision and recall at each score threshold: sweep the retrieval score from max to min, computing (precision, recall) at each threshold
Plot the PR curve and compute AUC-PR — compare against the random baseline of (num_relevant / corpus_size)
Identify the operating point that matches the application need: find the threshold where recall ≥ target (for recall-oriented) or precision ≥ target (for precision-oriented)
Compare systems: a PR curve that dominates another at all operating points is strictly better; if curves cross, the better system depends on the precision-recall tradeoff preference
Report the F1-optimal threshold as a default if no specific precision or recall target exists: find the threshold that maximizes 2PR/(P+R)
expected_outcome: A calibrated score threshold for the retrieval system with a documented precision-recall tradeoff curve that stakeholders can use to adjust the operating point.

title: Document-Level Metadata Enrichment for Retrieval
description: Enriching documents with extracted metadata before indexing enables both filtered search and metadata-augmented embeddings. Key metadata to extract: (1) Named entities (people, organizations, locations) using NER — enables entity-filtered search, (2) Document type classification (tutorial, API reference, research paper) — enables type-filtered retrieval, (3) Temporal references (dates, time periods) — enables time-scoped search, (4) Summary/abstract generation — creates a compressed representation for embedding that captures the full document's semantics. Metadata can be prepended to chunk text before embedding ('Topic: machine learning. Date: 2024. Content: ...') to improve embedding quality.
when_to_use: When raw text chunking loses important document-level context, when users need to filter by attributes (date, author, category), or when embedding quality suffers from missing contextual information.
steps: Define the metadata schema for your corpus: entity types, date formats, categories, and other attributes that users filter by
Extract metadata using NER (spaCy, Flair) for entities, regex/LLM for dates, and classifier or LLM for document type
Store metadata both as filterable fields in the vector database AND as prepended text in the chunk content for embedding
Prepend metadata to chunk text before embedding: 'Document: {title}. Section: {section_heading}. {chunk_text}' — this gives the embedding model document-level context
Validate by comparing retrieval quality with and without metadata enrichment on queries that reference metadata attributes (e.g., 'recent papers on transformers' should benefit from date metadata)
expected_outcome: Enriched document index with extractable metadata that enables both filtered search and improved embedding quality through contextual prepending.

title: Learned Sparse-Dense Hybrid Weighting
description: Instead of fixed weights for combining sparse (BM25) and dense retrieval, learned weighting adapts the combination per query. A lightweight classifier (logistic regression or small neural network) takes query features (length, entity count, vocabulary overlap with corpus) and predicts the optimal weight α for score_final = α·dense + (1-α)·sparse. Training data: for each query in a labeled set, sweep α from 0 to 1, find the α that maximizes NDCG@10, and use (query_features, optimal_α) pairs. Alternatively, use a cross-encoder as an oracle to re-score and find the optimal α per query. This typically outperforms fixed RRF by 3-5% NDCG on heterogeneous query workloads.
when_to_use: When query types are diverse (mix of keyword and natural language) and fixed fusion weights underperform on some query categories, or when optimizing hybrid search beyond the RRF baseline.
steps: Build a labeled evaluation set: for each query, compute the optimal fusion weight α by sweeping [0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 1.0] and finding the α that maximizes NDCG@10
Extract query features: query length (tokens), number of named entities, max IDF of query terms, average embedding similarity to corpus centroid, presence of quoted phrases
Train a regression model (ridge regression or gradient boosting) to predict optimal α from query features — use 5-fold cross-validation
At inference: extract features from the incoming query, predict α, compute score_final = α·normalized_dense + (1-α)·normalized_sparse
Monitor per-query-type performance: track α predictions and actual NDCG for keyword-heavy vs conceptual queries to verify the model adapts appropriately
expected_outcome: A query-adaptive hybrid search system that dynamically balances sparse and dense retrieval based on query characteristics, outperforming static fusion weights.

title: Blackboard Architecture for Shared State
description: The blackboard pattern uses a centralized data store where agents read and write partial solutions independently. Each agent monitors the blackboard for patterns it can contribute to, firing when its preconditions are met. Unlike message-passing, blackboard avoids O(n²) pairwise connections — but introduces contention: with >8 concurrent writers, throughput drops ~40% without optimistic locking or partitioned write regions.
when_to_use: When multiple agents must collaboratively build a shared artifact (e.g., a document, plan, or knowledge graph) and agents have heterogeneous capabilities that contribute to different parts of the solution.
steps: Step 1: Define blackboard schema with typed regions (e.g., 'hypotheses', 'evidence', 'conclusions') so agents only watch relevant partitions
Step 2: Implement knowledge sources (KS) as agent-trigger pairs — each KS specifies preconditions on blackboard state that activate it
Step 3: Add a control shell that resolves conflicts when multiple KS fire simultaneously (priority-based, recency-based, or round-robin)
Step 4: Use versioned writes with compare-and-swap to prevent lost updates when two agents modify the same region
Step 5: Monitor blackboard convergence by tracking change rate — when delta between iterations drops below threshold, declare solution stable
expected_outcome: A shared-state architecture where heterogeneous agents converge on a solution without direct inter-agent communication, with controlled write contention.

title: Leader Election via Bully Algorithm for Agent Coordination
description: When a multi-agent system needs a single coordinator (e.g., to assign tasks or break ties), the bully algorithm elects the agent with the highest capability score. Each agent can initiate an election by sending 'ELECTION' to all higher-ranked agents. If none respond within timeout, the initiator becomes leader. The algorithm guarantees exactly one leader but has O(n²) message complexity in the worst case. For LLM agents, 'capability score' can be derived from model size, context window, or domain-specific benchmark results.
when_to_use: When a group of agents needs a single decision-maker for task allocation, tie-breaking, or final approval, and the leader may fail or become unavailable during execution.
steps: Step 1: Assign each agent a unique priority score based on capability metrics (model quality, specialization match, available context window)
Step 2: When an agent detects the current leader is unresponsive (heartbeat timeout > 3 cycles), it initiates election by messaging all higher-priority agents
Step 3: Higher-priority agents respond with 'ALIVE' and start their own elections upward; the initiator backs off
Step 4: The highest-priority responding agent sends 'COORDINATOR' to all agents, becoming the new leader
Step 5: Implement leader heartbeat at 2x the expected task completion interval to detect failures before they cascade
expected_outcome: A self-healing coordination layer where leader failure is automatically detected and a new leader is elected within bounded time, maintaining system liveness.

title: Debate-Style Adversarial Verification Between LLM Agents
description: In debate-style verification (Du et al., 2023), two LLM instances argue opposing positions on a claim while a judge agent evaluates both arguments. This reduces sycophancy by ~35% compared to single-agent verification because neither debater has incentive to agree with the other. The key design choice is whether debaters see each other's arguments (simultaneous debate) or respond sequentially (sequential debate). Sequential debate produces better arguments but costs 2-3x more tokens.
when_to_use: When verifying factual claims, reviewing generated code for correctness, or evaluating whether a plan is sound — any scenario where a single agent might confidently produce wrong answers.
steps: Step 1: Frame the proposition clearly and assign one agent as advocate (argues FOR) and one as critic (argues AGAINST)
Step 2: Run 2-3 rounds of sequential argumentation — each debater sees the other's previous argument and must directly address it
Step 3: The judge agent receives the full debate transcript and evaluates based on argument quality, evidence cited, and logical coherence
Step 4: If the judge's confidence is below 0.7, escalate to a second judge or add a third debate round
Step 5: Log the final verdict with the judge's reasoning chain for auditability and downstream calibration
expected_outcome: Higher-accuracy verification of claims and outputs by forcing adversarial examination, with a documented reasoning trail for each decision.

title: Hierarchical Task Decomposition with Manager-Worker Topology
description: A manager agent breaks complex tasks into subtasks and delegates them to specialized worker agents. The manager maintains a task dependency DAG and only dispatches subtasks whose predecessors are complete. Critical design constraint: the manager should never execute subtasks itself — this prevents bottlenecking and keeps the manager's context window free for coordination. Worker results flow back through the manager, which synthesizes them into the final output.
when_to_use: When a task is too complex for a single agent's context window, requires multiple distinct capabilities (e.g., code generation + fact-checking + formatting), or has parallelizable subtasks.
steps: Step 1: Manager decomposes the task into a DAG of subtasks, identifying dependencies and parallelizable branches
Step 2: For each subtask, manager selects the best-suited worker based on capability matching (domain expertise, tool access, model strength)
Step 3: Dispatch independent subtasks in parallel; hold dependent subtasks until predecessors return results
Step 4: As results return, manager validates each against the subtask specification — reject and retry if output doesn't meet criteria
Step 5: Manager synthesizes worker outputs into a coherent final result, resolving any inconsistencies between parallel branches
expected_outcome: Complex tasks completed through coordinated specialization, with parallel execution of independent subtasks and quality-gated synthesis.

title: Structured Message Schemas for Inter-Agent Communication
description: Free-form natural language messages between agents cause interpretation drift — by round 5 of a multi-agent conversation, agents lose ~20% of the original task constraints (observed in AutoGen benchmarks). Structured message schemas with typed fields (action, payload, confidence, metadata) reduce this drift to <5%. The FIPA-ACL standard defines performatives (INFORM, REQUEST, PROPOSE, REJECT) that disambiguate intent, preventing agents from confusing status updates with action requests.
when_to_use: When agents exchange information more than 3 times in a workflow, when misinterpretation of agent messages has caused errors, or when building systems with >3 agents that must interoperate.
steps: Step 1: Define a message schema with required fields: sender_id, recipient_id, performative (INFORM/REQUEST/PROPOSE/CONFIRM/REJECT), content, timestamp
Step 2: Add typed payload schemas per performative — e.g., REQUEST must include 'action', 'parameters', 'deadline'; INFORM must include 'claim', 'evidence', 'confidence'
Step 3: Implement message validation at send and receive — reject malformed messages with specific error codes rather than guessing intent
Step 4: Include a 'reply_to' field linking responses to original messages, enabling conversation threading and audit trails
Step 5: Add a 'context_hash' field that checksums the shared state at message time, detecting when agents are operating on stale information
expected_outcome: Reliable inter-agent communication with low interpretation drift, clear intent signaling, and traceable conversation history.

title: Consensus Protocols for Multi-Agent Agreement
description: When multiple agents must agree on a single answer (e.g., a diagnosis, classification, or plan), majority voting is the simplest consensus mechanism but ignores agent confidence and expertise. Weighted voting assigns higher weight to agents with better track records on similar tasks. For LLM agents, the Dawid-Skene model estimates agent reliability and true labels simultaneously via EM, improving accuracy by 8-15% over simple majority vote on NLP annotation tasks.
when_to_use: When 3+ agents produce different answers to the same question and the system must commit to a single response, especially when agent quality varies or some agents may be unreliable.
steps: Step 1: Collect responses from all agents along with their self-reported confidence scores (softmax probability, chain-of-thought certainty, etc.)
Step 2: Apply confidence-weighted voting as a baseline: aggregate = Σ(w_i × vote_i) where w_i is calibrated confidence for agent i
Step 3: If historical performance data exists, fit Dawid-Skene model to estimate per-agent confusion matrices and adjust weights accordingly
Step 4: Check for unanimous agreement — if all agents agree, accept with high confidence; if split, flag for potential ambiguity in the task
Step 5: When consensus fails (no option exceeds 0.6 weighted support), trigger a deliberation round where dissenting agents explain their reasoning before re-voting
expected_outcome: A single high-confidence answer derived from multiple agents, with calibrated confidence reflecting agreement strength and agent reliability.

title: Role Specialization via Persona Prompting
description: Assigning distinct roles (e.g., 'security auditor', 'performance optimizer', 'UX advocate') to identical LLM instances produces meaningfully different outputs because the persona constrains the agent's attention and evaluation criteria. Research from Microsoft's AutoGen shows that role-specialized agents outperform a single generalist agent by 15-25% on tasks requiring diverse expertise, but only when roles are non-overlapping and clearly scoped. Over-specified personas (>200 words of role description) cause the agent to roleplay rather than apply expertise.
when_to_use: When a task benefits from multiple perspectives (code review, strategy evaluation, content creation) and you want diverse critiques without using multiple different models.
steps: Step 1: Identify 3-5 distinct evaluation dimensions the task requires (e.g., correctness, efficiency, readability, security for code review)
Step 2: Create one agent role per dimension with a 50-100 word persona that specifies expertise, evaluation criteria, and what to ignore
Step 3: Include a 'anti-persona' clause: 'You do NOT evaluate [X]' to prevent role overlap and reduce redundant feedback
Step 4: Have each role-specialized agent independently evaluate the artifact, producing structured feedback in its domain only
Step 5: A synthesizer agent merges feedback, resolving conflicts between roles (e.g., security vs. usability trade-offs) with explicit trade-off reasoning
expected_outcome: Multi-perspective evaluation from a single model family, producing diverse, non-redundant feedback that covers all critical dimensions of the task.

title: DAG-Based Workflow Orchestration with Conditional Branching
description: Multi-agent workflows are best modeled as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) where nodes are agent tasks and edges are data dependencies. Unlike linear pipelines, DAGs support parallel execution of independent branches and conditional routing (e.g., if code passes tests → deploy; else → debug agent). LangGraph and CrewAI implement this pattern. Critical failure mode: cycles in the graph cause infinite loops — validate DAG acyclicity at construction time using topological sort, not at runtime.
when_to_use: When a multi-agent workflow has branching logic, parallel stages, or conditional paths that depend on intermediate results rather than following a fixed linear sequence.
steps: Step 1: Map the workflow as a DAG — each node specifies: agent, input schema, output schema, timeout, retry policy
Step 2: Validate acyclicity via topological sort at construction time; reject graphs with cycles and suggest where to break them
Step 3: Implement conditional edges as predicate functions on predecessor outputs (e.g., 'if result.score > 0.8 → node_A else → node_B')
Step 4: Execute independent branches in parallel using an async executor; join nodes wait for all predecessors to complete before firing
Step 5: Add a global timeout and circuit breaker — if any branch exceeds 3x expected duration, cancel it and route to a fallback path
expected_outcome: A structured, executable workflow with parallel execution, conditional routing, and failure handling that completes complex multi-step tasks efficiently.

title: Error Propagation and Cascading Failure Prevention
description: In multi-agent pipelines, an upstream agent's error propagates to all downstream agents, often amplified. A hallucinated fact from agent A becomes 'established context' for agents B, C, D — this is cascade amplification. Studies on GPT-4 multi-agent chains show that a 5% per-stage error rate compounds to 23% end-to-end error over 5 stages. The mitigation is stage-gating: each agent's output passes a validation check before being forwarded. Failed validations trigger retry with modified prompts, not immediate propagation.
when_to_use: When building pipelines of 3+ agents in sequence where each agent's input depends on the previous agent's output, especially when individual agents have non-trivial error rates.
steps: Step 1: Identify the critical path through the agent DAG — errors here have the highest blast radius
Step 2: Insert validation gates between stages: format checks (schema compliance), semantic checks (contradiction detection), and confidence thresholds
Step 3: Implement retry with rephrasing — when a gate rejects output, retry the failing agent with a modified prompt that highlights the specific validation failure
Step 4: Set a retry budget (max 2 retries per stage) to prevent infinite retry loops; after budget exhaustion, route to a human-in-the-loop fallback
Step 5: Log all gate failures with the original output and rejection reason — these logs are the primary signal for identifying which agents need improvement
expected_outcome: Multi-stage pipelines with bounded error propagation, where upstream failures are caught and corrected before contaminating downstream agents.

title: Agent Spawning and Dynamic Lifecycle Management
description: Static agent pools waste resources when workload is variable. Dynamic spawning creates agents on-demand and terminates them when idle. The key design decision is spawn granularity: per-task spawning (new agent per request) provides perfect isolation but high latency (~2-5s for LLM agent initialization); pooled spawning (pre-warmed agents reused across tasks) reduces latency to <100ms but requires state cleanup between tasks. Memory leaks in long-lived agents — accumulated context causing degraded performance — are the primary argument for per-task spawning.
when_to_use: When workload is bursty (e.g., 10x traffic spikes), when tasks require different agent configurations, or when long-lived agents accumulate stale context that degrades output quality.
steps: Step 1: Define agent templates specifying model, system prompt, tools, and maximum context lifetime for each agent type
Step 2: Implement a spawn manager that creates agents from templates on-demand with unique session IDs for tracking
Step 3: Set maximum context lifetime (e.g., 50 turns or 100K tokens) — agents approaching the limit are gracefully drained and replaced
Step 4: Implement idle timeout — agents with no activity for >5 minutes are terminated to free resources, with state checkpointed for potential resumption
Step 5: Monitor agent pool metrics: spawn rate, active count, average lifetime, termination reason distribution — these drive capacity planning
expected_outcome: An elastic agent pool that scales with demand, prevents context degradation through lifecycle limits, and provides operational visibility into agent fleet health.

title: Conflict Resolution via Structured Argumentation
description: When two agents produce contradictory outputs (e.g., different code implementations, conflicting recommendations), naive resolution strategies like 'pick the longer answer' or 'pick the first' lose information. Structured argumentation requires each agent to provide: (1) its claim, (2) supporting evidence, (3) confidence level, (4) known limitations of its approach. A resolver agent then applies Dung's argumentation framework: arguments that attack each other are evaluated for 'acceptability' based on whether their supporting arguments are themselves undefeated.
when_to_use: When two or more agents produce mutually exclusive outputs and the system must choose one or synthesize a resolution, especially when both outputs appear plausible.
steps: Step 1: Detect conflict by comparing agent outputs — use semantic similarity (cosine < 0.7) or explicit contradiction detection (entailment model classifies as 'contradiction')
Step 2: Request structured arguments from each conflicting agent: claim, evidence chain, confidence score, acknowledged weaknesses
Step 3: Identify attack relations: where does Agent A's evidence undermine Agent B's claim, and vice versa
Step 4: Compute grounded extension — the maximal set of arguments that are conflict-free and defend themselves against all attacks
Step 5: If no clear winner, synthesize a merged response that incorporates the strongest undefeated arguments from both agents, noting the unresolved disagreement
expected_outcome: Principled resolution of inter-agent conflicts that preserves the strongest arguments from each side and makes the resolution reasoning transparent.

title: Shared Context Synchronization via Append-Only Logs
description: When multiple agents need to stay synchronized on evolving context (e.g., a growing document, accumulating evidence), mutable shared state causes race conditions. Append-only logs (inspired by event sourcing) solve this: each agent appends events to a shared log, and any agent can reconstruct current state by replaying the log. This provides natural auditability and conflict-free synchronization. The trade-off is growing read cost — after ~500 events, agents should work from materialized snapshots updated every N events rather than replaying the full log.
when_to_use: When multiple agents must maintain a consistent view of evolving shared state, especially when audit trails are required or when agents operate asynchronously at different speeds.
steps: Step 1: Define event types for the domain (e.g., FACT_ADDED, HYPOTHESIS_UPDATED, CONCLUSION_REACHED) with schemas for each
Step 2: Implement the append-only log with monotonically increasing sequence numbers — agents include their last-seen sequence number when appending to enable gap detection
Step 3: Each agent maintains a local materialized view by applying new events since its last checkpoint
Step 4: Create periodic snapshots (every 100 events) so new agents or restarted agents don't replay the full history
Step 5: Implement event compaction for long-running sessions — merge redundant events (e.g., multiple updates to the same field) to keep the log manageable
expected_outcome: Consistent shared state across asynchronous agents with full auditability, no race conditions, and bounded read costs through snapshots.

title: Round-Robin and Weighted Load Balancing Across Agent Pools
description: When multiple instances of the same agent type are available (e.g., a pool of code-review agents), load balancing determines which instance handles each request. Round-robin is simplest but ignores agent utilization — an agent processing a complex task gets the same traffic as an idle one. Least-connections routing sends requests to the agent with fewest active tasks. For LLM agents, context utilization is a better metric: route to the agent with the most remaining context window, since context-full agents produce lower-quality outputs.
when_to_use: When running multiple instances of the same agent type to handle concurrent requests, and request complexity varies so simple round-robin leads to uneven agent loading.
steps: Step 1: Instrument each agent with utilization metrics: active tasks, context tokens used, average response latency, error rate in last N requests
Step 2: Implement least-context-utilization routing: route new tasks to the agent with the most remaining context window capacity
Step 3: Add affinity routing for related subtasks — tasks from the same parent should go to the same agent to leverage cached context
Step 4: Implement circuit-breaking: if an agent's error rate exceeds 10% over the last 20 requests, remove it from the pool and trigger health check
Step 5: Monitor queue depth per agent — if any agent's queue exceeds 3x the pool average, redistribute its pending tasks to less-loaded agents
expected_outcome: Even distribution of work across agent pools with context-aware routing, automatic removal of unhealthy agents, and affinity for related tasks.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Escalation Patterns
description: Not every decision should be fully automated. Human-in-the-loop (HITL) integration in multi-agent systems requires defining escalation triggers, timeout behavior, and how to incorporate human feedback back into the agent workflow. The critical anti-pattern is blocking: if the system halts entirely waiting for human input, throughput drops to zero. Non-blocking escalation queues the decision, continues processing other tasks, and merges human input when available. Slack/email/webhook integrations should include the full agent reasoning context, not just the question.
when_to_use: When multi-agent systems handle tasks with varying risk levels, when certain decisions require human judgment (e.g., financial approvals, content moderation), or when agent confidence is too low for autonomous action.
steps: Step 1: Define escalation triggers: confidence below threshold (e.g., < 0.6), flagged content categories, dollar amount above limit, conflicting agent outputs with no resolution
Step 2: Package the escalation request with full context: original task, agent reasoning chain, options considered, recommended action, and why the agent is uncertain
Step 3: Implement non-blocking escalation — queue the decision, tag the workflow as 'pending-human', and continue processing independent tasks
Step 4: Set escalation timeouts with default actions: if human doesn't respond within T minutes, either apply the agent's best guess (low-risk) or reject the task (high-risk)
Step 5: When human feedback arrives, route it back to the originating agent along with the human's reasoning so the agent can learn the decision pattern
expected_outcome: A multi-agent system that autonomously handles routine decisions while escalating edge cases to humans without blocking the overall workflow.

title: Trust and Reputation Systems for Agent Reliability Tracking
description: In open multi-agent systems where agents may have variable quality or even adversarial behavior, trust scores quantify how reliable each agent's outputs have been historically. EigenTrust (Kamvar et al., 2003) computes global trust scores by aggregating local trust values through iterative matrix multiplication, converging to a stationary distribution. For LLM agents, local trust is computed from output-validation pairs: correct outputs increase trust, gate failures decrease it. Trust decay (multiplying by 0.95 per round) prevents stale high-trust scores from hiding recent degradation.
when_to_use: When agent outputs vary in quality over time, when the system includes agents from different providers or model versions, or when you need to dynamically weight agent contributions based on track record.
steps: Step 1: Initialize trust scores uniformly (1/n for n agents) and define the local trust update rule: t_ij += α on success, t_ij -= β on failure, where α < β to penalize failures more
Step 2: Normalize local trust values per agent: c_ij = max(t_ij, 0) / Σ_j max(t_ij, 0) to get a probability distribution over trust in other agents
Step 3: Compute global trust via power iteration: t^(k+1) = C^T × t^(k), iterating until ||t^(k+1) - t^(k)|| < ε
Step 4: Apply trust decay each round (t_i *= 0.95) to ensure recent performance matters more than historical performance
Step 5: Use trust scores to weight agent contributions in consensus, to determine task routing priority, and to trigger replacement when trust drops below 0.3
expected_outcome: Dynamic reliability scores for each agent that reflect recent performance, enabling the system to preferentially route tasks to high-trust agents and quarantine unreliable ones.

title: Flat vs. Hierarchical Agent Topologies: Selection Criteria
description: Flat topologies (all agents communicate peer-to-peer) scale poorly beyond ~5 agents because communication overhead grows O(n²), but they're more resilient — no single point of failure. Hierarchical topologies (tree of manager-worker relationships) scale to hundreds of agents with O(n log n) communication but the root manager becomes a bottleneck and single point of failure. Hybrid approaches use flat communication within small teams (3-5 agents) and hierarchical coordination between teams.
when_to_use: When designing a multi-agent system from scratch and deciding how agents should be organized, especially when the number of agents exceeds 5 or when tasks have natural hierarchical decomposition.
steps: Step 1: Count the agents and estimate communication frequency — if n ≤ 5 and tasks are collaborative (not decomposable), use flat topology
Step 2: If tasks have natural subtask hierarchy (e.g., research → analyze → write → edit), use hierarchical with one manager per decomposition level
Step 3: For >10 agents, group into teams of 3-5 by capability overlap and use flat intra-team, hierarchical inter-team communication
Step 4: Add redundancy to hierarchical systems by designating a deputy manager who receives all manager communications and can take over on failure
Step 5: Evaluate by measuring message count, end-to-end latency, and failure recovery time — switch topology if metrics degrade beyond thresholds
expected_outcome: An agent topology matched to the system's scale and task structure, with appropriate trade-offs between communication efficiency and fault tolerance.

title: Inter-Agent Tool Sharing and Delegation Protocols
description: Not all agents have access to the same tools — one agent might have web search, another has code execution, a third has database access. Tool delegation allows an agent to request another agent to execute a tool on its behalf. The critical protocol design question is trust boundary: should the requesting agent send the full tool input (risking prompt injection via tool parameters), or should the delegatee agent reformulate the tool call from a high-level description? The latter is safer but introduces interpretation error.
when_to_use: When agents have heterogeneous tool access and a task requires tools that the primary agent lacks, or when centralizing dangerous tools (e.g., code execution, API calls) to a single audited agent.
steps: Step 1: Create a tool registry mapping each tool to its owning agent, input schema, and trust level (public, restricted, privileged)
Step 2: Implement a REQUEST_TOOL performative: requesting agent sends tool_name, intent description, and parameters; owning agent validates and executes
Step 3: For restricted tools, the owning agent reformulates the tool call from the intent description rather than blindly executing provided parameters
Step 4: Return tool results wrapped in a result envelope: {status, output, execution_time, truncated} — never raw tool output, which may contain injection payloads
Step 5: Log all tool delegations with requester, owner, tool, parameters (sanitized), and result for audit and rate limiting
expected_outcome: Secure, auditable tool sharing across agents with appropriate trust boundaries, enabling specialized tool agents without giving every agent direct access to dangerous capabilities.

title: Multi-Agent Collaborative Planning with Plan Merging
description: In collaborative planning, multiple agents each generate partial plans from their perspective, and these must be merged into a coherent joint plan. The partial-order planning approach represents each agent's plan as a set of (action, precondition, effect) triples with ordering constraints. Merging detects conflicts (two actions with contradictory effects) and resolves them by adding ordering constraints or substituting actions. This outperforms centralized planning for tasks >20 steps because it parallelizes the search across agents.
when_to_use: When a complex task requires planning across multiple domains of expertise and no single agent has sufficient knowledge to generate a complete plan, or when planning time must be reduced through parallelization.
steps: Step 1: Distribute the goal specification to all planning agents, each with its domain-specific knowledge and action repertoire
Step 2: Each agent generates a partial plan as an ordered list of (action, preconditions, effects) addressing the goals within its expertise
Step 3: Collect partial plans and detect conflicts: actions with contradictory effects, resource contention, or violated preconditions
Step 4: Resolve conflicts by inserting ordering constraints (action A must precede action B), substituting equivalent actions, or requesting replanning from the conflicting agent
Step 5: Validate the merged plan by simulating execution: check that all preconditions are satisfied at each step and all goals are achieved by the final state
expected_outcome: A coherent joint plan that combines expertise from multiple specialized agents, with conflicts detected and resolved through principled constraint satisfaction.

title: Emergent Behavior Monitoring via Statistical Process Control
description: Multi-agent systems can exhibit emergent behaviors — patterns not designed into any individual agent — that range from beneficial (self-organization) to harmful (collusion, echo chambers, infinite loops). Statistical process control (SPC) detects emergence by monitoring aggregate metrics (message rate, consensus time, vocabulary diversity) and flagging when they deviate >2σ from historical baselines. The Western Electric rules (4 of 5 points on one side of mean, 2 of 3 points beyond 2σ, etc.) catch gradual drift that single-threshold alarms miss.
when_to_use: When running multi-agent systems in production and needing to detect unintended collective behaviors like echo chambers, coordinated hallucination, runaway message loops, or gradual quality degradation.
steps: Step 1: Define key aggregate metrics: messages per minute, unique vocabulary per round, average inter-agent agreement rate, task completion time distribution
Step 2: Establish baseline statistics (mean, σ) from a burn-in period of 50+ normal workflow executions
Step 3: Apply SPC rules in real-time: flag if any metric exceeds 3σ (alarm), or if Western Electric patterns appear (warning for investigation)
Step 4: For flagged events, capture the full agent interaction log from the preceding 5 minutes for human review

Step 5: Build a catalog of known emergent patterns (echo chamber: agreement > 0.95 for 10+ rounds; death spiral: message rate increasing exponentially; hallucination cascade: novel claim referenced by 3+ agents without external evidence)
expected_outcome: Early detection of unintended emergent behaviors in production multi-agent systems, with captured context for diagnosis and a growing pattern library for faster future detection.

title: Message-Passing Architectures: Point-to-Point vs. Pub/Sub vs. Broadcast
description: Point-to-point messaging (agent A sends directly to agent B) is simplest but requires sender to know recipient. Publish-subscribe (agents publish to topics, others subscribe) decouples senders from receivers — critical when agent composition changes at runtime. Broadcast (every agent receives every message) ensures no information loss but scales terribly beyond ~8 agents (each agent processes O(n) messages per round). For LLM agents, pub/sub with topic-based filtering is the sweet spot: agents only receive messages in topics relevant to their role, keeping context windows focused.
when_to_use: When designing the communication substrate for a multi-agent system, especially when agents are added or removed at runtime or when agents have varying information needs.
steps: Step 1: Assess communication patterns: if agents have fixed, known communication partners, use point-to-point; if communication partners change dynamically, use pub/sub
Step 2: For pub/sub, define topics aligned with workflow stages or data types (e.g., 'research-findings', 'code-reviews', 'deployment-status') — not agent names
Step 3: Implement subscription filters so agents only receive messages matching their role: a security agent subscribes to 'code-reviews' but not 'deployment-status'
Step 4: Add message ordering guarantees within each topic (FIFO) but allow out-of-order across topics for parallelism
Step 5: Implement dead-letter queues for messages no agent subscribes to — these indicate misconfigured topics or missing agent roles
expected_outcome: A communication architecture that scales with agent count, keeps agent context windows focused, and supports dynamic agent composition without rewiring.

title: Reflection Loops: Self-Correction Through Internal Critique Agents
description: A reflection loop adds an internal critic agent that reviews the primary agent's output before it's finalized. The critic evaluates against task requirements, logical consistency, and common failure modes. Madaan et al. (2023) show that self-refinement (same model critiques its own output) improves output quality by 10-25% on reasoning tasks, but plateaus after 2 iterations and can degrade after 4+ iterations as the model over-corrects. Using a different model for the critic avoids self-consistency bias.
when_to_use: When agent outputs require high reliability, when tasks involve reasoning or analysis where first-draft outputs typically contain correctable errors, or when single-pass generation is insufficient quality.
steps: Step 1: Generate initial output from the primary agent with its standard prompt
Step 2: Pass the output to a critic agent with a specialized prompt: 'Identify specific errors, unsupported claims, logical gaps, and deviations from the task requirements'
Step 3: If the critic identifies issues, send the output + critique back to the primary agent (or a refinement agent) with instruction to address each identified issue
Step 4: Cap iterations at 2-3 — track improvement delta between iterations and stop when delta < 5% or quality score stops increasing
Step 5: Log each iteration's output and critique for later analysis of which error types the critic catches most reliably
expected_outcome: Higher-quality agent outputs through structured self-correction, with bounded iteration to prevent over-refinement and tracked improvement metrics.

title: Contract Net Protocol for Dynamic Task Allocation
description: The Contract Net Protocol (CNP) allocates tasks through a market-like mechanism: a manager agent broadcasts a task announcement, worker agents evaluate and bid based on their capability and current load, and the manager awards the contract to the best bidder. This outperforms static assignment when agent capabilities overlap and workload is unpredictable. The bid should include: estimated completion time, confidence in ability to complete, current utilization, and any required resources. CNP naturally load-balances because busy agents bid lower.
when_to_use: When tasks arrive dynamically, multiple agents could handle each task, and optimal assignment depends on current agent state (load, context, recent performance) rather than static role mapping.
steps: Step 1: Manager broadcasts task announcement with requirements: input data, expected output format, deadline, required tools/capabilities
Step 2: Eligible worker agents evaluate the task against their capabilities and current load, generating bids: {capability_match: 0-1, estimated_time, current_utilization, confidence}
Step 3: Manager collects bids within a timeout window and selects winner using a scoring function: score = w1*capability + w2*(1-utilization) + w3*confidence
Step 4: Manager sends AWARD to winner and REJECT to others; winner acknowledges and begins execution within a commit timeout
Step 5: If winner fails to acknowledge or deliver by deadline, manager re-announces the task to remaining bidders (excluding the failed agent)
expected_outcome: Dynamic, capability-aware task allocation that naturally adapts to changing agent loads and produces better task-agent matching than static assignment.

title: Context Window Management in Multi-Agent Conversations
description: LLM agents in multi-agent conversations face context window pressure: with 5 agents exchanging 10 messages each, the conversation consumes ~50K tokens before any agent does work. Strategies to manage this: summarization checkpoints (compress every N messages into a summary), selective context (each agent only sees messages tagged for its role), and sliding window (keep last K messages + a persistent summary of earlier ones). The summarize-and-forget approach loses ~15% of details but extends effective conversation length by 4x.
when_to_use: When multi-agent conversations exceed 20 messages, when agents are hitting context window limits, or when conversation quality degrades as the interaction length grows.
steps: Step 1: Monitor token count per agent's context — trigger management when any agent exceeds 60% of its context window capacity

Step 2: Implement summarization checkpoints: every 10 messages, a dedicated summarizer agent compresses the conversation into a structured summary preserving key decisions, open questions, and assigned tasks
Step 3: Apply selective routing: tag each message with relevant roles, and only inject messages into agents whose roles match the tags
Step 4: Maintain a 'persistent memory' section in each agent's prompt with: original task, current role assignment, key decisions made, and pending actions — this survives summarization

Step 5: When an agent's context is refreshed (summary replaces history), inject a continuity prompt: 'You are continuing a conversation. Here is the summary of prior discussion and your current objectives'
expected_outcome: Multi-agent conversations that maintain coherence over long interactions without hitting context limits, with controlled information loss through structured summarization.

title: Horizontal Scaling with Stateless Agent Instances
description: To horizontally scale multi-agent systems, agents must be stateless — all state lives in external stores (databases, caches, message queues). A stateless agent can be replicated across N instances with a load balancer in front. The state externalization pattern stores conversation history in Redis, task state in PostgreSQL, and pending messages in a queue (RabbitMQ/SQS). This enables auto-scaling: spin up agents when queue depth > threshold, scale down when idle. The main pitfall is consistency — two instances processing the same conversation must coordinate via distributed locks.
when_to_use: When a multi-agent system must handle variable throughput (e.g., 10x traffic spikes), when individual agent instances fail and must be replaced without state loss, or when deploying agents across multiple servers.
steps: Step 1: Identify all state an agent holds: conversation history, task progress, tool session data, accumulated context — externalize each to a named store
Step 2: Use Redis for hot state (current conversation, active task context) with TTL-based expiration, and PostgreSQL for durable state (completed tasks, audit logs)
Step 3: Implement a message queue between agent tiers — upstream agents enqueue tasks, downstream agents dequeue and process, decoupling production rate from consumption rate
Step 4: Add distributed locking (Redis SETNX or PostgreSQL advisory locks) to prevent two agent instances from processing the same task simultaneously
Step 5: Configure auto-scaling rules: scale up when queue depth > 10 or p95 latency > 5s; scale down when queue is empty for > 2 minutes and instance count > minimum
expected_outcome: A multi-agent system that scales horizontally with demand, tolerates instance failures without state loss, and auto-adjusts capacity based on workload.

title: Multi-Agent Evaluation via Trajectory Analysis
description: Evaluating a multi-agent system requires analyzing the full interaction trajectory, not just the final output.
