Key metrics: communication efficiency (tokens exchanged per task), coordination overhead (fraction of messages that are coordination vs. substance), convergence rate (rounds until agents align), and value-added per agent (how much does removing each agent degrade output quality). Ablation studies — removing one agent at a time and measuring quality delta — reveal which agents are load-bearing vs. redundant.
when_to_use: When assessing whether a multi-agent system is working efficiently, when deciding whether to add or remove agents, or when debugging why a multi-agent system underperforms a single agent.
steps: Step 1: Record full interaction trajectories: every message, tool call, and state change with timestamps and token counts
Step 2: Compute communication efficiency: total tokens exchanged / task complexity score — compare against single-agent baseline to quantify coordination overhead
Step 3: Run ablation experiments: remove each agent one at a time, run the same tasks, and measure quality delta — agents with delta < 2% are candidates for removal
Step 4: Analyze convergence patterns: plot inter-agent agreement over rounds — healthy systems converge monotonically; oscillating agreement indicates a conflict resolution failure
Step 5: Compute per-agent ROI: quality improvement attributed to agent / cost of running agent (tokens, latency) — agents with ROI < 1.0 cost more than they contribute
expected_outcome: Quantitative understanding of multi-agent system efficiency, identification of redundant or underperforming agents, and data-driven decisions about system composition.

title: Mutex and Semaphore Patterns for Shared Resource Access
description: When multiple agents share resources (a database, an API with rate limits, a file system), uncontrolled concurrent access causes conflicts. A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) ensures only one agent accesses the resource at a time — essential for write operations. A semaphore allows N concurrent readers but exclusive writers (reader-writer lock pattern). For LLM agent systems, token-bucket rate limiting on shared API calls is more practical than strict mutexes: each agent draws from a shared token bucket at the API's rate limit, preventing 429 errors.
when_to_use: When multiple agents make concurrent API calls to rate-limited services, write to the same database tables, or access any shared resource where concurrent access causes errors or corruption.
steps: Step 1: Inventory shared resources and classify access patterns: read-only (safe to parallelize), read-write (needs coordination), write-only (needs mutual exclusion)
Step 2: For rate-limited APIs, implement a token bucket shared across all agents: bucket refills at the API's rate limit, agents acquire tokens before calling
Step 3: For shared databases, use reader-writer locks: multiple agents can read simultaneously, but writes acquire exclusive access and block readers
Step 4: Implement lock timeouts (max 30 seconds) to prevent deadlocks — if an agent holds a lock beyond timeout, force-release it and mark the agent's operation as failed
Step 5: Monitor lock contention metrics: average wait time, timeout frequency, and utilization — high contention indicates the resource needs partitioning or the agent pool needs restructuring
expected_outcome: Conflict-free concurrent access to shared resources with bounded wait times, preventing rate limit errors and data corruption in multi-agent systems.

title: Competitive Multi-Agent Planning for Adversarial Robustness
description: In competitive planning, agents generate plans against adversarial opponents (e.g., red-team/blue-team security testing). Game-theoretic approaches model this as a two-player game where each agent maximizes its own utility. Minimax planning finds the strategy that minimizes the maximum loss. For LLM-based red-teaming, the red agent generates attack prompts while the blue agent patches defenses. Nash equilibrium in this setting means neither agent can improve by changing strategy unilaterally — reaching this state typically requires 5-8 adversarial rounds.
when_to_use: When testing system robustness against adversarial inputs, performing red-team/blue-team security analysis, or generating stress tests where an attacker agent actively tries to break a defender agent.
steps: Step 1: Define the competitive objective clearly: red agent maximizes successful attacks (bypasses, jailbreaks, edge cases); blue agent minimizes attack surface
Step 2: Red agent generates an initial batch of attack strategies, prioritized by estimated impact and likelihood of success
Step 3: Blue agent analyzes each attack, implements defenses (prompt hardening, input validation, output filtering), and reports residual vulnerabilities
Step 4: Red agent adapts strategies to bypass new defenses — this adversarial loop continues for 5-8 rounds or until attack success rate stabilizes below threshold
Step 5: Document the final defense posture, all discovered attack vectors (including patched ones), and the convergence trajectory showing diminishing attack success
expected_outcome: A hardened system that has been adversarially tested through iterative attack-defense cycles, with documented attack vectors and defense strategies.

title: Gossip Protocols for Decentralized Knowledge Propagation
description: Gossip (epidemic) protocols propagate information in decentralized agent networks without a central coordinator. Each agent periodically shares its knowledge with a randomly selected peer, who merges the new information with its own and propagates further. Information reaches all N agents in O(log N) rounds with high probability. Unlike broadcast, gossip is fault-tolerant — even if 30% of agents are temporarily unreachable, information eventually propagates. The trade-off is redundant message delivery: each piece of information is received ~ln(N) times on average.
when_to_use: When agents operate in a decentralized network without a central coordinator, when the network is unreliable with frequent agent disconnections, or when broadcast is too expensive for the network size.
steps: Step 1: Each agent maintains a local knowledge store with version vectors — each fact has a (source_agent, sequence_number) pair for tracking freshness
Step 2: Every T seconds, each agent selects a random peer and sends a digest (list of fact IDs and versions it holds, not the full facts)
Step 3: The receiving peer compares digests, identifies facts it's missing or has newer versions of, and requests/sends the delta
Step 4: Implement 'push-pull' gossip for faster convergence: both agents exchange deltas simultaneously, halving convergence time vs. push-only
Step 5: Use rumor mongering with feedback: an agent stops propagating a fact after it's been shared with k peers who already have it (k=3 is typical), preventing endless redundant transmission
expected_outcome: Decentralized, fault-tolerant knowledge propagation across an agent network with O(log N) convergence and bounded redundancy.

title: Parallel Execution with Fan-Out/Fan-In Aggregation
description: Fan-out/fan-in is the most common parallelization pattern in multi-agent systems: a coordinator splits a task into N independent subtasks (fan-out), dispatches each to a separate agent, collects all results, and merges them (fan-in). The aggregation step is where complexity lives — simple concatenation works for information gathering, but analytical tasks require reconciling potentially contradictory agent outputs. Stragglers (slow agents) are the main latency risk: the fan-in step blocks until the slowest agent completes. Mitigate with timeouts and partial aggregation.
when_to_use: When a task can be decomposed into independent subtasks that don't depend on each other's results, such as parallel web searches, multi-document analysis, or generating diverse candidate solutions.
steps: Step 1: Decompose the task into N independent subtasks with clear input/output schemas — verify independence by checking that no subtask references another's output
Step 2: Dispatch all subtasks simultaneously with per-subtask timeouts (2x expected completion time) and unique correlation IDs for tracking
Step 3: As results arrive, validate each against the expected output schema — reject and optionally retry malformed results
Step 4: Implement partial aggregation with a quorum: proceed with fan-in when M of N results are available (e.g., 4 of 5), marking missing results as 'unavailable'
Step 5: Apply domain-appropriate aggregation: concatenation for information gathering, voting for classification, synthesis for analysis, best-of-N for generation
expected_outcome: Parallel task execution with latency equal to the slowest required subtask (not the slowest overall), with graceful handling of stragglers and failed subtasks.

title: Agent Memory Architecture: Short-Term, Long-Term, and Episodic
description: Multi-agent systems benefit from three memory tiers inspired by cognitive science. Short-term memory (STM) is the current context window — limited but high-fidelity. Long-term memory (LTM) is a vector store or database persisting across sessions — unlimited but lossy retrieval. Episodic memory records specific interaction trajectories for later recall — useful for 'last time we handled X, we did Y.' MemGPT (Packer et al., 2023) demonstrated that explicit memory management (agents deciding what to write to LTM and when to query it) outperforms passive context stuffing by 30% on long-horizon tasks.
when_to_use: When agents need to reference information from previous conversations, when tasks span multiple sessions, or when the system must learn from past interaction patterns to improve over time.
steps: Step 1: Implement STM as the agent's context window — keep only the current task, last 5 messages, and the most relevant LTM retrievals
Step 2: Implement LTM as a vector store (e.g., Pinecone, ChromaDB) with explicit write triggers: agent calls 'save_to_memory(fact, importance_score)' when encountering information worth preserving
Step 3: Implement episodic memory as a structured log of (task, approach, outcome, lessons_learned) tuples — searchable by task similarity
Step 4: Add memory retrieval to the agent's tool set — before starting a task, automatically query LTM and episodic memory for relevant past experience
Step 5: Implement memory consolidation: periodically review LTM entries, merge duplicates, update stale facts, and decay importance scores of unused memories
expected_outcome: Agents that effectively leverage past experience across sessions, with explicit memory management that prevents context window overflow while retaining critical knowledge.

title: Deadlock Detection and Resolution in Agent Dependencies
description: Deadlock occurs when agents are cyclically waiting for each other: Agent A waits for B's output, B waits for C's, C waits for A's. In multi-agent systems, deadlocks are harder to detect than in traditional concurrent systems because the 'waiting' is implicit — an agent simply doesn't produce output because it's waiting for input that never arrives. Detection requires building a wait-for graph from pending requests and checking for cycles. Resolution: break the cycle by having one agent proceed with a default or partial result.
when_to_use: When a multi-agent workflow stalls without error messages, when agents depend on each other's outputs and the dependency structure might contain cycles, or when designing dependency-safe workflows.
steps: Step 1: Build a wait-for graph: for each agent, track which other agents it has pending requests to — edge A→B means A is waiting for B's output
Step 2: Run cycle detection (DFS with gray/black coloring) on the wait-for graph every T seconds or whenever a new dependency is registered
Step 3: When a cycle is detected, identify the lowest-priority agent in the cycle and force it to proceed with a default/empty result, breaking the cycle
Step 4: Notify all agents in the cycle that a deadlock was resolved, including which agent produced a default result — downstream agents should treat this input as low-confidence
Step 5: Log deadlock occurrences and analyze patterns — recurring deadlocks at the same dependency edges indicate a design flaw that should be fixed by restructuring the workflow
expected_outcome: Automatic detection and resolution of deadlocked agent dependencies, preventing indefinite stalls while preserving information about where the resolution may have degraded output quality.

title: Multi-Agent Debugging via Interaction Replay and Counterfactual Analysis
description: Debugging multi-agent systems is harder than single-agent because the bug may emerge from the interaction pattern, not from any individual agent. Interaction replay records the full message sequence and allows re-executing the workflow from any point with modified inputs. Counterfactual analysis asks 'what if Agent B had given a different response at step 5?' by forking the replay at a specific message and substituting an alternative. This isolates whether a bad outcome was caused by a specific agent's response or by the overall interaction dynamic.
when_to_use: When a multi-agent system produces an incorrect or unexpected result and the cause isn't obvious from individual agent outputs, or when trying to understand how a specific inter-agent exchange influenced the final outcome.
steps: Step 1: Record the full interaction trace with deterministic IDs: every message, tool call, and agent internal state at each step
Step 2: Identify the point where the output first diverges from expected — compare intermediate outputs at each step against known-good baselines
Step 3: Fork the replay at the divergence point: substitute Agent X's actual response with a corrected response and re-execute the remaining workflow
Step 4: Compare original and counterfactual outcomes — if the counterfactual produces the correct result, Agent X at that step is the root cause
Step 5: If the counterfactual doesn't fix the outcome, move the fork point earlier and test other agents — repeat until the root cause is isolated
expected_outcome: Identified root cause of multi-agent failures through systematic interaction replay, with counterfactual evidence showing exactly which agent response led to the incorrect outcome.

title: Capability-Based Access Control for Agent Permissions
description: In multi-agent systems, unrestricted agent permissions create security risks — a compromised or hallucinating agent with write access to production databases can cause real damage. Capability-based access control (CBAC) grants agents specific, revocable capability tokens for each resource. Unlike role-based access control (RBAC), capabilities are fine-grained and task-scoped: an agent gets 'write to table X for task Y, expiring in 10 minutes' rather than a permanent 'database writer' role. This follows the principle of least privilege and limits blast radius from agent failures.
when_to_use: When agents interact with external systems (databases, APIs, file systems) and failures or adversarial behavior could cause real-world damage, or when regulatory requirements mandate access auditing.
steps: Step 1: Define a capability token schema: {resource, action (read/write/execute), scope (specific table/endpoint), task_id, expiration, issuer}
Step 2: Issue capabilities just-in-time: when a manager assigns a task requiring database writes, it issues a time-limited write capability for only the relevant tables
Step 3: Enforce capabilities at the resource gateway: every tool call must present a valid, non-expired capability token matching the requested action
Step 4: Implement capability revocation: when a task completes or an agent is flagged for anomalous behavior, immediately revoke all its active capabilities
Step 5: Log all capability grants, exercises, and revocations for audit — review logs to identify agents that consistently request capabilities they don't use (possible reconnaissance)
expected_outcome: Fine-grained, auditable access control that limits each agent's permissions to exactly what its current task requires, minimizing damage from agent failures or compromises.

title: Mixture-of-Agents for Quality-Weighted Output Generation
description: Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) uses a layered architecture where multiple LLM agents generate candidate responses in the first layer, and an aggregator agent in the second layer synthesizes the best elements from all candidates. Wang et al. (2024) showed that MoA with 3 proposer agents + 1 aggregator consistently outperforms any single agent, achieving AlpacaEval scores competitive with GPT-4 using only open-source models. The aggregator prompt is critical: it must explicitly reference and compare specific elements from each candidate, not just 'combine the best parts.'
when_to_use: When output quality is more important than latency or cost, when different models excel at different aspects of the task, or when generating important deliverables (reports, analyses) that justify multi-agent overhead.
steps: Step 1: Deploy 3-5 proposer agents with diverse characteristics: different models, different temperatures (0.3-0.9), or different prompting strategies (CoT, few-shot, direct)
Step 2: Have all proposers generate independent responses to the same prompt — do not let them see each other's outputs
Step 3: Feed all candidate responses to the aggregator agent with a structured prompt: 'For each aspect of the task, identify which candidate handled it best and why'
Step 4: The aggregator synthesizes a final response by selecting and integrating the strongest elements, resolving contradictions between candidates
Step 5: Optionally add a second aggregation layer: 2-3 aggregators produce synthesis attempts, and a final aggregator selects or merges the best synthesis
expected_outcome: Output quality exceeding any individual agent by systematically combining the strengths of diverse candidates, at the cost of 3-5x token usage.

title: Circuit Breaker Pattern for Agent Failure Isolation
description: The circuit breaker pattern, adapted from microservices architecture, prevents cascading failures in multi-agent systems by monitoring agent error rates and temporarily removing failing agents from the workflow. Three states: CLOSED (normal operation, tracking failures), OPEN (agent is bypassed, requests routed to fallback), HALF-OPEN (limited traffic sent to test recovery). Without circuit breakers, a failing downstream agent causes upstream agents to retry indefinitely, consuming tokens and creating backpressure throughout the system.
when_to_use: When multi-agent systems must remain operational even when individual agents experience temporary failures (API timeouts, rate limits, model degradation), especially in production systems with uptime requirements.
steps: Step 1: Wrap each agent in a circuit breaker proxy that tracks: success count, failure count, last failure timestamp, current state (CLOSED/OPEN/HALF-OPEN)
Step 2: Define failure thresholds: CLOSED→OPEN when failure rate exceeds 50% over the last 10 requests or 3 consecutive failures occur
Step 3: In OPEN state, immediately route requests to a fallback (simpler model, cached response, or human escalation) without calling the failing agent
Step 4: After a cooldown period (30-60 seconds), transition to HALF-OPEN: send one test request to the agent — if it succeeds, return to CLOSED; if it fails, return to OPEN with doubled cooldown
Step 5: Emit circuit breaker state change events for monitoring dashboards — track mean time to recovery and correlate with external factors (API outages, rate limit windows)
expected_outcome: Fault-tolerant multi-agent systems that gracefully degrade when individual agents fail, with automatic recovery testing and bounded retry costs.

title: NDCG@K for Graded Relevance Ranking Evaluation
description: NDCG@K measures ranking quality by comparing predicted ordering to ideal: DCG@K = Σ(2^rel_i - 1)/log2(i+1) for positions i=1..K, normalized by dividing by ideal DCG (the DCG of a perfect ranking). Unlike MAP, NDCG handles graded relevance (not just binary). Critical pitfall: NDCG is undefined when ideal DCG is 0 (no relevant docs in the ground truth for that query) — convention is to assign 0 or exclude the query, but this choice materially affects averages on sparse datasets.
when_to_use: When evaluating ranked lists where relevance is graded (e.g., 0-3 scale), comparing retrieval or recommendation systems, or when binary precision/recall metrics lose too much information about ranking quality.
steps: Step 1: Compute DCG@K for the system's ranking — for each position i from 1 to K, accumulate (2^rel_i - 1) / log2(i+1)
Step 2: Compute ideal DCG@K by sorting all ground-truth relevance labels in descending order and applying the same formula
Step 3: Divide system DCG by ideal DCG to get NDCG@K; handle queries with no relevant documents explicitly (assign 0 or exclude, document the choice)
Step 4: Average NDCG@K across queries; report standard error to quantify variance across queries
Step 5: Compare NDCG at multiple K values (e.g., @5, @10, @100) to understand whether the system excels at top-heavy or deep ranking
expected_outcome: A normalized score in [0,1] per query measuring ranking quality with graded relevance, averaged across queries with documented handling of edge cases.

title: Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) for First-Hit Evaluation
description: MRR = (1/|Q|) Σ 1/rank_i where rank_i is the position of the first relevant result for query i. MRR is ideal when users care only about the first correct answer (e.g., question answering, navigational search). Key limitation: MRR ignores all relevant documents beyond the first — if a system returns 10 relevant docs but the first appears at position 5, MRR sees only 1/5 = 0.2 regardless of the other 9.
when_to_use: When the task involves finding a single correct answer (factoid QA, entity lookup, navigational queries) and the position of the first relevant result is what matters, not recall of all relevant items.
steps: Step 1: For each query, find the rank position of the first relevant document in the returned list
Step 2: Compute the reciprocal 1/rank for each query; if no relevant document is returned, assign 0
Step 3: Average reciprocal ranks across all queries to get MRR
Step 4: Report alongside recall@K to ensure the system isn't sacrificing coverage for top-1 accuracy
expected_outcome: A single score in (0,1] summarizing how quickly the system surfaces the first relevant result, with complementary recall metrics for coverage.

title: Mean Average Precision (MAP) for Binary Relevance Rankings
description: MAP = (1/|Q|) Σ AP(q) where AP(q) = (1/|R_q|) Σ Precision@k × rel(k), summing over all positions k where a relevant document appears, and |R_q| is the total number of relevant documents for query q. MAP rewards systems that rank relevant documents higher and penalizes those that scatter them across the list. Unlike NDCG, MAP assumes binary relevance — a document is either relevant or not. Critical: if a relevant document is never retrieved, it contributes 0 to the AP sum but still counts in |R_q|, lowering the score.
when_to_use: When evaluating retrieval systems with binary relevance judgments and you need a single metric that captures both precision and recall across the full ranking, especially in document retrieval or passage retrieval tasks.
steps: Step 1: For each query, walk through the ranked results and compute Precision@k at every position k where a relevant document appears
Step 2: Sum these precision values and divide by the total number of relevant documents for that query (including unretrieved ones) to get Average Precision
Step 3: Average AP across all queries to get MAP
Step 4: Segment MAP by query difficulty (queries with few vs many relevant docs) to diagnose whether the system struggles with recall-heavy queries
Step 5: Compare with NDCG to determine if graded relevance would change conclusions
expected_outcome: A single score in [0,1] capturing precision-recall tradeoff across the full ranking for binary relevance, with per-query AP distribution for variance analysis.

title: LLM-as-Judge Single-Point Scoring
description: Using an LLM to score outputs on a Likert scale (e.g., 1-5) by providing a rubric, the output to evaluate, and optionally a reference answer. Single-point scoring is simpler than pairwise comparison but suffers from position bias, verbosity bias (longer responses get higher scores), and self-enhancement bias (models prefer their own outputs). Empirically, GPT-4 as judge agrees with human preferences ~80% of the time on MT-Bench, but agreement drops below 60% on creative/subjective tasks.
when_to_use: When evaluating open-ended LLM outputs at scale where human annotation is too expensive, and you need absolute scores rather than relative rankings between systems.
steps: Step 1: Design a detailed rubric with concrete criteria for each score level — vague rubrics like 'good quality' produce noisy scores
Step 2: Include 2-3 calibration examples in the prompt showing outputs at different score levels with explanations
Step 3: Request chain-of-thought reasoning before the final score to reduce random scoring
Step 4: Run each evaluation 3-5 times and take the modal score to reduce variance; flag items where scores span >2 points as unreliable
Step 5: Validate on a human-scored subset (≥50 examples) computing Cohen's kappa between LLM and human scores
expected_outcome: Scaled evaluation scores for LLM outputs with documented rubric, inter-trial consistency metrics, and human validation statistics.

title: LLM-as-Judge Pairwise Comparison Protocol
description: Pairwise comparison asks an LLM judge to choose which of two outputs is better, avoiding the calibration problems of absolute scoring. Position bias is the dominant failure mode — models prefer the response presented first (or second, depending on the model). The standard mitigation is to evaluate each pair twice with swapped positions; if the judge disagrees with itself, mark the pair as a tie. Chatbot Arena uses this approach with Elo ratings derived from thousands of pairwise comparisons.
when_to_use: When comparing two LLM systems head-to-head, building Elo-style leaderboards, or when absolute scoring produces too much noise due to subjective evaluation criteria.
steps: Step 1: Format each comparison with both outputs labeled neutrally (Response A / Response B), never revealing which system produced which
Step 2: Run each pair twice with A/B positions swapped to detect and cancel position bias
Step 3: Score outcomes: consistent win = 1 point, position-inconsistent = 0.5 (tie), consistent loss = 0 points
Step 4: Aggregate pairwise results into a ranking using Bradley-Terry model or Elo rating system
Step 5: Compute bootstrap confidence intervals on the win rate by resampling the comparison set with replacement (≥1000 resamples)
expected_outcome: Head-to-head win rates with position-bias correction and confidence intervals, optionally converted to Elo ratings for multi-system comparison.

title: Reference-Guided LLM Evaluation
description: A variant of LLM-as-judge where the model is given a gold-standard reference answer and scores the candidate output based on factual alignment with the reference. This dramatically improves judge reliability for factual tasks (accuracy jumps from ~65% to ~85% agreement with humans on factual QA). The reference acts as an anchor, reducing the judge's reliance on its own knowledge (which may be wrong). Critical failure mode: the judge may penalize correct answers that diverge from the reference in phrasing or approach.
when_to_use: When evaluating factual accuracy of LLM outputs and gold-standard references exist, especially for QA, summarization with key facts, or knowledge-intensive tasks where the judge's parametric knowledge is unreliable.
steps: Step 1: Prepare reference answers that cover all acceptable correct responses, not just one canonical form
Step 2: Instruct the judge to evaluate factual alignment, explicitly telling it that different phrasing of the same facts is acceptable
Step 3: Use a structured output format: list each claim in the candidate, mark it as supported/contradicted/not-covered by reference
Step 4: Compute a factual precision score (supported claims / total claims) and recall score (covered reference facts / total reference facts)
Step 5: Validate on edge cases where the candidate is correct but uses different terminology than the reference
expected_outcome: Factual alignment scores decomposed into precision and recall against reference, with explicit handling of paraphrase and alternative-but-correct answers.

title: Inter-Annotator Agreement with Cohen's Kappa
description: Cohen's kappa κ = (p_o - p_e) / (1 - p_e) measures agreement between two raters adjusted for chance, where p_o is observed agreement and p_e is expected agreement by chance. κ < 0.2 is poor, 0.2-0.4 fair, 0.4-0.6 moderate, 0.6-0.8 substantial, >0.8 near-perfect. Critical limitation: κ is undefined when one rater uses only one category, and it can be paradoxically low when prevalence is extreme (high agreement but low κ because chance agreement is also high). Cohen's κ only works for exactly 2 raters.
when_to_use: When measuring reliability of human evaluation with exactly two annotators on categorical judgments, or validating whether an annotation scheme produces consistent labels before using the annotations for model evaluation.
steps: Step 1: Build the confusion matrix between the two raters' labels across all items
Step 2: Compute observed agreement p_o = sum of diagonal / total
Step 3: Compute expected chance agreement p_e = sum of (row_marginal × col_marginal) / total² for each category
Step 4: Calculate κ = (p_o - p_e) / (1 - p_e) and interpret using Landis-Koch scale
Step 5: If κ < 0.4, examine the confusion matrix to identify which categories cause disagreement and revise annotation guidelines before proceeding
expected_outcome: A chance-corrected agreement score with interpretation, and if agreement is low, specific identification of problematic categories driving disagreement.

title: Krippendorff's Alpha for Multi-Rater Reliability
description: Krippendorff's alpha generalizes agreement metrics to any number of raters, missing data, and multiple scale types (nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio). α = 1 - D_o/D_e where D_o is observed disagreement and D_e is expected disagreement by chance. Unlike Fleiss' kappa, alpha handles missing data naturally (raters don't need to label every item) and ordinal scales (a 1-point disagreement is penalized less than a 3-point disagreement). Threshold: α > 0.667 is tentatively acceptable, α > 0.8 is reliable.
when_to_use: When you have 3+ annotators, some items are not labeled by all raters, or you need agreement metrics for ordinal/interval scales (e.g., Likert ratings), which Cohen's kappa cannot handle properly.
steps: Step 1: Choose the appropriate distance metric for your scale type: nominal (0/1 mismatch), ordinal (rank-based squared distance), interval (squared difference), ratio (squared log-ratio)
Step 2: Compute observed disagreement D_o by averaging the distance between all rater pairs for each item, then averaging across items
Step 3: Compute expected disagreement D_e from the overall distribution of values across all raters and items
Step 4: Calculate α = 1 - D_o/D_e; report the bootstrap 95% CI by resampling items (not raters)
Step 5: If α < 0.667, identify specific items with maximal disagreement for annotation guideline revision
expected_outcome: A single reliability coefficient valid across rater counts and scale types, with confidence interval and diagnosis of disagreement sources if reliability is insufficient.

title: Benchmark Contamination Detection via N-gram Overlap
description: Training data contamination occurs when test examples (or near-duplicates) appear in the training set, inflating benchmark scores. Detection approaches include exact match deduplication, n-gram overlap analysis (typically 8-gram or 13-gram overlap thresholds), and embedding similarity search. GPT-4's technical report found 25% of the BoolQ benchmark was contaminated at the 13-gram level. Even partial contamination (similar but not identical examples) can inflate scores by 2-10% on multiple-choice benchmarks.
when_to_use: When reporting benchmark results for a model trained on large web corpora, validating that evaluation results reflect genuine capability rather than memorization, or designing new benchmarks that must resist contamination.
steps: Step 1: Extract all n-grams (n=8 and n=13) from each test example after lowercasing and removing punctuation
Step 2: Check each test example's n-gram set against the training corpus; flag examples where >70% of 8-grams appear in a single training document
Step 3: Compute benchmark scores separately for clean and contaminated subsets — a gap >5% strongly suggests memorization
Step 4: Apply the 'canary string' approach for new benchmarks: embed unique synthetic strings that can be detected if they leak into training data
Step 5: Report both contaminated and clean scores; if clean subset is too small (<100 examples), the benchmark may be unreliable for this model
expected_outcome: Contamination rate per benchmark with split clean/contaminated performance scores, enabling accurate assessment of genuine model capability.

title: Expected Calibration Error (ECE) Measurement
description: ECE measures how well a model's predicted probabilities match actual outcomes: ECE = Σ (|B_m|/n) × |accuracy(B_m) - confidence(B_m)| where B_m are bins of predictions grouped by confidence level. A perfectly calibrated model with 70% confidence should be correct 70% of the time. Standard binning uses 10-15 equal-width bins, but this is sensitive to bin count. Adaptive binning (equal-mass bins) is more robust. LLMs are typically overconfident — after RLHF, calibration degrades significantly compared to the base model.
when_to_use: When assessing whether a model's confidence scores are trustworthy for downstream decision-making, comparing calibration across models, or deciding whether to apply post-hoc calibration (temperature scaling).
steps: Step 1: Extract predicted class probabilities and true labels for the evaluation set (minimum ~1000 examples for stable estimates)
Step 2: Bin predictions into 15 equal-width bins by confidence level; also compute with adaptive (equal-mass) binning for comparison
Step 3: For each bin, compute the absolute gap between average confidence and actual accuracy, weighted by bin size
Step 4: Plot a reliability diagram (confidence vs accuracy per bin) with a diagonal reference line — points above the diagonal indicate underconfidence, below indicate overconfidence
Step 5: Report both ECE and maximum calibration error (MCE = max bin gap) to catch pathological miscalibration in any confidence range
expected_outcome: ECE and MCE scores with reliability diagram, clearly showing where the model is over/underconfident and by how much.

title: Temperature Scaling for Post-Hoc Calibration
description: Temperature scaling divides logits by a scalar T > 0 before softmax: p_i = exp(z_i/T) / Σ exp(z_j/T). T > 1 softens probabilities (reduces overconfidence), T < 1 sharpens them. Unlike Platt scaling (which fits a full logistic regression per class), temperature scaling uses a single parameter, making it far less prone to overfitting on small calibration sets. It preserves the ranking of predictions (accuracy unchanged) while improving calibration. Optimal T is found by minimizing negative log-likelihood on a held-out calibration set via grid search or L-BFGS.
when_to_use: When a model's accuracy is acceptable but its confidence scores are miscalibrated (high ECE), and you need reliable probabilities for downstream decisions without retraining the model.
steps: Step 1: Hold out a calibration set (1000-5000 examples) separate from both training and test data
Step 2: Extract pre-softmax logits from the model for all calibration examples
Step 3: Optimize T by minimizing NLL on the calibration set — use scipy.optimize or grid search over T ∈ [0.1, 10.0]
Step 4: Apply the learned T to test set logits and recompute ECE to verify calibration improvement

Step 5: Compare ECE before and after; typical improvement is 3-10× reduction in ECE. If improvement is minimal, the model may have deeper calibration issues requiring Platt scaling or isotonic regression
expected_outcome: A single temperature parameter that reduces ECE without changing accuracy, with before/after calibration comparison on the test set.

title: A/B Testing Statistical Power for ML Systems
description: Statistical power is the probability of detecting a true effect — power = P(reject H0 | H1 is true). For ML A/B tests, the required sample size is n = (z_{α/2} + z_β)² × 2σ² / δ² where δ is the minimum detectable effect and σ² is the metric variance. ML metrics often have high variance (e.g., per-query NDCG has σ ≈ 0.3), requiring thousands of samples. Critical pitfall: ML systems have temporal correlations (user behavior shifts over time), violating the i.i.d. assumption — use time-based blocking or CUPED variance reduction.
when_to_use: When designing online experiments to compare ML models, determining how long to run an A/B test, or when an A/B test returned a non-significant result and you need to determine if the test was underpowered.
steps: Step 1: Estimate the metric variance σ² from historical data or an A/A test (where both groups get the same model)
Step 2: Define the minimum detectable effect size δ that would be practically meaningful (e.g., 1% lift in CTR)
Step 3: Compute required sample size per group: n = (z_{0.025} + z_{0.8})² × 2σ² / δ² ≈ 7.85 × 2σ²/δ² for 80% power at α=0.05
Step 4: Apply CUPED (Controlled-experiment Using Pre-Experiment Data) to reduce variance: use pre-experiment metric values as covariates, typically reducing required sample by 30-50%
Step 5: Plan test duration based on n and daily traffic; never peek at results before reaching planned sample size without alpha-spending corrections
expected_outcome: A power analysis with required sample size, expected test duration, and variance reduction strategy, ensuring the experiment can detect meaningful effects.

title: Multiple Comparisons Correction for ML Evaluation
description: Testing k models on m metrics produces k×m comparisons, inflating false positive rates. With 10 tests at α=0.05, the family-wise error rate is 1-(1-0.05)^10 ≈ 0.40. Bonferroni correction (α/n_tests) is too conservative for correlated metrics. Holm-Bonferroni is strictly more powerful while still controlling FWER. For large-scale evaluations (dozens of metrics), FDR control via Benjamini-Hochberg is preferred — it controls the expected proportion of false discoveries rather than any false discovery.
when_to_use: When comparing multiple models across multiple metrics simultaneously, running many statistical tests on the same evaluation dataset, or publishing benchmark results where false discoveries would be misleading.
steps: Step 1: Count the total number of independent statistical tests being performed (models × metrics × datasets)
Step 2: For moderate numbers of tests (<20), apply Holm-Bonferroni: sort p-values ascending, compare p_(i) to α/(n-i+1), reject until the first failure
Step 3: For large numbers of tests (>20), use Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control: sort p-values, find largest i where p_(i) ≤ (i/n)×α, reject all p_(1)...p_(i)
Step 4: Report both raw and adjusted p-values to let readers apply their own threshold
Step 5: Consider using a permutation test-based approach (e.g., MaxT) when metrics are highly correlated, as it naturally accounts for the correlation structure
expected_outcome: Adjusted p-values or significance decisions that control the appropriate error rate (FWER or FDR) given the number of simultaneous comparisons.

title: Bootstrap Confidence Intervals for ML Metrics
description: The bootstrap constructs confidence intervals by resampling the test set with replacement B times (B ≥ 1000), computing the metric on each resample, and using the percentile method: CI = [θ*_{α/2}, θ*_{1-α/2}] where θ* are the sorted bootstrap estimates. For ML metrics, resample at the instance level (not the class level) to preserve the class distribution. Critical: bootstrap CIs are unreliable when the test set has fewer than ~200 examples, and they undercover (actual coverage < nominal) for metrics at boundaries (e.g., accuracy near 0 or 1).
when_to_use: When reporting ML evaluation results and need uncertainty estimates, comparing two models on the same test set, or when parametric assumptions (normality) don't hold for the metric distribution.
steps: Step 1: Resample the test set with replacement B=2000 times, maintaining the pairing of predictions and ground truth
Step 2: Compute the metric of interest on each bootstrap sample
Step 3: For a single metric CI, use the percentile method: sort bootstrap values and take the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles for a 95% CI
Step 4: For comparing two models, compute the paired difference on each bootstrap sample and check if the CI for the difference excludes 0
Step 5: Use the BCa (bias-corrected and accelerated) variant if the bootstrap distribution is skewed, which adjusts percentiles for bias and skewness
expected_outcome: Confidence intervals for metrics and metric differences that enable rigorous model comparison without parametric assumptions.

title: BLEU Score: Mechanics, Variants, and Failure Modes
description: BLEU computes modified n-gram precision across n=1..4, combined via geometric mean, with a brevity penalty for short outputs: BLEU = BP × exp(Σ w_n × log p_n). The brevity penalty BP = min(1, exp(1 - r/c)) where r is reference length and c is candidate length. BLEU is computed at the corpus level (accumulating n-gram counts across all examples), not averaged per sentence — sentence-level BLEU is extremely noisy. Known failure: BLEU penalizes valid paraphrases and rewards n-gram overlap even when meaning diverges. SacreBLEU standardizes tokenization to ensure reproducibility.
when_to_use: When evaluating machine translation or text generation tasks where lexical overlap with references is a reasonable proxy for quality, or when comparing against published results that report BLEU.
steps: Step 1: Tokenize candidates and references consistently — use SacreBLEU's internal tokenizer to avoid reproducibility issues from different tokenization schemes
Step 2: Compute modified n-gram precision for n=1,2,3,4: count matching n-grams clipped to reference frequency, divide by total candidate n-grams
Step 3: Compute the brevity penalty from the total corpus candidate and reference lengths
Step 4: Combine using geometric mean with uniform weights (w_n = 0.25) and the brevity penalty
Step 5: Report the full signature string (tokenization, casing, reference count) for reproducibility; always compare BLEU scores computed with identical settings
expected_outcome: A corpus-level BLEU score with full reproducibility metadata, interpreted with awareness of BLEU's known limitations for non-literal generation.

title: BERTScore for Semantic Similarity Evaluation
description: BERTScore computes soft semantic overlap between candidate and reference using contextual embeddings: for each token in the candidate, find the maximum cosine similarity with any reference token (and vice versa), then average to get precision, recall, and F1. Uses IDF weighting to downweight common tokens. BERTScore correlates better with human judgment than BLEU/ROUGE on summarization (+0.1-0.15 Kendall's tau). Key limitation: BERTScore is computationally expensive (requires GPU inference per pair) and scores are not interpretable on an absolute scale — always report with baseline rescaling.
when_to_use: When evaluating generated text where paraphrasing is expected and lexical overlap metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) unfairly penalize valid reformulations, especially for summarization, data-to-text, and dialogue evaluation.
steps: Step 1: Select the appropriate model layer for embeddings — for RoBERTa-large, layer 17 gives best correlation with human judgment on most tasks
Step 2: Compute pairwise cosine similarities between all candidate tokens and all reference tokens using contextual embeddings
Step 3: Apply greedy matching: for precision, each candidate token matches its most-similar reference token; for recall, each reference token matches its most-similar candidate token
Step 4: Apply IDF weighting from the reference corpus to upweight rare/informative tokens
Step 5: Apply baseline rescaling (subtract corpus-level mean and normalize) so scores are interpretable — raw BERTScore F1 typically ranges 0.85-0.95 even for mediocre outputs
expected_outcome: Precision, recall, and F1 BERTScores with baseline rescaling, providing semantic similarity evaluation that tolerates paraphrasing better than n-gram metrics.

title: ROUGE Variants for Summarization Evaluation
description: ROUGE measures recall-oriented overlap between system and reference summaries. ROUGE-N computes n-gram recall: ROUGE-1 (unigrams) captures content coverage, ROUGE-2 (bigrams) captures fluency. ROUGE-L uses longest common subsequence, rewarding in-order matching without requiring consecutive n-grams. ROUGE-Lsum applies ROUGE-L per-sentence then aggregates, which is better for multi-sentence summaries. Like BLEU, ROUGE penalizes valid paraphrases. Stemming is standard (Porter stemmer) and increases scores by ~2-5 points.
when_to_use: When evaluating text summarization systems against reference summaries, especially for extractive summarization where lexical overlap is high. ROUGE-2 F1 is the most commonly reported single metric.
steps: Step 1: Apply consistent preprocessing: lowercasing, stemming (Porter), sentence splitting for ROUGE-Lsum
Step 2: Compute ROUGE-1 (unigram recall/precision/F1) as the primary content coverage metric
Step 3: Compute ROUGE-2 (bigram recall/precision/F1) as the primary fluency and content ordering metric
Step 4: Compute ROUGE-L or ROUGE-Lsum (for multi-sentence outputs) to capture longest common subsequence matching
Step 5: Report F1 variants (not just recall) to penalize excessively long summaries; include confidence intervals via bootstrap over the test set
expected_outcome: ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L/Lsum F1 scores with confidence intervals, providing complementary views of content coverage and ordering quality.

title: MAUVE for Open-Ended Text Generation Quality
description: MAUVE measures the gap between the distribution of machine-generated text and human-written text using the area under a divergence frontier curve. It computes KL divergences in both directions (KL(P||Q) and KL(Q||P)) at multiple mixture ratios, producing a single score in [0,1] where 1 means the distributions are identical. Unlike BLEU/ROUGE, MAUVE doesn't need paired references — it compares distributions of text. MAUVE is sensitive to the embedding model used (GPT-2 embeddings are standard) and the quantization resolution (k-means clustering of embeddings into 500 clusters is default).
when_to_use: When evaluating open-ended generation (stories, dialogue, continuations) where there's no single correct reference, and you want to measure whether the model's output distribution matches the style and content distribution of human text.
steps: Step 1: Collect ≥1000 samples each from the model and from human-written text in the same domain/context
Step 2: Embed both sets using GPT-2 features and cluster into 500 bins via k-means to create discrete distributions P (human) and Q (model)
Step 3: Compute KL(P||Rλ) and KL(Q||Rλ) for mixtures Rλ = λP + (1-λ)Q at multiple λ values from 0 to 1
Step 4: Plot the divergence frontier and compute the area under it — this is the MAUVE score
Step 5: Compare MAUVE across decoding strategies (nucleus sampling p=0.9 vs 0.95 vs beam search) to find the generation setting that best matches human text distribution
expected_outcome: A distribution-level score measuring how well model-generated text matches the statistical properties of human text, without requiring per-example reference pairing.

title: Reward Model Evaluation and Reward Hacking Detection
description: Reward models (RMs) for RLHF must be evaluated for both accuracy (agreement with human preferences) and vulnerability to hacking (outputs that score high on RM but low on true quality). RM accuracy is measured by pairwise accuracy on held-out human preference data. Reward hacking manifests as KL divergence between the RL policy and the SFT policy increasing while true quality plateaus or decreases — the model learns to exploit RM patterns rather than genuinely improve. Common hacking patterns: excessive hedging, sycophantic agreement, verbose padding, and style mimicry.
when_to_use: When training or evaluating RLHF systems, diagnosing degradation in model quality despite increasing reward scores, or selecting reward models for alignment training.
steps: Step 1: Evaluate RM pairwise accuracy on a held-out preference test set stratified by preference margin (easy vs hard pairs)
Step 2: Plot reward score vs KL divergence from the SFT policy during RL training — look for a point where reward increases but quality (measured by independent evaluation) plateaus

Step 3: Check for known hacking signatures: correlate reward with output length (length hacking), count hedging phrases (safety hacking), measure sentiment toward the user's premise (sycophancy hacking)
Step 4: Evaluate the RM on adversarially constructed examples designed to exploit surface patterns (e.g., high-reward low-quality outputs from a previous RL run)
Step 5: Compute the 'reward overoptimization' gap: the difference between RM-predicted quality and human-judged quality as a function of optimization pressure (KL budget)
expected_outcome: A comprehensive RM evaluation including accuracy on clean data, vulnerability to known exploitation patterns, and the reward overoptimization curve showing where to stop RL training.

title: Demographic Parity and Equalized Odds Fairness Metrics
description: Demographic parity requires P(Ŷ=1|A=a) = P(Ŷ=1|A=b) for all groups A — equal positive prediction rates regardless of group membership. Equalized odds requires equal TPR and FPR across groups: P(Ŷ=1|Y=1,A=a) = P(Ŷ=1|Y=1,A=b) AND P(Ŷ=1|Y=0,A=a) = P(Ŷ=1|Y=0,A=b). These are provably incompatible with calibration when base rates differ across groups (Chouldechova 2017, Kleinberg 2016). The 80% rule (four-fifths rule): if the selection rate for any group is less than 80% of the highest group's rate, there is prima facie evidence of adverse impact.
when_to_use: When evaluating ML systems that make decisions affecting people (hiring, lending, content moderation) and need to measure whether the system treats demographic groups equitably.
steps: Step 1: Define the protected attribute groups and ensure sufficient sample sizes per group (minimum ~100 per group for stable estimates)
Step 2: Compute demographic parity difference: |P(Ŷ=1|A=a) - P(Ŷ=1|A=b)| and the four-fifths ratio: min_group_rate / max_group_rate
Step 3: Compute equalized odds: TPR and FPR per group. The equalized odds difference is max(|TPR_a - TPR_b|, |FPR_a - FPR_b|)
Step 4: Compute calibration per group: P(Y=1|Ŷ=p, A=a) ≈ p for all groups. Plot group-specific reliability diagrams
Step 5: Document which fairness criteria are prioritized and why, given the impossibility of satisfying all simultaneously when base rates differ
expected_outcome: Quantified fairness metrics across groups with explicit documentation of which fairness criterion was chosen and acknowledgment of the impossibility theorem tradeoffs.

title: Adversarial Robustness Evaluation for NLP Models
description: Adversarial robustness testing applies semantic-preserving perturbations to inputs and measures accuracy degradation. For NLP: character-level attacks (typos, homoglyphs), word-level attacks (synonym substitution via WordNet or embedding neighbors), and sentence-level attacks (paraphrasing via back-translation). TextFooler achieves >90% attack success rate on BERT classifiers by replacing <15% of words. Key metrics: attack success rate, average perturbation rate, and the accuracy-robustness tradeoff curve (accuracy on clean vs perturbed inputs at different perturbation budgets).
when_to_use: When deploying NLP models in adversarial environments (spam detection, content moderation, fact-checking) or when evaluating how brittle a model is to natural input variation like typos and paraphrases.
steps: Step 1: Select attack types appropriate to the threat model: character-level for typo robustness, word-level (TextFooler, BAE) for semantic robustness, sentence-level for paraphrase robustness
Step 2: Run attacks on the correctly-classified test examples; compute attack success rate = (successfully fooled) / (correctly classified originally)
Step 3: Measure perturbation magnitude: % of words changed, semantic similarity between original and adversarial (USE cosine similarity should remain >0.8)
Step 4: Filter out adversarial examples where the perturbation changes the ground truth label (semantic validity check, ideally human-verified on a sample)
Step 5: Plot the accuracy-robustness curve: evaluate accuracy at different perturbation budgets (5%, 10%, 20% of words changed) to characterize graceful vs cliff-edge degradation
expected_outcome: Robustness profile showing how model accuracy degrades under controlled adversarial perturbations, with attack success rates and semantic validity verification.

title: Recall@K and Precision@K for Retrieval Evaluation
description: Recall@K = |relevant ∩ retrieved@K| / |relevant| measures what fraction of all relevant documents appear in the top K results. Precision@K = |relevant ∩ retrieved@K| / K measures what fraction of the top K results are relevant. For retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), recall@K is usually more important than precision@K because the downstream LLM can ignore irrelevant passages but cannot recover unretrieved information. R-Precision uses K = |relevant| (the number of relevant docs for that query) as a parameter-free variant.
when_to_use: When evaluating retrieval systems (search engines, RAG pipelines, recommendation systems) and need to understand the precision-recall tradeoff at specific cutoffs, especially when the downstream consumer can filter irrelevant results.
steps: Step 1: For each query, determine the set of relevant documents from ground truth judgments
Step 2: Compute Precision@K and Recall@K at multiple cutoffs (K = 1, 5, 10, 20, 100) to map the tradeoff curve
Step 3: Compute R-Precision for each query as a parameter-free alternative
Step 4: For RAG systems, emphasize Recall@K where K matches the number of passages fed to the LLM (e.g., recall@5 if the LLM sees 5 passages)

Step 5: Stratify by query type (easy/hard based on number of relevant docs) — systems often have high recall on easy queries with many relevant docs but poor recall on hard queries with 1-2 relevant docs
expected_outcome: Precision-recall tradeoff curves at multiple K values, with emphasis on the K that matches the downstream application's context window.

title: Online vs Offline Evaluation Alignment Analysis
description: Offline metrics (computed on static test sets) often disagree with online metrics (A/B test outcomes in production). Common causes: offline metrics don't capture user behavior dynamics (e.g., a model that's better on accuracy but slower loses users), offline datasets are stale or non-representative, and offline metrics don't account for feedback loops (better recommendations change user behavior). Studies at Netflix and Spotify found offline NDCG correlated only r=0.3-0.5 with online engagement. The gold standard is to establish the offline-online correlation empirically before trusting offline evaluation alone.
when_to_use: When deciding whether offline evaluation results are sufficient to ship a model change, designing evaluation pipelines that predict online outcomes, or diagnosing why an offline-winning model underperformed in production.
steps: Step 1: Collect paired data: models that were evaluated both offline (test set metrics) and online (A/B test outcomes) over at least 10 model iterations
Step 2: Compute rank correlation (Kendall's tau) between offline metric rankings and online metric rankings of those models

Step 3: If correlation < 0.5, investigate systematic biases: check if offline test set demographics match production traffic, whether temporal patterns are captured, and whether latency differences affect online metrics
Step 4: Build a counterfactual evaluation pipeline using logged production data and inverse propensity scoring to better estimate online outcomes from offline data
Step 5: Establish a threshold: offline improvements below a certain size (e.g., <2% NDCG gain) are unreliable predictors of online gains and require A/B testing
expected_outcome: A quantified relationship between offline and online metrics with a documented reliability threshold below which offline evaluation is insufficient.

title: Data Leakage Detection in Train-Test Splits
description: Data leakage occurs when information from the test set inadvertently influences training, producing overly optimistic evaluation. Common forms: temporal leakage (using future data to predict the past), group leakage (same entity appearing in both train and test), feature leakage (a feature that proxies the label and wouldn't be available at prediction time), and preprocessing leakage (fitting scalers/encoders on the full dataset before splitting). Leakage can inflate metrics by 10-50% — one Kaggle analysis found leakage in 40% of competition datasets.
when_to_use: When evaluation metrics seem suspiciously high, when creating new train/test splits, or when auditing an existing ML pipeline for validity of reported performance numbers.
steps: Step 1: Check for entity-level leakage: if data has groups (users, documents, sessions), ensure no group spans both train and test
Step 2: Check for temporal leakage: if data has timestamps, verify that all test examples are strictly later than all training examples
Step 3: Check for feature leakage: compute mutual information between each feature and the label, flag features with I(X;Y) > 0.5 bits for manual inspection
Step 4: Check for preprocessing leakage: verify that all transformations (normalization, encoding, feature selection) were fit only on training data
Step 5: Run a 'leakage canary': train a model to predict whether an example is from train or test — if accuracy > 55%, there's a distribution difference suggesting leakage or improper splitting
expected_outcome: A systematic audit identifying any leakage sources in the evaluation pipeline, with corrected splits and honest performance estimates.

title: Multi-Dimensional Evaluation: HHH Framework for LLMs
description: Anthropic's HHH framework evaluates LLMs on three axes: Helpful (provides useful, accurate information), Harmless (avoids generating harmful content or assisting harmful actions), and Honest (doesn't fabricate information, expresses uncertainty appropriately). These dimensions often conflict: a maximally helpful model might answer dangerous questions, and a maximally harmless model might refuse benign requests. Evaluation requires separate scoring on each axis with explicit tracking of tension points where improving one dimension degrades another.
when_to_use: When evaluating general-purpose LLM assistants where single-metric evaluation is insufficient, designing reward models that balance competing objectives, or auditing model behavior across safety-capability tradeoffs.
steps: Step 1: Construct or select evaluation sets targeting each dimension independently: factual QA for helpfulness, safety red-teaming for harmlessness, calibration probes for honesty
Step 2: Evaluate helpfulness using task-specific accuracy metrics (QA accuracy, instruction following rate, user satisfaction scores)
Step 3: Evaluate harmlessness using refusal rate on harmful requests AND over-refusal rate on benign requests — both matter
Step 4: Evaluate honesty by measuring calibration (does the model say 'I'm not sure' when it should?) and factual accuracy on knowledge probes

Step 5: Construct tension analysis: identify examples where the model sacrificed one dimension for another (e.g., refused a helpful request due to false positive safety filter) and compute the Pareto frontier
expected_outcome: A three-dimensional evaluation profile with explicit tension analysis showing where the model trades off between helpfulness, harmlessness, and honesty.

title: Thompson Sampling for Evaluation and Bandit Testing
description: Thompson Sampling maintains a posterior distribution over each arm's reward parameter and selects arms by sampling from the posterior. For Bernoulli rewards: maintain Beta(α, β) per arm, initialized as Beta(1,1); on success update α += 1, on failure β += 1; sample θ_i ~ Beta(α_i, β_i) and select arm with highest θ_i. Thompson Sampling naturally balances exploration and exploitation and converges to the best arm with Bayesian regret O(√(KT log T)) for K arms over T rounds. Advantage over epsilon-greedy: it explores proportionally to uncertainty, not uniformly.
when_to_use: When comparing models or variants in an online setting where you want to minimize regret during the evaluation period, or when running an A/B test where routing most traffic to the better variant during the test is important.
steps: Step 1: Initialize Beta(1,1) priors for each model variant (arm), representing complete uncertainty about each variant's success rate
Step 2: For each incoming request, sample a success probability θ_i from each arm's current Beta posterior
Step 3: Route the request to the arm with the highest sampled θ_i; observe the outcome (success/failure)
Step 4: Update the selected arm's posterior: Beta(α + success, β + failure)
Step 5: Monitor convergence: when one arm's 95% credible interval no longer overlaps with others', the evaluation can be concluded. Report the posterior distributions, not just point estimates
expected_outcome: A bandit-based evaluation that converges to the best variant while minimizing regret, with full posterior distributions over each variant's performance.

title: Cost-Quality Pareto Frontier Analysis
description: When evaluating models with different computational costs (API pricing, latency, FLOPs), a Pareto frontier identifies which models are efficient — no other model achieves better quality at equal or lower cost. A model is Pareto-dominated if another model is both cheaper and better. The Pareto frontier slopes between adjacent points give the marginal cost per quality unit. Common finding: the frontier is concave (diminishing returns), with the jump from small to medium models yielding 10× more quality-per-dollar than medium to large.
when_to_use: When selecting among models with different cost-quality tradeoffs (e.g., GPT-4 vs GPT-3.5 vs local models), optimizing deployment budgets, or justifying infrastructure costs for ML system upgrades.
steps: Step 1: Define quality metric(s) and cost metric(s) — cost can be API price per 1K tokens, latency p95, GPU-hours, or a composite
Step 2: Evaluate all candidate models on both dimensions using the same evaluation set
Step 3: Identify the Pareto frontier: for each model, check if any other model is strictly better on both dimensions. Non-dominated models form the frontier
Step 4: Compute the marginal cost-quality slope between adjacent frontier points: Δquality/Δcost to find which upgrades offer the best value

Step 5: Plot the frontier with dominated models shown in grey; annotate the 'knee point' where the marginal return drops sharply — this is often the optimal operating point for budget-constrained deployments
expected_outcome: A Pareto frontier plot with marginal cost-quality slopes identifying the most efficient models and the optimal operating point for a given budget constraint.

title: Agent Trajectory Evaluation with Step-Level Scoring
description: Multi-step agent trajectories require evaluation at both the outcome level (did the agent achieve the goal?) and the process level (was each step reasonable?). Outcome-only evaluation misses important information: an agent that achieves the right answer by luck (random exploration that happens to work) is worse than one that follows a sound strategy but hits an edge case. Step-level evaluation scores each action for: relevance (does this action move toward the goal?), efficiency (was there a shorter path?), and safety (did any step risk irreversible harm?).
when_to_use: When evaluating AI agents that take multi-step actions (tool-using LLMs, code agents, web agents), especially when understanding failure modes and improving agent strategies requires knowing where in the trajectory things go wrong.
steps: Step 1: Score outcome: binary success/failure, plus partial credit based on how close the final state is to the goal state
Step 2: Score efficiency: compare trajectory length to the shortest known solution path. Compute the ratio (optimal steps / actual steps) as an efficiency score
Step 3: Score each step individually: was it a valid action in context? Did it make progress? Did it introduce any risk?
Step 4: Identify the first 'critical error' — the earliest step where the agent deviated from a successful strategy — this is more actionable than average step quality for debugging
Step 5: Aggregate into a trajectory quality score: weight outcome (0.4), efficiency (0.3), step quality (0.2), and safety (0.1), adjusting weights based on the application's risk tolerance
expected_outcome: A multi-level trajectory evaluation with outcome, efficiency, and step-level scores, plus identification of critical failure points for targeted improvement.

title: Platt Scaling for Binary Classifier Calibration
description: Platt scaling fits a logistic regression on the classifier's raw outputs (logits or scores) to produce calibrated probabilities: P(y=1|f) = 1/(1 + exp(Af + B)) where A and B are learned on a calibration set. Unlike temperature scaling (one parameter), Platt scaling has two parameters allowing both shift and scale correction. It's the right choice when the classifier's output distribution is not symmetric around the decision boundary. Key pitfall: Platt scaling can overfit with <500 calibration examples — use cross-validation to estimate A, B in that regime.
when_to_use: When a binary classifier's outputs need to be converted to well-calibrated probabilities, especially for SVMs or boosted models that don't naturally output probabilities, or when temperature scaling alone is insufficient.
steps: Step 1: Hold out a calibration set (500-5000 examples) or use k-fold cross-validation on the training set if data is limited
Step 2: Extract raw model outputs (logits, SVM decision values, or uncalibrated probabilities) for the calibration set
Step 3: Fit logistic regression: P(y=1|f) = σ(Af + B) using maximum likelihood, where f is the raw output
Step 4: Apply the learned transformation to test set outputs; verify calibration improvement via ECE and reliability diagram
Step 5: If calibration is still poor, consider isotonic regression (non-parametric, more flexible) but requires larger calibration sets (>2000 examples) to avoid overfitting
expected_outcome: Calibrated probability outputs from a binary classifier with documented improvement in ECE, suitable for downstream decision-making under uncertainty.

title: Distribution Shift Detection via Two-Sample Testing
description: Distribution shift between training and deployment data degrades model performance. Detection uses two-sample tests to determine whether the distributions differ: Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) maps data into a reproducing kernel Hilbert space and compares distribution means; the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test compares CDFs per feature; and classifier two-sample testing trains a model to distinguish train from deploy data — if accuracy exceeds 55%, shift is likely present. Shift types: covariate shift (P(X) changes), label shift (P(Y) changes), and concept drift (P(Y|X) changes).
when_to_use: When deploying models in production where the input distribution may change over time, monitoring model reliability, or diagnosing unexplained performance degradation in deployed systems.
steps: Step 1: Collect representative samples from both distributions (training/reference and production/current), at least 500 examples each
Step 2: Apply per-feature KS tests to identify which individual features have shifted significantly (Bonferroni-correct for multiple features)
Step 3: Compute MMD with RBF kernel in the model's representation space (penultimate layer features) to detect shift in the space the model actually uses
Step 4: Train a domain classifier to distinguish the two distributions; feature importance of this classifier reveals which aspects shifted most

Step 5: Classify the shift type: if P(X) changed but P(Y|X) didn't (performance stable on shifted examples), it's covariate shift; if P(Y|X) changed (performance drops even on similar inputs), it's concept drift
expected_outcome: A shift detection report identifying whether distribution shift exists, which features are affected, the type of shift, and its likely impact on model performance.

title: Paired Permutation Testing for Model Comparison
description: A paired permutation test (also called a randomization test) determines whether the difference between two models is statistically significant without assuming normality. For each of B iterations (B ≥ 10000), randomly swap model A and B's predictions for each test example independently, recompute the metric difference, and build the null distribution. The p-value is the fraction of permuted differences that exceed the observed difference. This test is exact (no distributional assumptions), handles any metric, and accounts for the paired structure (same test examples).
when_to_use: When comparing two models on the same test set and need a non-parametric significance test, especially for metrics where bootstrap or t-test assumptions are questionable (e.g., BLEU, NDCG, F1).
steps: Step 1: Compute the actual metric for both models and their difference δ_observed on the test set
Step 2: For B=10000 iterations: for each test example, flip a fair coin to decide whether to swap the two models' predictions, then recompute the metric difference δ*_b
Step 3: Compute p-value = (1 + Σ I(|δ*_b| ≥ |δ_observed|)) / (1 + B) — the +1 prevents p=0 and accounts for the observed statistic itself
Step 4: If p < α (typically 0.05), the difference is significant. Report the p-value and the effect size (δ_observed) together — significance without meaningful effect size is not actionable
Step 5: For comparing more than two models, apply the permutation test pairwise with Holm-Bonferroni correction
expected_outcome: A non-parametric p-value for the difference between two models with no distributional assumptions, paired with effect size for practical significance assessment.

title: Evaluation Set Design: Size, Stratification, and Power
description: Evaluation set design critically affects the reliability of conclusions. For binary classification, detecting a 2% accuracy difference with 80% power requires ~4000 examples per class. For retrieval evaluation, ~50 queries is the minimum for stable NDCG estimates, but 200+ is preferred. Stratification ensures representation across important subgroups — without it, rare categories may have zero examples. Key principle: the evaluation set should be designed before model development, not created post hoc from convenient data.
when_to_use: When creating new benchmarks, designing evaluation datasets for a specific project, or assessing whether an existing evaluation set is large and diverse enough to support the conclusions drawn from it.
steps: Step 1: Define the target metric and the minimum detectable difference — use power analysis to determine required sample size per stratum
Step 2: Identify stratification dimensions (difficulty level, input type, domain, demographic group) and set minimum sample sizes per stratum (≥30 for stable statistics, ≥100 for CI estimation)
Step 3: Source evaluation examples independently of training data — use temporal splits, separate data collections, or domain experts
Step 4: Include adversarial and edge-case examples (5-10% of the set) to stress-test models beyond average-case performance
Step 5: Document the evaluation set's construction methodology, known biases, and limitations — this metadata is essential for interpreting results correctly
expected_outcome: A well-sized, stratified evaluation set with documented construction methodology, sufficient statistical power for intended comparisons, and built-in stress tests.

title: Calibration Across Demographic Groups
description: A model can be well-calibrated overall (low ECE) while being poorly calibrated for specific demographic groups — a phenomenon called calibration disparity. For example, a risk assessment model might be well-calibrated for the majority group (P(Y=1|score=0.7, group=A) ≈ 0.7) but overpredict for a minority group (P(Y=1|score=0.7, group=B) = 0.5). This is particularly dangerous in high-stakes applications where decisions rely on probability estimates. Group-conditional ECE decomposes calibration by group and can reveal hidden disparities masked by aggregate metrics.
when_to_use: When deploying probability-based decision systems (risk scoring, lending, medical diagnosis) to diverse populations, or when fairness auditing requires calibration analysis beyond aggregate metrics.
steps: Step 1: Compute ECE separately for each demographic group, using at least 200 examples per group for stable estimates
Step 2: Plot group-specific reliability diagrams on the same axes to visualize calibration disparities — differences in slope indicate systematic over/underprediction for specific groups
Step 3: Compute the max calibration disparity: the largest absolute difference in (predicted probability - actual frequency) across groups at any confidence level
Step 4: Test whether group-conditional calibration differences are statistically significant using a chi-squared test on binned predictions per group
Step 5: If disparities exist, evaluate post-hoc group-specific calibration (separate temperature per group) versus the tradeoff of requiring group membership at prediction time
expected_outcome: Group-level calibration analysis revealing whether probability estimates are equally trustworthy across demographic groups, with remediation options if disparities exist.

title: Contamination-Resistant Evaluation via Dynamic Benchmarks
description: Static benchmarks inevitably leak into training data as models train on web-scraped text. Contamination-resistant approaches include: dynamic benchmarks (periodically refreshed with new examples), private held-out sets (never published, only accessible via API), and procedurally generated tests (programmatic construction from templates ensures examples didn't exist before). Dynabench showed that model performance on dynamically adversarial benchmarks is 15-30% lower than on static benchmarks, indicating that static scores overestimate capability. The tradeoff: dynamic benchmarks are expensive and less comparable over time.
when_to_use: When designing benchmarks for models trained on internet-scale data where contamination is likely, or when evaluating a new model and suspecting that its high benchmark scores partly reflect memorization.
steps: Step 1: Assess contamination risk: if the benchmark is >2 years old and widely used, assume substantial contamination for any web-trained model
Step 2: For existing benchmarks, apply contamination detection (n-gram overlap) and report clean-subset scores alongside full scores
Step 3: Create a private held-out set: generate new evaluation examples in the same format but never publish them; evaluate models only through a controlled API

Step 4: Implement procedural generation where feasible: parameterized math problems, template-based reasoning questions, or adversarially filtered examples where a reference model was used to select challenging instances
Step 5: Version and timestamp all benchmark data; track model performance on each version to detect suspiciously large jumps that may indicate contamination of earlier versions
expected_outcome: An evaluation strategy resistant to training data contamination, combining contamination detection, private held-outs, and dynamic generation for robust capability assessment.

title: Effect Size Reporting for ML Experiments
description: Statistical significance (p < 0.05) says only that an effect exists, not how large it is. Effect size measures include Cohen's d = (μ1 - μ2) / s_pooled for continuous metrics (small: 0.2, medium: 0.5, large: 0.8), and the common language effect size (probability that a random sample from group A exceeds a random sample from group B). For ML metrics: report both absolute difference (e.g., +1.3% accuracy) and relative improvement (e.g., +4.2% relative). Large datasets can make tiny, meaningless differences statistically significant — a 0.1% accuracy improvement with p=0.001 on 100K examples may not justify deployment costs.
when_to_use: When reporting ML experimental results to ensure that statistically significant differences are also practically meaningful, or when making deployment decisions where the cost of switching models must be justified by the magnitude of improvement.
steps: Step 1: Report the absolute difference in the primary metric with 95% confidence interval — this is the most interpretable measure for stakeholders
Step 2: Compute Cohen's d or similar standardized effect size to enable comparison across different metrics and datasets
Step 3: Define the minimum practically meaningful difference (e.g., +0.5% accuracy based on business impact analysis) before running the experiment
Step 4: Compute the common language effect size: the probability that a random test example is handled better by model A than model B — this is intuitive for non-technical stakeholders
Step 5: Present the p-value, effect size, and confidence interval together in results tables — never report p-value alone as evidence of a meaningful improvement
expected_outcome: Comprehensive effect size reporting that distinguishes between statistical significance and practical significance, enabling informed deployment decisions.
